title: Multivariable Calculus in the Age of AI
text:
Welcome
Multivariable Calculus in the Age of Data, AI, and Applied Mathematics is a modern textbook for students who want to use calculus as a language for geometry, computation, statistics, machine learning, and applied mathematical modeling.
The guiding idea is simple:
A point in \(\mathbb R^n\) can represent a location in space, a data record, a parameter vector, a probability model, a neural-network weight vector, or the state of a dynamical system. A derivative becomes a gradient, a Jacobian, or a Hessian. An integral becomes volume, mass, probability, expectation, flux, or accumulated work. Vector calculus becomes the language of flow, conservation, circulation, and change over regions and surfaces.
This book develops the classical core of multivariable calculus while making the computational and data-centered viewpoint explicit from the beginning.
crumbs: Book
Welcome

title: Multivariable Calculus in the Age of AI
text:
Who this book is for
This book is designed for students preparing for graduate study or advanced undergraduate work in:

applied mathematics,
statistics,
data science,
artificial intelligence,
machine learning,
quantitative modeling,
scientific computing,
engineering and physical sciences,
mathematical foundations of modern computation.

The expected background is single-variable calculus and basic algebra. Linear algebra is introduced and reinforced where needed, especially through vectors, projections, hyperplanes, Jacobians, Hessians, quadratic forms, and optimization.

title: Multivariable Calculus in the Age of AI
text:
Learning philosophy
The goal is not only to compute derivatives and integrals. The goal is to see the structure behind the computations:

local linearity behind derivatives,
accumulation behind integrals,
geometry behind optimization,
curvature behind uncertainty,
coordinates behind transformations,
fields behind flows and conservation laws.

A student who finishes the book should be ready to enter advanced courses in applied mathematics, probability, statistics, machine learning, optimization, scientific computing, and mathematical modeling with a clear geometric and computational foundation.
crumbs: Book
Welcome

title: Introduction
text:
Why multivariable calculus matters now
Multivariable calculus is the language of change, accumulation, and geometry in many dimensions. Single-variable calculus studies functions such as \(f(x)\), where one input produces one output. Multivariable calculus studies functions such as
\[
f(x,y), \qquad f(x,y,z), \qquad f(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n).
\]
This apparently simple change from one input to many inputs changes the subject completely. A derivative is no longer only a slope. It becomes a collection of rates of change in many directions. An integral is no longer only signed area under a curve. It becomes volume, mass, probability, expected value, flux, and accumulated density over regions in the plane, space, and higher-dimensional models. A curve is no longer only the graph of a function. It may be a path through space, the motion of a particle, the trajectory of a dynamical system, or the path followed by an optimization algorithm.
This book develops multivariable calculus as a modern bridge to applied mathematics, statistics, data science, artificial intelligence, and machine learning. The classical topics remain central: vectors, planes, partial derivatives, gradients, multiple integrals, line integrals, surface integrals, Green’s theorem, Stokes’ theorem, and the divergence theorem. At the same time, the book treats these ideas as tools for understanding high-dimensional data, optimization, probability models, numerical computation, and mathematical modeling.
Modern quantitative work is naturally multivariable. A data point may contain many features. A statistical model may depend on several parameters. A neural network may depend on millions of weights. A physical system may assign temperature, pressure, velocity, or force to every point in a region. A probability distribution may describe several random variables simultaneously. In all of these examples, the central objects are functions of many variables.
Multivariable calculus provides the basic grammar for these objects:
The goal of this book is not only to teach formulas. The goal is to build a flexible way of thinking: to see formulas geometrically, to test them computationally, to use them in modeling, and to recognize them inside statistics and machine learning.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
text:
Geometry describes points, vectors, distances, directions, planes, surfaces, and regions.
Differentiation describes local change, sensitivity, approximation, gradients, Hessians, and optimization.
Integration describes accumulation over regions, volumes, probabilities, averages, mass, and density.
Vector calculus describes work, circulation, flux, conservation laws, and fields.
Computation turns the theory into experiments, visualizations, simulations, and algorithms.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
section: The organizing idea of the book
text:
The organizing idea of the book
The book is organized around one central principle:

Multivariable calculus studies how quantities change and accumulate over geometric objects.

The geometric object may be a line, plane, surface, solid, region, curve, parameter space, or high-dimensional feature space. The quantity may be height, temperature, density, force, probability, error, likelihood, risk, or energy. The calculus tells us how to measure local change and global accumulation.
This perspective explains why the same ideas appear across many fields. The gradient points in the direction of fastest increase of a function, but it also gives the direction of steepest ascent of a likelihood, the update direction in optimization, and the normal direction to a level surface. A double integral computes volume under a surface, but it also computes probability under a joint density. A Jacobian measures local area or volume distortion, but it also explains change of variables in probability. A divergence measures net outward flow, but it also expresses conservation laws.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
text:
What makes this book different
This is a calculus book for the age of computation and AI. It keeps the mathematical structure of a rigorous multivariable calculus course, but it adds three modern layers.
First, the book emphasizes visual understanding. Many concepts in multivariable calculus are difficult because they live in two or three dimensions. The chapters use surfaces, contour plots, vector fields, parametrized curves, regions, and coordinate systems to make the geometry visible.
Second, the book emphasizes Python computation. Each major topic is paired with computational labs. The goal is not to replace hand calculation, but to deepen it. Python helps students test examples, visualize surfaces, approximate integrals, simulate probability distributions, and implement optimization methods.
Third, the book emphasizes connections to statistics, data science, and machine learning. Gradients appear in gradient descent. Hessians describe curvature of loss functions. Multiple integrals define expectations. Jacobians appear in transformations of random variables and modern generative models. Vector calculus appears in physical modeling, conservation laws, and differential equations.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
text:
How the book is organized
The book has five parts.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
The first part develops the geometry needed for multivariable calculus. We study Euclidean space, vectors, dot products, projections, lines, planes, hyperplanes, cross products, and curves in space. These chapters build the geometric vocabulary for everything that follows.


Part II. Functions of Several Variables and Differentiation
The second part studies functions of several variables. We introduce domains, graphs, level curves, limits, continuity, partial derivatives, gradients, tangent planes, linear approximation, Jacobians, chain rules, automatic differentiation, directional derivatives, Hessians, optimization, and Lagrange multipliers.
This part is especially important for students preparing for applied mathematics, statistics, data science, and machine learning. It explains why gradients and Hessians are central to optimization and why local linear and quadratic approximations are so useful.


Part III. Multiple Integrals, Probability, and Change of Variables
The third part studies double and triple integrals. We begin with Riemann sums and iterated integrals, then move to general regions, polar coordinates, cylindrical coordinates, spherical coordinates, and change of variables. These ideas are connected to volume, mass, density, center of mass, average value, and probability.
This part builds a bridge from calculus to probability and statistics. A joint density is a surface or higher-dimensional function whose integral gives probability. A change of variables requires a Jacobian. Expected value is an integral. Many statistical and probabilistic ideas are best understood through multiple integrals.


Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
The fourth part studies vector fields and the major integral theorems of vector calculus. We study line integrals, work, conservative fields, Green’s theorem, curl, divergence, surface integrals, flux, Stokes’ theorem, and the divergence theorem.
These chapters connect local information to global information. Green’s theorem, Stokes’ theorem, and the divergence theorem show that integration over a region can often be transformed into integration over its boundary, and conversely. These are among the most powerful ideas in applied mathematics.


Part V. Modern Applications for Graduate Preparation
The final part synthesizes the course and connects multivariable calculus to probability, statistics, data science, machine learning, and applied mathematics. The goal is to help students see the course not as an isolated requirement, but as a foundation for later work in graduate-level modeling, inference, computation, and analysis.

title: Introduction
text:
How to use the book
A good way to study each chapter is to follow this cycle:

Read the motivation and the main definitions.
Work through the examples by hand.
Re-draw the figures or reproduce the geometry in your own notes.
Run the corresponding Python lab.
Modify the code and test your own examples.
Solve the practice problems without looking back at the examples.
Return to the chapter summary and formula map.

The computational work is not optional decoration. It is part of the mathematical learning process. A student who can compute a gradient by hand, visualize it on a contour plot, approximate it numerically, and use it in an optimization algorithm has a much deeper understanding than a student who only memorizes the formula.

title: Introduction
text:
Python labs and interactive pages
The book includes Python labs and interactive HTML pages.
The Python labs are collected on the page Python Labs. These notebooks are designed to run in Google Colab or a local Jupyter environment. They use standard scientific Python tools such as NumPy and Matplotlib, with occasional optional use of SymPy, SciPy, or Plotly.
The interactive HTML pages are collected on the page Interactive HTML Pages. These pages are intended for quick visualization, exploration, and review. They are especially useful for topics such as surfaces, contour plots, vector fields, coordinate systems, and numerical experiments.

Introduction

title: Introduction
text:
Prerequisites
The main prerequisite is a solid understanding of single-variable calculus:

functions and graphs,
limits and continuity,
derivatives and applications,
definite integrals,
substitution and integration techniques,
parametric curves and polar coordinates at an introductory level.

Some linear algebra is helpful, especially vectors, dot products, matrices, and linear systems. However, the necessary vector and matrix ideas are reviewed as they appear. Students preparing for graduate study should treat this book as a bridge to more advanced courses in linear algebra, probability, statistics, optimization, numerical analysis, differential equations, and machine learning.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
text:
Notation and style
The notation follows standard calculus conventions. Points and vectors in \(\mathbb R^n\) are written as ordered tuples such as \((x_1,\dots,x_n)\) or as vectors when the geometric interpretation is important. The gradient is denoted by \(\nabla f\). The dot product is denoted by \(u\cdot v\). Double and triple integrals are written as
\[
\iint_R f(x,y)\,dA, \qquad \iiint_E f(x,y,z)\,dV.
\]
For Quarto and Jupyter compatibility, this book uses dollar-sign math notation, such as $f(x,y)$ for inline mathematics and $$...$$ for displayed equations.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
section: A note on AI and computation
text:
A note on AI and computation
AI tools can help students explore examples, check algebra, generate code, and ask follow-up questions. However, AI tools should not replace mathematical thinking. In this book, computation is used to support understanding, not to bypass it.
When using computational or AI tools, ask three questions:

Does the answer make geometric sense?
Can I verify it by hand in a simpler case?
Can I explain why the method works?

A correct numerical answer without understanding is fragile. A concept understood geometrically, algebraically, and computationally is durable.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: Introduction
text:
The larger goal
Multivariable calculus is one of the central gateways into modern mathematical sciences. It prepares students for optimization, probability, statistics, machine learning, differential equations, numerical methods, mathematical physics, and applied modeling. It teaches students to move between local and global thinking, between formulas and geometry, and between theory and computation.
The aim of this book is to help students build that bridge carefully and confidently.
crumbs: Book
Introduction

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.1 Chapter overview
Single-variable calculus studies functions such as \(f(x)\), where one input number produces one output number. Multivariable calculus studies functions whose inputs may be points, vectors, measurements, parameters, or data records:
\[
f:
\mathbb R^n \longrightarrow \mathbb R,
\qquad
x=(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n) \longmapsto f(x).
\]
This change looks small, but it changes the whole subject. In one-variable calculus, we move left or right along a line. In multivariable calculus, we can move in infinitely many directions. In one-variable calculus, the graph of \(y=f(x)\) is a curve. In two-variable calculus, the graph of \(z=f(x,y)\) is a surface. In higher dimensions, the graph may be impossible to draw directly, but the same ideas remain: local approximation, rate of change, accumulation, optimization, and geometric structure.
This chapter is a bridge. It reviews the core ideas of single-variable calculus and explains how they become the language of multivariable calculus, applied mathematics, statistics, data science, and machine learning.

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.2 Learning goals
After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

Explain the difference between \(f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\), \(f:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\), \(f:\mathbb R^3\to\mathbb R\), and \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\).
Interpret a multivariable input as a point, vector, data record, parameter vector, or state.
Describe a function using formulas, tables, graphs, level sets, and computation.
Explain why derivatives become directional and why gradients are central.
Explain why integrals become accumulation over regions, volumes, and probability spaces.
Recognize the role of multivariable calculus in optimization, statistics, machine learning, and applied mathematics.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.3 1.1 Why multivariable calculus?
In single-variable calculus, a typical function has the form
\[
f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R,
\qquad
x\mapsto f(x).
\]
For example, \(f(x)=x^2\) takes one number \(x\) and returns one number \(x^2\). This is already powerful. We can ask for slopes, tangent lines, areas under curves, maximum values, and differential equations.
But modern quantitative problems rarely depend on only one variable.
A temperature model may depend on longitude, latitude, altitude, and time:
\[
T=T(x,y,z,t).
\]
A statistical loss function may depend on many model parameters:
\[
L=L(\beta_0,\beta_1,\dots,\beta_p).
\]
A neural network may have millions or billions of parameters, and training means minimizing a function of all of those parameters:
\[
\text{loss} = L(w_1,w_2,\dots,w_N).
\]
A probability density for two continuous random variables is a function
\[
p:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R,
\qquad
(x,y)\mapsto p(x,y).
\]
A velocity field assigns a vector to each point in space:
\[
F:\mathbb R^3\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
So the core move is this:
\[
\text{one input} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{many inputs}.
\]

Bridge to MS preparation. For MS Applied Mathematics, MS Statistics, data science, AI, and machine learning, the most important mental shift is to see formulas as functions on spaces. A loss function is a surface or landscape. A density is a surface or high-dimensional object whose total mass is one. A dynamical system is motion through a state space. A machine learning model is a map from features to predictions.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.4 1.2 Inputs, outputs, and spaces
A function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The input set is the domain. The output set is the codomain. In calculus, the most common spaces are Euclidean spaces:
\[
\mathbb R,
\quad
\mathbb R^2,
\quad
\mathbb R^3,
\quad
\mathbb R^n.
\]
The space \(\mathbb R^n\) consists of ordered \(n\)-tuples of real numbers:
\[
\mathbb R^n = \{(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n): x_i\in \mathbb R\}.
\]
The same \(n\)-tuple may be interpreted in different ways depending on the model.







Mathematical object
Possible interpretation




\((x,y)\)
A point in the plane, longitude and latitude, two features of an observation


\((x,y,z)\)
A point in space, three physical coordinates, RGB color values


\((x_1,\dots,x_n)\)
A data vector, a parameter vector, a state vector, a list of measurements


\(f(x,y)\)
Height, temperature, cost, density, loss, utility


\(F(x,y,z)\)
Vector field, force field, velocity field, gradient flow




1.4.1 Scalar-valued functions
A scalar-valued function has real-number output:
\[
f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R.
\]
Examples include:
\[
f(x,y)=x^2+y^2,
\qquad
T(x,y,z)=x^2e^y+\ln z,
\qquad
L(w,b)=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m (wx_i+b-y_i)^2.
\]
Scalar-valued functions are the main subject of partial derivatives, gradients, optimization, and multiple integrals.


1.4.2 Vector-valued functions
A vector-valued function has vector output:
\[
F:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m.
\]
Examples include:
\[
r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle,
\qquad
F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle,
\qquad
G(x,y,z)=\langle yz,xz,xy\rangle.
\]
Vector-valued functions appear in space curves, vector fields, motion, systems of equations, neural networks, and transformations.






TipNotation convention



In this book, vectors may be written as ordered tuples \((a,b,c)\) or angle brackets \(\langle a,b,c\rangle\). In applied and computational contexts, we often use a simple letter such as \(x\) or \(v\) for a vector when the context is clear.

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.5 1.3 A comparison with single-variable calculus
The table below gives a roadmap for the course.








Single-variable idea
Multivariable version
Modern interpretation




Number \(x\)
Vector \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\)
Feature vector or parameter vector


Function \(f(x)\)
Function \(f(x_1,\dots,x_n)\)
Loss, density, energy, response surface


Graph \(y=f(x)\)
Surface \(z=f(x,y)\) or high-dimensional graph
Landscape


Slope \(f'(a)\)
Partial derivatives and gradient \(\nabla f(a)\)
Sensitivity and learning signal


Tangent line
Tangent plane or linear approximation
Local linear model


Critical point \(f'(a)=0\)
Critical point \(\nabla f(a)=0\)
Candidate optimum or saddle point


Integral \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\)
Double/triple integral
Accumulation, mass, probability


Substitution
Change of variables and Jacobian
Transformation of densities and coordinates


Fundamental theorem of calculus
Green, Stokes, divergence theorem
Conservation and flow laws



The unifying theme is that calculus studies change and accumulation. Multivariable calculus studies change and accumulation when inputs live in spaces with dimension larger than one.

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
section: 1.6 1.4 Four ways to represent a function
text:
1.6 1.4 Four ways to represent a function
A modern calculus course should move comfortably among four representations:

Verbal: a description in words.
Numerical: a table, dataset, or sampled values.
Visual: a graph, contour plot, heatmap, vector field, or surface.
Algebraic: a formula.

A fifth representation is now equally important:

Computational: a function implemented in code.


1.6.1 Example: temperature on a plate
Suppose the temperature on a rectangular metal plate is modeled by
\[
T(x,y)=80-2x^2-y^2.
\]
The same object can be represented in several ways.







Representation
Description




Verbal
Temperature is highest near the origin and decreases quadratically away from it.


Algebraic
\(T(x,y)=80-2x^2-y^2\)


Visual
Surface plot or contour plot


Numerical
Table of values on a grid of \((x,y)\) points


Computational
A Python function T(x, y)




import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 200)
y = np.linspace(-5, 5, 200)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
T = 80 - 2*X**2 - Y**2

plt.figure()
cs = plt.contour(X, Y, T, levels=15)
plt.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8)
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.gca().set_aspect("equal")
plt.title("Level curves of T(x,y)=80-2x^2-y^2")
plt.show()







Figure 1.1: Contour plot for the temperature model \(T(x,y)=80-2x^2-y^2\).





The contour plot does not draw the full surface in three dimensions. Instead, it draws curves where the function has constant value. These curves are called level curves. They are one of the most important visualization tools in multivariable calculus.

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
section: 1.7 1.5 Graphs, level curves, and level sets
text:
1.7 1.5 Graphs, level curves, and level sets
For a function of one variable \(f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\), the graph is a subset of \(\mathbb R^2\):
\[
\{(x,y)\in\mathbb R^2: y=f(x)\}.
\]
For a function of two variables \(f:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\), the graph is a subset of \(\mathbb R^3\):
\[
\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^3: z=f(x,y)\}.
\]
For example,
\[
f(x,y)=x^2+y^2
\]
has graph
\[
z=x^2+y^2,
\]
an elliptic paraboloid.
A level curve is a curve where the output is constant:
\[
f(x,y)=c.
\]
For \(f(x,y)=x^2+y^2\), the level curves are
\[
x^2+y^2=c,
\]
which are circles centered at the origin when \(c>0\).

from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

x = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 120)
y = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 120)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = X**2 + Y**2

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, linewidth=0, antialiased=True, alpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_zlabel("z")
ax.set_title("Surface z = x^2 + y^2")
plt.show()







Figure 1.2: The surface \(z=x^2+y^2\) shows a simple multivariable landscape.











ImportantWhy level sets matter



In higher dimensions, full graphs are usually impossible to draw. Level sets remain useful. For \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\), a level set has the form
\[
\{x\in\mathbb R^n: f(x)=c\}.
\]
In statistics, level sets of a likelihood or log-likelihood show parameter values with equal fit. In machine learning, level sets of a loss function show parameter values with equal training error.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.8 1.6 Derivatives become directional
In one-variable calculus, the derivative is the limit
\[
f'(a)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}.
\]
This measures the instantaneous rate of change as \(x\) moves along the real line.
For a function \(f(x,y)\), the input point \((x,y)\) can move in many directions. From \((a,b)\), it can move in the \(x\) direction, the \(y\) direction, diagonally, along a curve, or along any unit vector \(u\).
This leads to several related ideas:

Partial derivatives: rates of change along coordinate directions.
Directional derivatives: rates of change along arbitrary directions.
Gradient: the vector that organizes all directional derivative information.
Jacobian: the matrix that represents the derivative of a vector-valued function.

For a scalar-valued function \(f(x,y)\), the gradient is
\[
\nabla f(x,y)=\left\langle \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}(x,y),\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}(x,y)\right\rangle.
\]
For \(f(x,y,z)\),
\[
\nabla f(x,y,z)=\left\langle \frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\frac{\partial f}{\partial y},\frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\right\rangle.
\]
The gradient points in the direction of fastest increase. This is why gradients are central in optimization and machine learning.

1.8.1 Example: a first gradient computation
Let
\[
f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}=2x,
\qquad
\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}=4y,
\]
so
\[
\nabla f(x,y)=\langle 2x,4y\rangle.
\]
At the point \((1,1)\),
\[
\nabla f(1,1)=\langle 2,4\rangle.
\]
This means that near \((1,1)\), the function increases fastest in the direction of \(\langle 2,4\rangle\).

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = X**2 + 2*Y**2

# Coarser grid for arrows
xs = np.linspace(-2, 2, 13)
ys = np.linspace(-2, 2, 13)
XS, YS = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
U = 2*XS
V = 4*YS

plt.figure()
plt.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=14)
plt.quiver(XS, YS, U, V)
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.gca().set_aspect("equal")
plt.title("Gradient field for f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2")
plt.show()







Figure 1.3: Gradient vectors are perpendicular to level curves and point toward increasing values.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.9 1.7 Integrals become accumulation over regions
In one-variable calculus, the definite integral
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
\]
accumulates values of \(f\) over an interval.
In multivariable calculus, we accumulate over regions and solids:
\[
\iint_R f(x,y)\,dA,
\qquad
\iiint_E f(x,y,z)\,dV.
\]
If \(f(x,y)\ge 0\), then
\[
\iint_R f(x,y)\,dA
\]
can be interpreted as the volume under the surface \(z=f(x,y)\) above the region \(R\).
If \(\rho(x,y,z)\) is a density, then
\[
\iiint_E \rho(x,y,z)\,dV
\]
is the mass of the object occupying the solid region \(E\).
If \(p(x,y)\) is a joint probability density, then
\[
\iint_R p(x,y)\,dA
\]
is the probability that \((X,Y)\) lies in the region \(R\).






NoteStatistics preview



For a joint density \(p(x,y)\), total probability means
\[
\iint_{\mathbb R^2} p(x,y)\,dA = 1.
\]
Expected values, variances, covariances, and conditional distributions are all built from multivariable integrals.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.10 1.8 The geometry of optimization
Optimization is one of the main bridges from calculus to statistics and machine learning.
In one variable, we minimize or maximize a curve \(f(x)\). In several variables, we minimize or maximize a surface or high-dimensional landscape \(f(x_1,\dots,x_n)\).
A basic unconstrained optimization problem is
\[
\min_{x\in\mathbb R^n} f(x).
\]
For a differentiable function, candidate optima often satisfy
\[
\nabla f(x)=0.
\]
In machine learning, the function \(f\) is often a loss function. Training a model means finding parameters that make the loss small.

1.10.1 Example: least-squares loss for a line
Suppose we have data points \((x_i,y_i)\) and want to fit a line
\[
\widehat y = wx+b.
\]
The mean squared error is
\[
L(w,b)=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m (wx_i+b-y_i)^2.
\]
This is a function of two variables, \(w\) and \(b\). The input point \((w,b)\) is not a physical point in space; it is a point in parameter space.

rng = np.random.default_rng(2321)
x_data = np.linspace(-2, 2, 20)
y_data = 1.5*x_data - 0.4 + rng.normal(scale=0.35, size=x_data.shape)

w = np.linspace(-1, 4, 120)
b = np.linspace(-2, 2, 120)
W, B = np.meshgrid(w, b)

Loss = np.zeros_like(W)
for xi, yi in zip(x_data, y_data):
    Loss += (W*xi + B - yi)**2
Loss = Loss / len(x_data)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(W, B, Loss, linewidth=0, antialiased=True, alpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlabel("w")
ax.set_ylabel("b")
ax.set_zlabel("L(w,b)")
ax.set_title("Least-squares loss surface")
plt.show()







Figure 1.4: A least-squares loss surface over slope \(w\) and intercept \(b\).





The goal is to move downhill on this surface. In later chapters, gradient descent will update parameters by
\[
(w,b) \leftarrow (w,b)-\eta\nabla L(w,b),
\]
where \(\eta>0\) is a step size called the learning rate.

AI connection. Training a neural network is conceptually similar to minimizing \(L(w,b)\), but the parameter vector may have millions of components. The derivative is not just a slope. It is a high-dimensional gradient, and the chain rule becomes backpropagation.

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.12 1.10 High-dimensional thinking
Most of this book builds geometric intuition in \(\mathbb R^2\) and \(\mathbb R^3\). But the real goal is to understand ideas that work in \(\mathbb R^n\).
A data point with \(n\) features can be written as
\[
x=(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb R^n.
\]
A linear prediction model may have the form
\[
\widehat y=\beta_0+\beta_1x_1+\cdots+\beta_nx_n.
\]
A loss function for the parameters \(\beta=(\beta_0,\beta_1,\dots,\beta_n)\) may be written as
\[
L(\beta)=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m (\beta_0+\beta_1x_{i1}+\cdots+\beta_nx_{in}-y_i)^2.
\]
This is a scalar-valued function on a high-dimensional parameter space. Even when we cannot draw the graph, we can still compute:
\[
\nabla L(\beta),
\qquad
\nabla^2 L(\beta),
\qquad
\min_\beta L(\beta).
\]
These are multivariable calculus objects.






WarningCommon misconception



High-dimensional calculus is not a different subject. It is the same subject with fewer pictures and more structure. We use lower-dimensional pictures to build intuition, then rely on vectors, matrices, gradients, Jacobians, Hessians, and integrals to work in general dimensions.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.13 1.11 Worked examples

1.13.1 Example 1. Classifying functions by domain and output
Classify each function by its domain and output type.

\(f(x)=x^2+1\).
\(g(x,y)=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\).
\(T(x,y,z)=x^2e^y+\ln z\).
\(r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle\).
\(F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle\).

Solution.

\(f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a scalar-valued function of one variable.
\(g:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\) is a scalar-valued function of two variables.
\(T\) is a scalar-valued function of three variables. Its natural domain is \(z>0\) because \(\ln z\) is defined only for positive \(z\).
\(r:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R^3\) is a vector-valued function of one variable. It parameterizes a curve in space.
\(F:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R^2\) is a vector field on the plane.



1.13.2 Example 2. Reading a level curve
Let
\[
f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2.
\]
Describe the level curve \(f(x,y)=8\).
Solution.
The level curve is
\[
x^2+2y^2=8.
\]
Dividing by \(8\) gives
\[
\frac{x^2}{8}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1.
\]
This is an ellipse centered at the origin. The \(x\)-intercepts are \(\pm\sqrt{8}=\pm 2\sqrt 2\), and the \(y\)-intercepts are \(\pm 2\).
Interpretation. On this ellipse, every point has the same function value \(8\). If \(f\) is a loss function, all parameter pairs on this ellipse have equal loss. If \(f\) is height, all points on this ellipse have equal elevation.


1.13.3 Example 3. From a table to partial rates of change
Suppose \(T(x,y)\) is temperature on a plate. The table below gives approximate values.



\(T(x,y)\)
\(y=0\)
\(y=1\)
\(y=2\)




\(x=0\)
80
78
72


\(x=1\)
76
74
68


\(x=2\)
64
62
56



Estimate the rate of change in the \(x\) direction near \((1,1)\) using centered differences.
Solution.
Holding \(y=1\) fixed, use the values at \(x=0\) and \(x=2\):
\[
\frac{T(2,1)-T(0,1)}{2-0}
=
\frac{62-78}{2}
=
-8.
\]
So near \((1,1)\), the temperature decreases by about \(8\) units per unit increase in \(x\), with \(y\) held fixed.
Interpretation. This is the numerical idea behind a partial derivative. Instead of needing a formula, we estimate local change from nearby data.


1.13.4 Example 4. A loss function as a multivariable function
Let one data point be \((x,y)=(2,5)\), and consider the prediction model
\[
\widehat y=wx+b.
\]
The squared error for this one point is
\[
L(w,b)=(2w+b-5)^2.
\]
Find all parameter pairs \((w,b)\) with zero loss.
Solution.
Zero loss means
\[
(2w+b-5)^2=0.
\]
Thus
\[
2w+b-5=0,
\]
or
\[
b=5-2w.
\]
So all points \((w,b)\) on the line \(b=5-2w\) give perfect prediction for this single data point.
Interpretation. A single data point does not determine a unique line. With more data points, the loss surface changes, and the best parameters usually become more constrained.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking

section: 1.14 1.12 Python mini-lab: from curves to surfaces to loss landscapes
text:
1.14 1.12 Python mini-lab: from curves to surfaces to loss landscapes
This mini-lab previews the computational style of the book. Each full chapter will also have a separate notebook in the labs/ folder.

1.14.1 Task A. A one-variable curve

x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
y = x**3 - 3*x

plt.figure()
plt.plot(x, y)
plt.axhline(0, linewidth=0.8)
plt.axvline(0, linewidth=0.8)
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("f(x)")
plt.title("One-variable function: f(x)=x^3-3x")
plt.show()







Figure 1.5: A one-variable function gives a curve.







1.14.2 Task B. A two-variable contour plot

x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 250)
y = np.linspace(-3, 3, 250)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = X**2 - Y**2

plt.figure()
cs = plt.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=21)
plt.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8)
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.gca().set_aspect("equal")
plt.title("Level curves of f(x,y)=x^2-y^2")
plt.show()







Figure 1.6: A two-variable function can be studied through level curves.







1.14.3 Task C. A first numerical gradient
The gradient can be approximated by finite differences. For
\[
f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2,
\]
we know exactly that
\[
\nabla f(x,y)=\langle 2x,4y\rangle.
\]
The following code approximates the gradient at a point.



Listing 1.1


def f_xy(x, y):
    return x**2 + 2*y**2

def numerical_gradient(f, x0, y0, h=1e-5):
    df_dx = (f(x0 + h, y0) - f(x0 - h, y0)) / (2*h)
    df_dy = (f(x0, y0 + h) - f(x0, y0 - h)) / (2*h)
    return np.array([df_dx, df_dy])

point = (1.0, 1.0)
print("Numerical gradient:", numerical_gradient(f_xy, *point))
print("Exact gradient:    ", np.array([2*point[0], 4*point[1]]))

Numerical gradient: [2. 4.]
Exact gradient:     [2. 4.]






1.14.4 Task D. A first gradient descent experiment

def f_val(x, y):
    return x**2 + 2*y**2

def grad_f(x, y):
    return np.array([2*x, 4*y])

eta = 0.12
point = np.array([2.5, 2.0])
path = [point.copy()]

for _ in range(25):
    point = point - eta * grad_f(point[0], point[1])
    path.append(point.copy())

path = np.array(path)

x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 250)
y = np.linspace(-3, 3, 250)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = f_val(X, Y)

plt.figure()
plt.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=20)
plt.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], marker="o")
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.gca().set_aspect("equal")
plt.title("Gradient descent for f(x,y)=x^2+2y^2")
plt.show()







Figure 1.7: Gradient descent paths move downhill on a loss landscape.

text:
1.16 1.14 Practice problems

1.16.1 Conceptual problems

Give three examples of functions of one variable and three examples of functions of several variables. For each example, identify the input and output.
Explain the difference between a point in physical space and a point in parameter space.
Why can \(f:\mathbb R^3\to\mathbb R\) not be graphed in ordinary three-dimensional space in the same way as \(f:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\)?
Explain why a contour map is useful even when a surface plot is available.
In your own words, explain why derivatives become more complicated when the input has more than one dimension.



1.16.2 Computation problems

Classify each function by domain and codomain:

\(f(x)=e^{-x^2}\)
\(g(x,y)=x^2-y^2\)
\(h(x,y,z)=\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2}\)
\(r(t)=\langle t,t^2,t^3\rangle\)
\(F(x,y)=\langle x+y,x-y\rangle\)

For \(f(x,y)=x^2+y^2\), describe the level curves \(f(x,y)=1\), \(f(x,y)=4\), and \(f(x,y)=9\).
For \(f(x,y)=x^2+4y^2\), describe the level curve \(f(x,y)=16\).
Compute \(\nabla f(x,y)\) for \(f(x,y)=3x^2+xy+y^2\).
Compute \(\nabla T(x,y,z)\) for \(T(x,y,z)=x^2e^y+\ln z\).
Let \(L(w,b)=(2w+b-5)^2+(w+b-3)^2\). Compute \(L(1,1)\) and \(L(2,1)\).
Let \(p(x,y)=Ce^{-(x^2+y^2)}\). Explain what condition \(C\) must satisfy if \(p\) is to be a probability density on \(\mathbb R^2\).



1.16.3 Python problems

Modify the contour plot for \(f(x,y)=x^2+y^2\) to plot \(f(x,y)=x^2+4y^2\). Describe how the level curves change.
Write a Python function for \(f(x,y)=\sin(xy)\). Plot its contour map on \([-3,3]\times[-3,3]\).
Numerically approximate the gradient of \(f(x,y)=\sin(x)+\cos(y)\) at \((0,0)\) using finite differences.
Create a least-squares loss surface for data generated from \(y=2x+1\) with small random noise.
Run gradient descent on \(f(x,y)=x^2+y^2\) from three different starting points. Plot all three paths.



1.16.4 Discussion and writing problems

Write a short paragraph explaining the phrase: “A loss function is a landscape.”
Write a short paragraph explaining why multiple integrals are important for probability and statistics.
Choose one application area: physics, economics, biology, statistics, AI, or machine learning. Describe one natural function of several variables in that area.

title: 1  Chapter 1. From Single-Variable Calculus to Multivariable Thinking
text:
1.17 1.15 Chapter summary
Single-variable calculus studies functions with one input. Multivariable calculus studies functions whose inputs live in spaces such as \(\mathbb R^2\), \(\mathbb R^3\), and \(\mathbb R^n\). This makes the geometry richer and the applications broader.
The main ideas of the book are already visible:

A function \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\) can represent a surface, density, loss, risk, energy, or potential.
When there are many input directions, derivatives become partial derivatives, directional derivatives, gradients, Jacobians, and Hessians.
Integrals become accumulation over regions, solids, surfaces, and probability spaces.
Optimization becomes the study of landscapes in parameter space.
Python helps us visualize, approximate, and experiment with objects that are difficult or impossible to draw by hand.

The next chapters build the geometric foundation: Euclidean space, vectors, dot products, projections, lines, planes, cross products, and curves. These objects are the language needed for functions, derivatives, integrals, and vector calculus.

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.1 Chapter overview
Multivariable calculus begins by changing what an input is. In single-variable calculus, an input is one number \(x\). In multivariable calculus, an input may be a point \((x,y)\) in the plane, a point \((x,y,z)\) in space, or a long vector
\[
(x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n)
\]
whose components store measurements, features, parameters, or decisions. The common home for all of these objects is Euclidean space.
This chapter builds the geometric language used throughout the book. We will study points, coordinates, distance, projections, spheres, balls, and equations that describe regions in two, three, and higher dimensions. The same ideas will later support tangent planes, gradients, multiple integrals, optimization, probability densities, and machine-learning loss functions.

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
section: 2.3 2.1 From the real line to \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.3 2.1 From the real line to \(\mathbb R^n\)
The real line \(\mathbb R\) consists of real numbers. A point on the line is described by one coordinate:
\[
x\in\mathbb R.
\]
The plane \(\mathbb R^2\) consists of ordered pairs:
\[
(x,y)\in\mathbb R^2.
\]
Three-dimensional space \(\mathbb R^3\) consists of ordered triples:
\[
(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^3.
\]
More generally, \(n\)-dimensional Euclidean space is the set
\[
\mathbb R^n=\{(x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n):x_i\in\mathbb R\text{ for }i=1,\ldots,n\}.
\]
Each number \(x_i\) is called a coordinate or component.






TipA useful mental model



Think of \(\mathbb R^n\) as a universal coordinate system. In geometry, a point in \(\mathbb R^3\) may be a location in space. In statistics, a point in \(\mathbb R^n\) may be one observation with \(n\) measured features. In machine learning, a point in \(\mathbb R^n\) may be a vector of model weights.



2.3.1 Examples

\((3,-2)\) is a point in \(\mathbb R^2\).
\((1,0,-4)\) is a point in \(\mathbb R^3\).
\((2,-1,0,5)\) is a point in \(\mathbb R^4\).
\((x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_{100})\) can represent a data record with 100 numerical features.
\((w_0,w_1,\ldots,w_p)\) can represent the parameters of a linear regression model.

We can draw \(\mathbb R^2\) and \(\mathbb R^3\), but we usually cannot directly draw \(\mathbb R^n\) for large \(n\). The power of Euclidean geometry is that the formulas still work.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.4 2.2 Points, vectors, and displacement
A point records position. A vector records displacement, direction, or magnitude. The same ordered tuple may be used for either purpose, depending on context.
If
\[
A=(a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n),\qquad B=(b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_n),
\]
then the displacement from \(A\) to \(B\) is
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A=(b_1-a_1,b_2-a_2,\ldots,b_n-a_n).
\]
For example, if \(A=(1,-2,3)\) and \(B=(2,1,5)\), then
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=(2-1,1-(-2),5-3)=(1,3,2).
\]






ImportantPoint versus vector



The notation \((1,3,2)\) can mean either a point or a vector. As a point, it is a location. As a vector, it is a displacement. The algebra is the same, but the interpretation is different.



import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

A = np.array([1, 1])
B = np.array([4, 3])
v = B - A

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter([A[0], B[0]], [A[1], B[1]])
ax.text(A[0] - 0.15, A[1] - 0.25, 'A')
ax.text(B[0] + 0.10, B[1] + 0.05, 'B')
ax.arrow(A[0], A[1], v[0], v[1], head_width=0.12, length_includes_head=True)
ax.set_xlim(0, 5)
ax.set_ylim(0, 4)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.grid(True)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
plt.show()







Figure 2.1: A displacement vector from A to B in the plane.

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.5 2.3 Coordinates, axes, planes, and octants
In \(\mathbb R^2\), the coordinate axes are the \(x\)-axis and the \(y\)-axis. The two axes divide the plane into four quadrants.
In \(\mathbb R^3\), the coordinate axes are the \(x\)-, \(y\)-, and \(z\)-axes. We use a right-handed coordinate system. The three coordinate planes are
\[
\begin{aligned}
xy\text{-plane}:&\quad z=0,\\
yz\text{-plane}:&\quad x=0,\\
xz\text{-plane}:&\quad y=0.
\end{aligned}
\]
The coordinate planes divide \(\mathbb R^3\) into eight octants. The first octant is
\[
x\ge 0,\qquad y\ge 0,\qquad z\ge 0.
\]

2.5.1 Projection onto coordinate planes
For a point \(P=(a,b,c)\) in \(\mathbb R^3\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{projection onto the }xy\text{-plane}&=(a,b,0),\\
\text{projection onto the }yz\text{-plane}&=(0,b,c),\\
\text{projection onto the }xz\text{-plane}&=(a,0,c).
\end{aligned}
\]
The idea is simple: to project onto a coordinate plane, set the missing coordinate equal to zero.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

P = np.array([2, 3, 4])
P_xy = np.array([2, 3, 0])
P_yz = np.array([0, 3, 4])
P_xz = np.array([2, 0, 4])
points = [P, P_xy, P_yz, P_xz]
labels = ['P', 'xy projection', 'yz projection', 'xz projection']

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
for Q, label in zip(points, labels):
    ax.scatter(Q[0], Q[1], Q[2])
    ax.text(Q[0], Q[1], Q[2], '  ' + label)

for Q in [P_xy, P_yz, P_xz]:
    ax.plot([P[0], Q[0]], [P[1], Q[1]], [P[2], Q[2]], linestyle='--')

ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_xlim(0, 4)
ax.set_ylim(0, 4)
ax.set_zlim(0, 5)
plt.show()







Figure 2.2: A point in \(\mathbb R^3\) and its projections onto the coordinate planes.







2.5.2 Worked example 2.1
Let \(P=(5,-2,7)\). Find its projections onto the coordinate planes.
Solution.
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_{xy}&=(5,-2,0),\\
P_{yz}&=(0,-2,7),\\
P_{xz}&=(5,0,7).
\end{aligned}
\]
The \(xy\)-projection removes height. The \(yz\)-projection removes the \(x\)-coordinate. The \(xz\)-projection removes the \(y\)-coordinate.

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.6 2.4 Distance in Euclidean space
Distance is the most important measurement in Euclidean geometry. It is also fundamental in statistics and machine learning, where distance measures similarity, error, residual size, and model change.
For points
\[
A=(a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n),\qquad B=(b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_n)
\]
in \(\mathbb R^n\), the Euclidean distance between \(A\) and \(B\) is
\[
d(A,B)=\sqrt{(a_1-b_1)^2+(a_2-b_2)^2+\cdots+(a_n-b_n)^2}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
d(A,B)=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n(a_i-b_i)^2}.
\]
In \(\mathbb R^3\), this becomes
\[
d(A,B)=\sqrt{(a_1-b_1)^2+(a_2-b_2)^2+(a_3-b_3)^2}.
\]

2.6.1 Worked example 2.2
Find the distance from \(P=(1,2,3)\) to \(Q=(3,4,2)\).
Solution.
\[
\begin{aligned}
d(P,Q)
&=\sqrt{(1-3)^2+(2-4)^2+(3-2)^2}\\
&=\sqrt{4+4+1}\\
&=3.
\end{aligned}
\]


2.6.2 Worked example 2.3
Find the distance between
\[
a=(2,-1,0,4),\qquad b=(-1,3,5,2)
\]
in \(\mathbb R^4\).
Solution.
\[
\begin{aligned}
d(a,b)
&=\sqrt{(2-(-1))^2+(-1-3)^2+(0-5)^2+(4-2)^2}\\
&=\sqrt{3^2+(-4)^2+(-5)^2+2^2}\\
&=\sqrt{9+16+25+4}\\
&=\sqrt{54}=3\sqrt 6.
\end{aligned}
\]


2.6.3 Python check

import numpy as np

P = np.array([1, 2, 3])
Q = np.array([3, 4, 2])
distance = np.linalg.norm(P - Q)
distance

3.0

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.7 2.5 Spheres and balls
A sphere is the set of points at a fixed distance from a center. A ball is the set of points within a fixed distance from a center.
In \(\mathbb R^3\), the sphere with center \(C=(h,k,\ell)\) and radius \(r\) has equation
\[
(x-h)^2+(y-k)^2+(z-\ell)^2=r^2.
\]
If the center is the origin, this becomes
\[
x^2+y^2+z^2=r^2.
\]
In \(\mathbb R^n\), the sphere with center \(c=(c_1,\ldots,c_n)\) and radius \(r\) is
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-c_i)^2=r^2.
\]
The corresponding closed ball is
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-c_i)^2\le r^2,
\]
and the corresponding open ball is
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-c_i)^2<r^2.
\]






TipBoundary versus region



The equation \(x^2+y^2+z^2=9\) describes only the boundary sphere of radius \(3\). The inequality \(x^2+y^2+z^2\le 9\) describes the solid ball inside and including the boundary.



import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = 2
u = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 80)
v = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 40)
x = r * np.outer(np.cos(u), np.sin(v))
y = r * np.outer(np.sin(u), np.sin(v))
z = r * np.outer(np.ones_like(u), np.cos(v))

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(x, y, z, alpha=0.6, linewidth=0)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 2.3: A sphere centered at the origin in \(\mathbb R^3\).






2.7.1 Worked example 2.4
Find the equation of the sphere with center \((2,-5,1)\) and radius \(2\).
Solution.
Using \((x-h)^2+(y-k)^2+(z-\ell)^2=r^2\), we get
\[
(x-2)^2+(y+5)^2+(z-1)^2=4.
\]


2.7.2 Worked example 2.5
Find the equation of the sphere for which one diameter has endpoints
\[
A=(2,-1,1),\qquad B=(-2,-3,2).
\]
Solution.
The center is the midpoint of the diameter:
\[
C=\frac{A+B}{2}=\left(0,-2,\frac32\right).
\]
The diameter length is
\[
|AB|=\sqrt{(2-(-2))^2+(-1-(-3))^2+(1-2)^2}
=\sqrt{16+4+1}=\sqrt{21}.
\]
Therefore the radius is \(r=\sqrt{21}/2\), and the equation is
\[
x^2+(y+2)^2+\left(z-\frac32\right)^2=\frac{21}{4}.
\]

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
section: 2.8 2.6 Equations depend on the ambient space
text:
2.8 2.6 Equations depend on the ambient space
An equation does not describe an object until we know the space in which it lives.
For example:

In \(\mathbb R^2\), the equation \(y=2\) is a line.
In \(\mathbb R^3\), the equation \(y=2\) is a plane parallel to the \(xz\)-plane.
In \(\mathbb R^3\), the equation \(z=2\) is a plane parallel to the \(xy\)-plane.

Similarly, the equation
\[
x^2+y^2=4
\]
has different meanings in different spaces.
In \(\mathbb R^2\), it is a circle of radius \(2\) centered at the origin. In \(\mathbb R^3\), it is a cylinder of radius \(2\) around the \(z\)-axis, because \(z\) is free.
If we add the equation \(z=3\), then
\[
x^2+y^2=4,\qquad z=3
\]
describes a circle of radius \(2\) lying in the horizontal plane \(z=3\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 80)
z_vals = np.linspace(0, 4, 40)
Theta, Z = np.meshgrid(theta, z_vals)
X = 2*np.cos(Theta)
Y = 2*np.sin(Theta)

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.35, linewidth=0)
ax.plot(2*np.cos(theta), 2*np.sin(theta), 3*np.ones_like(theta), linewidth=3)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 2.4: The surface \(x^2+y^2=4\) is a cylinder in \(\mathbb R^3\); intersecting it with \(z=3\) gives a circle.






2.8.1 Worked example 2.6
Describe the surface \(x=y\) in \(\mathbb R^3\).
Solution.
The equation \(x=y\) places one restriction on three variables. The variable \(z\) is free. Therefore the surface is a plane through the \(z\)-axis. Points on the plane have the form
\[
(x,y,z)=(t,t,s),
\]
where \(s\) and \(t\) are real numbers.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(-2, 2, 30)
s = np.linspace(-2, 2, 30)
T, S = np.meshgrid(t, s)
X = T
Y = T
Z = S

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.55, linewidth=0)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 2.5: The equation \(x=y\) describes a plane in \(\mathbb R^3\).

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.9 2.7 Regions described by inequalities
Equations describe boundaries. Inequalities describe regions.
The inequality
\[
x^2+y^2+z^2\le 9
\]
describes the solid ball of radius \(3\) centered at the origin.
The inequality
\[
x^2+y^2+z^2>9
\]
describes all points outside that ball.

2.9.1 Worked example 2.7
Describe the region
\[
x^2+y^2+z^2>2z.
\]
Solution.
Move all terms to one side and complete the square in \(z\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
x^2+y^2+z^2&>2z,\\
x^2+y^2+z^2-2z&>0,\\
x^2+y^2+(z-1)^2-1&>0,\\
x^2+y^2+(z-1)^2&>1.
\end{aligned}
\]
This is the set of all points whose distance from \((0,0,1)\) is greater than \(1\). Thus the region is the outside of the sphere of radius \(1\) centered at \((0,0,1)\). The boundary sphere itself is not included because the inequality is strict.






WarningCommon mistake



The inequality \(x^2+y^2+(z-1)^2>1\) is not the sphere. It is the outside region. The sphere is the boundary where equality holds.



import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = 1
center = np.array([0, 0, 1])
u = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 80)
v = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 40)
x = center[0] + r * np.outer(np.cos(u), np.sin(v))
y = center[1] + r * np.outer(np.sin(u), np.sin(v))
z = center[2] + r * np.outer(np.ones_like(u), np.cos(v))

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(x, y, z, alpha=0.6, linewidth=0)
ax.scatter([0], [0], [1])
ax.text(0, 0, 1, '  center')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 2.6: Boundary sphere for the region \(x^2+y^2+z^2>2z\).

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
section: 2.10 2.8 Open balls, closed balls, and neighborhoods
text:
2.10 2.8 Open balls, closed balls, and neighborhoods
In one-variable calculus, limits are described using intervals around a point. In multivariable calculus, the natural replacement is a ball.
For \(a\in\mathbb R^n\) and \(r>0\), the open ball centered at \(a\) with radius \(r\) is
\[
B_r(a)=\{x\in\mathbb R^n:d(x,a)<r\}.
\]
The closed ball centered at \(a\) with radius \(r\) is
\[
\overline B_r(a)=\{x\in\mathbb R^n:d(x,a)\le r\}.
\]
The boundary is the sphere
\[
S_r(a)=\{x\in\mathbb R^n:d(x,a)=r\}.
\]
These objects are essential for defining limits, continuity, open sets, and local behavior.






NotePreview of limits



When we say \((x,y)\) approaches \((a,b)\), we mean the distance \(\sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}\) approaches \(0\). This is why Euclidean distance appears immediately in the definition of a multivariable limit.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
section: 2.11 2.9 High-dimensional Euclidean space and data geometry
text:
2.11 2.9 High-dimensional Euclidean space and data geometry
A data set with \(m\) observations and \(n\) features can be represented as a matrix
\[
X=\begin{bmatrix}
- & x_1^T & -\\
- & x_2^T & -\\
& \vdots & \\
- & x_m^T & -
\end{bmatrix},
\]
where each row \(x_i\in\mathbb R^n\) is a data vector.
Euclidean distance is often used to measure similarity:
\[
d(x_i,x_j)=\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^n(x_{ik}-x_{jk})^2}.
\]
This idea appears in:

nearest-neighbor classification;
clustering;
anomaly detection;
least-squares regression;
gradient-based optimization;
measuring error between predicted and observed vectors.

However, high-dimensional geometry can behave differently from low-dimensional geometry.

2.11.1 Experiment: distances in high dimension
The following experiment samples random points from the unit cube \([0,1]^n\) and compares pairwise distances.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2321)
dimensions = [2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100]
means = []
stds = []

for n in dimensions:
    A = rng.random((1000, n))
    B = rng.random((1000, n))
    distances = np.linalg.norm(A - B, axis=1)
    means.append(distances.mean())
    stds.append(distances.std())

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.errorbar(dimensions, means, yerr=stds, marker='o', capsize=4)
ax.set_xlabel('dimension n')
ax.set_ylabel('distance between random points')
ax.set_title('Distances in $[0,1]^n$')
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()







Figure 2.7: Typical distances between random points increase and concentrate as dimension grows.





The mean distance tends to grow as dimension increases. More importantly, relative distances can become less informative when many points are far apart in similar ways. This is one reason high-dimensional data analysis often needs normalization, projection, dimension reduction, and carefully chosen metrics.






TipConnection to AI



Modern AI systems represent images, text, users, and documents as high-dimensional vectors. Euclidean geometry gives the first language for comparing such objects, but high-dimensional geometry also warns us that intuition from the plane may be misleading.

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.12 2.10 Standard geometric objects in \(\mathbb R^n\)
The following objects appear repeatedly in multivariable calculus and applied mathematics.








Object
Description
Typical equation or inequality




Line in \(\mathbb R^2\)
one-dimensional straight set
\(ax+by=c\)


Plane in \(\mathbb R^3\)
two-dimensional flat surface
\(ax+by+cz=d\)


Hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\)
codimension-one flat set
\(a_1x_1+\cdots+a_nx_n=b\)


Sphere
fixed distance from a center
\(\sum_i(x_i-c_i)^2=r^2\)


Ball
distance at most \(r\) from a center
\(\sum_i(x_i-c_i)^2\le r^2\)


Cylinder in \(\mathbb R^3\)
one variable free
\(x^2+y^2=r^2\)


First octant
nonnegative coordinates in \(\mathbb R^3\)
\(x\ge0\), \(y\ge0\), \(z\ge0\)




2.12.1 Hyperplanes and machine learning
A hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\) has equation
\[
w_1x_1+w_2x_2+\cdots+w_nx_n=b.
\]
In machine learning, this is the basic form of a linear decision boundary. For example, a classifier may assign one label when
\[
w\cdot x-b>0
\]
and another label when
\[
w\cdot x-b<0.
\]
This is one reason multivariable calculus and linear algebra are both central to data science.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.13 2.11 Worked examples

2.13.1 Example 2.8: detecting collinearity using distances
Determine whether the points
\[
A=(2,4,2),\qquad B=(3,7,-2),\qquad C=(1,3,3)
\]
lie on a straight line.
Solution.
Compute the three distances:
\[
\begin{aligned}
|AB|&=\sqrt{(3-2)^2+(7-4)^2+(-2-2)^2}=\sqrt{26},\\
|AC|&=\sqrt{(1-2)^2+(3-4)^2+(3-2)^2}=\sqrt{3},\\
|BC|&=\sqrt{(1-3)^2+(3-7)^2+(3-(-2))^2}=\sqrt{45}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For three points to lie on a line, one of the three distances must equal the sum of the other two. Here this does not happen. Therefore the points do not lie on a straight line.
A more algebraic method is to compare displacement vectors:
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=(1,3,-4),\qquad \overrightarrow{AC}=(-1,-1,1).
\]
These are not scalar multiples of each other, so the three points are not collinear.


2.13.2 Example 2.9: intersections with coordinate planes
Consider the sphere
\[
(x-2)^2+(y+5)^2+(z-1)^2=4.
\]
Describe its intersection with the coordinate planes.
Solution.
For the \(xy\)-plane, set \(z=0\):
\[
(x-2)^2+(y+5)^2+1=4.
\]
Thus
\[
(x-2)^2+(y+5)^2=3,
\]
which is a circle in the plane \(z=0\).
For the \(xz\)-plane, set \(y=0\):
\[
(x-2)^2+25+(z-1)^2=4.
\]
This has no solution, so the sphere does not intersect the \(xz\)-plane.
For the \(yz\)-plane, set \(x=0\):
\[
4+(y+5)^2+(z-1)^2=4.
\]
Thus
\[
(y+5)^2+(z-1)^2=0,
\]
so the intersection is the single point \((0,-5,1)\).






ImportantCheck the signs



The center of \((x-2)^2+(y+5)^2+(z-1)^2=4\) is \((2,-5,1)\), not \((2,5,1)\). The sign inside the square is opposite the coordinate of the center.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.14 2.12 Computational practice: nearest points
The next small computation shows how distance is used in data analysis. Given a query point and several data points, the nearest neighbor is the point with smallest Euclidean distance.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
X = rng.normal(size=(20, 2))
query = np.array([0.75, 0.25])
dists = np.linalg.norm(X - query, axis=1)
idx = np.argmin(dists)
nearest = X[idx]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], label='data points')
ax.scatter([query[0]], [query[1]], marker='x', s=120, label='query point')
ax.scatter([nearest[0]], [nearest[1]], s=120, label='nearest point')
ax.plot([query[0], nearest[0]], [query[1], nearest[1]], linestyle='--')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.grid(True)
ax.legend()
plt.show()

idx, dists[idx]







Figure 2.8: Nearest-neighbor search uses Euclidean distance to compare data points.





The same idea extends to high-dimensional vectors. For example, two images may be compared by first converting them to vectors and then measuring the distance between those vectors.

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
section: 2.15 2.13 Common mistakes and how to avoid them
text:
2.15 2.13 Common mistakes and how to avoid them

2.15.1 Mistake 1: confusing a circle and a sphere
The equation
\[
x^2+y^2=4
\]
is a circle in \(\mathbb R^2\) but a cylinder in \(\mathbb R^3\). Always identify the ambient space.


2.15.2 Mistake 2: forgetting free variables
The equation \(z=2\) in \(\mathbb R^3\) does not describe one point. The variables \(x\) and \(y\) are free, so it describes a plane.


2.15.3 Mistake 3: using equality when the problem asks for a region
The equation
\[
x^2+y^2+z^2=1
\]
describes a sphere. The inequality
\[
x^2+y^2+z^2\le 1
\]
describes a solid ball.


2.15.4 Mistake 4: sign errors in sphere centers
The sphere
\[
(x-h)^2+(y-k)^2+(z-\ell)^2=r^2
\]
has center \((h,k,\ell)\). Thus \((y+5)^2\) corresponds to \(k=-5\).

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space $\mathbb R^n$

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.16 2.14 Summary
The main objects of this chapter are summarized below.

2.16.1 Euclidean space
\[
\mathbb R^n=\{(x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n):x_i\in\mathbb R\}.
\]


2.16.2 Displacement
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A.
\]


2.16.3 Distance
\[
d(A,B)=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n(a_i-b_i)^2}.
\]


2.16.4 Coordinate-plane projections in \(\mathbb R^3\)
For \(P=(a,b,c)\),
\[
P_{xy}=(a,b,0),\qquad P_{yz}=(0,b,c),\qquad P_{xz}=(a,0,c).
\]


2.16.5 Sphere in \(\mathbb R^3\)
\[
(x-h)^2+(y-k)^2+(z-\ell)^2=r^2.
\]


2.16.6 Ball in \(\mathbb R^n\)
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-c_i)^2\le r^2.
\]

title: 2  Chapter 2. Euclidean Space \(\mathbb R^n\)
text:
2.17 2.15 Exercises

2.17.1 Conceptual questions

Explain the difference between a point and a vector.
Why does the equation \(y=2\) describe different objects in \(\mathbb R^2\) and \(\mathbb R^3\)?
What is the difference between a sphere and a ball?
In \(\mathbb R^3\), why does \(x^2+y^2=4\) describe a cylinder?
Give an example of a real data point that could naturally be modeled as a point in \(\mathbb R^5\).



2.17.2 Computation and geometry

Find the distance between \(A=(2,-1,4)\) and \(B=(-3,2,1)\).
Find the displacement vector from \(A=(-1,5,2)\) to \(B=(4,0,-3)\).
Find the projections of \(P=(4,-2,6)\) onto the three coordinate planes.
Write the equation of the sphere with center \((1,-3,2)\) and radius \(5\).
Find the center and radius of

\[
(x+2)^2+(y-4)^2+(z+1)^2=16.
\]

Describe the region

\[
x^2+y^2+z^2\le 4.
\]

Describe the region

\[
x^2+y^2+(z+2)^2>9.
\]

Describe the surface \(x=3\) in \(\mathbb R^3\).
Describe the surface \(x^2+z^2=1\) in \(\mathbb R^3\).
Determine whether the points \((0,0,0)\), \((1,2,3)\), and \((2,4,6)\) lie on a line.



2.17.3 Applications and data geometry

A data set records height, weight, age, systolic blood pressure, and cholesterol for each patient. What is the natural Euclidean space for one patient record?
Two feature vectors are

\[
x=(1,2,0,3),\qquad y=(2,1,-1,5).
\]
Compute their Euclidean distance.

In a nearest-neighbor classifier, why might it be important to standardize features before computing Euclidean distances?
Suppose model parameters change from \(w=(1,0,-2)\) to \(w'=(0.5,0.2,-1.4)\). Compute the size of the parameter update.
Explain why high-dimensional data geometry can be different from the geometry of the plane.



2.17.4 Challenge problems

Find all points on the \(z\)-axis that are distance \(5\) from the point \((3,4,0)\).
Find the intersection of the sphere

\[
x^2+y^2+z^2=25
\]
with the plane \(z=4\).

Complete the square and describe the region

\[
x^2+y^2+z^2-4x+6y-2z\le 10.
\]

Show that the set of points equidistant from \(A=(1,0,0)\) and \(B=(-1,0,0)\) is a plane. What is its equation?
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb R^n\). Show that \(d(a,b)=d(b,a)\).

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.1 Chapter overview
Chapter 2 introduced Euclidean space as the natural home for points such as \((x,y)\), \((x,y,z)\), and \((x_1,\dots,x_n)\). This chapter develops the algebra and geometry of vectors in that space.
A vector can represent a displacement, a velocity, a force, a row of data, a direction of change, a model parameter, or a feature embedding. In multivariable calculus, vectors are the language used to describe motion, tangent lines, tangent planes, gradients, flux, optimization, and linear approximation. In statistics and machine learning, vectors are also used to store observations, coefficients, residuals, gradients, and learned representations.
The main ideas are:

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
section: 3.3 3.1 Vectors as displacement, direction, and data
text:
3.3 3.1 Vectors as displacement, direction, and data
A vector in \(\mathbb R^n\) is an ordered list
\[
v=(v_1,v_2,\ldots,v_n).
\]
Geometrically, we often draw a vector as an arrow. Algebraically, we compute with its components. The same component list can represent different kinds of objects:



Context
Vector meaning




Geometry
displacement, direction, normal vector


Physics
force, velocity, acceleration


Statistics
data point, residual vector, coefficient vector


Machine learning
feature vector, weight vector, gradient vector


Optimization
search direction, update direction



If \(A=(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\) and \(B=(b_1,\ldots,b_n)\) are points, the displacement from \(A\) to \(B\) is
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A=(b_1-a_1,\ldots,b_n-a_n).
\]

3.3.1 Example 3.1: A displacement vector in space
Let
\[
A=(1,-2,3), \qquad B=(2,1,5).
\]
Then
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=(2-1,1-(-2),5-3)=(1,3,2).
\]
This vector tells us how to move from \(A\) to \(B\): increase \(x\) by \(1\), increase \(y\) by \(3\), and increase \(z\) by \(2\).






TipPoint versus vector



The ordered triple \((1,3,2)\) can be interpreted as a point or as a vector. As a point, it is a location. As a vector, it is a displacement. In calculus and applied mathematics, the interpretation matters as much as the arithmetic.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.4 3.2 Vector addition and scalar multiplication
If
\[
u=(u_1,\ldots,u_n), \qquad v=(v_1,\ldots,v_n),
\]
then vector addition is defined componentwise:
\[
u+v=(u_1+v_1,\ldots,u_n+v_n).
\]
If \(c\) is a real number, scalar multiplication is
\[
cv=(cv_1,\ldots,cv_n).
\]
Geometrically:

\(u+v\) means follow displacement \(u\) and then displacement \(v\);
\(v-u\) means the displacement from the endpoint of \(u\) to the endpoint of \(v\);
\(cv\) stretches \(v\) by the factor \(|c|\);
if \(c<0\), then \(cv\) also reverses direction.


3.4.1 Example 3.2: Net displacement
A particle is displaced first by \((3,2)\) and then by \((2,-1)\). Its net displacement is
\[
(3,2)+(2,-1)=(5,1).
\]
The final location depends on the initial location, but the net displacement does not.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

u = np.array([3.0, 2.0])
v = np.array([2.0, -1.0])
w = u + v

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
origin = np.array([0.0, 0.0])

ax.quiver(*origin, *u, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.008)
ax.quiver(*u, *v, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.008)
ax.quiver(*origin, *w, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.006, alpha=0.7)

ax.text(u[0]/2, u[1]/2 + 0.15, r'$u$', fontsize=12)
ax.text(u[0] + v[0]/2, u[1] + v[1]/2 - 0.35, r'$v$', fontsize=12)
ax.text(w[0]/2, w[1]/2 - 0.45, r'$u+v$', fontsize=12)

ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 6)
ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 3)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
plt.show()







Figure 3.1: Vector addition by following one displacement after another.

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.5 3.3 Norm, length, and unit vectors
The Euclidean norm of a vector \(v=(v_1,\ldots,v_n)\) is
\[
\|v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2+\cdots+v_n^2}.
\]
This generalizes the Pythagorean theorem. In \(\mathbb R^2\), it gives the length of an arrow in the plane. In \(\mathbb R^3\), it gives the length of an arrow in space. In \(\mathbb R^n\), it gives the standard size of a data vector or parameter vector.
A vector \(u\) is a unit vector if
\[
\|u\|=1.
\]
If \(v\ne 0\), then the unit vector in the direction of \(v\) is
\[
\widehat v=\frac{v}{\|v\|}.
\]
The vector \(v\) can be decomposed as
\[
v=\|v\|\widehat v.
\]
This formula says that a nonzero vector is determined by two pieces of information: its magnitude and its direction.

3.5.1 Example 3.3: Unit vector parallel to \((3,-4)\)
Let
\[
v=(3,-4).
\]
Then
\[
\|v\|=\sqrt{3^2+(-4)^2}=5.
\]
Therefore a unit vector parallel to \(v\) and pointing in the same direction is
\[
\widehat v=\frac{1}{5}(3,-4)=\left(\frac35,-\frac45\right).
\]
A unit vector parallel to \(v\) but pointing in the opposite direction is
\[
-\widehat v=\left(-\frac35,\frac45\right).
\]


3.5.2 Example 3.4: Unit vectors perpendicular to \((3,-4)\)
In the plane, if \(v=(a,b)\), then \(( -b,a)\) and \((b,-a)\) are perpendicular to \(v\). For \(v=(3,-4)\), two perpendicular vectors are
\[
(4,3), \qquad (-4,-3).
\]
Both have length \(5\), so the corresponding unit vectors are
\[
\left(\frac45,\frac35\right), \qquad \left(-\frac45,-\frac35\right).
\]






WarningCommon mistake



Do not confuse a vector with its norm. The vector \((3,-4)\) has direction and magnitude. The norm \(\|(3,-4)\|=5\) is only a number.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.6 3.4 Distance as norm of displacement
The distance between two points is the length of the displacement vector connecting them. If
\[
A=(a_1,\ldots,a_n), \qquad B=(b_1,\ldots,b_n),
\]
then
\[
\operatorname{dist}(A,B)=\|B-A\|=\sqrt{(b_1-a_1)^2+\cdots+(b_n-a_n)^2}.
\]
This is one of the most important bridges between geometry and computation. In data science, distance is used in nearest-neighbor methods, clustering, anomaly detection, and similarity search.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

v = np.array([3.0, -4.0])
vhat = v / np.linalg.norm(v)
origin = np.array([0.0, 0.0])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(*origin, *v, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.008)
ax.quiver(*origin, *vhat, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.010, alpha=0.8)
ax.text(1.5, -2.2, r'$v=(3,-4)$', fontsize=12)
ax.text(0.25, -0.9, r'$\widehat v$', fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-1, 4)
ax.set_ylim(-5, 1)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
plt.show()







Figure 3.2: A vector and its unit direction have the same direction but different lengths.

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.7 3.5 Dot product
The dot product of two vectors
\[
u=(u_1,\ldots,u_n), \qquad v=(v_1,\ldots,v_n)
\]
is
\[
u\cdot v=u_1v_1+u_2v_2+\cdots+u_nv_n.
\]
The dot product takes two vectors and returns one number. It measures alignment.
The geometric identity is
\[
u\cdot v=\|u\|\,\|v\|\cos\theta,
\]
where \(\theta\) is the angle between \(u\) and \(v\).
Therefore, for nonzero vectors,
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|u\|\,\|v\|}.
\]
This formula is central in geometry, statistics, and machine learning.

3.7.1 Dot product interpretation
Suppose \(u\) and \(v\) are nonzero.

If \(u\cdot v>0\), the angle is acute and the vectors point generally in the same direction.
If \(u\cdot v=0\), the angle is \(90^\circ\) and the vectors are orthogonal.
If \(u\cdot v<0\), the angle is obtuse and the vectors point generally in opposite directions.



3.7.2 Example 3.5: Angle between two vectors
Let
\[
F=(1,2), \qquad v=(-1,3).
\]
Then
\[
F\cdot v=(1)(-1)+(2)(3)=5,
\]
and
\[
\|F\|=\sqrt{1^2+2^2}=\sqrt5, \qquad \|v\|=\sqrt{(-1)^2+3^2}=\sqrt{10}.
\]
Thus
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{F\cdot v}{\|F\|\|v\|}=\frac{5}{\sqrt5\sqrt{10}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}.
\]
So
\[
\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}.
\]






NoteInterpretation



The angle is \(45^\circ\), so the two vectors are strongly aligned. The dot product is positive because they point generally in the same direction.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.8 3.6 Orthogonality
Two vectors \(u\) and \(v\) are orthogonal if
\[
u\cdot v=0.
\]
Orthogonality generalizes perpendicularity. In \(\mathbb R^2\) and \(\mathbb R^3\), it means the vectors meet at a right angle. In \(\mathbb R^n\), where we may not be able to draw the vectors, the equation \(u\cdot v=0\) is still meaningful.

3.8.1 Example 3.6: Checking orthogonality
Let
\[
u=(2,-1,3), \qquad v=(1,5,1).
\]
Then
\[
u\cdot v=(2)(1)+(-1)(5)+(3)(1)=2-5+3=0.
\]
Therefore \(u\) and \(v\) are orthogonal.






TipWhy orthogonality matters



Orthogonality is one of the main organizing ideas of applied mathematics. It appears in least squares, projections, Fourier series, PCA, QR factorization, residual analysis, and the geometry of independent directions in data.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.9 3.7 Projection onto a direction
Projection answers a basic question:

How much of one vector points in the direction of another vector?

Let \(v\ne 0\). The vector projection of \(u\) onto \(v\) is
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v.
\]
The scalar component of \(u\) in the direction of \(v\) is
\[
\operatorname{comp}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|v\|}.
\]
If \(\widehat v=v/\|v\|\) is the unit vector in the direction of \(v\), then
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=(u\cdot \widehat v)\widehat v.
\]

3.9.1 Example 3.7: Projecting \((1,2)\) onto \((-1,3)\)
Let
\[
F=(1,2), \qquad v=(-1,3).
\]
Then
\[
F\cdot v=5, \qquad v\cdot v=(-1)^2+3^2=10.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(F)=\frac{F\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v
=\frac{5}{10}(-1,3)
=\left(-\frac12,\frac32\right).
\]
This is the part of \(F\) that lies in the direction of \(v\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

u = np.array([1.0, 2.0])
v = np.array([-1.0, 3.0])
proj = (u @ v) / (v @ v) * v
resid = u - proj
origin = np.array([0.0, 0.0])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(*origin, *u, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.008)
ax.quiver(*origin, *v, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.006, alpha=0.5)
ax.quiver(*origin, *proj, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.008)
ax.quiver(*proj, *resid, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.006, alpha=0.8)

ax.text(u[0]+0.1, u[1], r'$u$', fontsize=12)
ax.text(v[0]-0.5, v[1], r'$v$', fontsize=12)
ax.text(proj[0]-0.75, proj[1]-0.15, r'$\operatorname{proj}_v(u)$', fontsize=12)
ax.text(proj[0]+resid[0]/2+0.05, proj[1]+resid[1]/2, r'$u-\operatorname{proj}_v(u)$', fontsize=11)

ax.set_xlim(-2, 2)
ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 3.5)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
plt.show()







Figure 3.3: Projection decomposes a vector into a part parallel to v and a residual perpendicular to v.







3.9.2 Projection creates an orthogonal decomposition
Let
\[
p=\operatorname{proj}_v(u), \qquad r=u-p.
\]
Then
\[
u=p+r,
\]
where \(p\) is parallel to \(v\) and \(r\) is orthogonal to \(v\).
To check this, compute
\[
r\cdot v=(u-p)\cdot v
=u\cdot v-\left(\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v\right)\cdot v
=u\cdot v-\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}(v\cdot v)=0.
\]
This orthogonal decomposition is the geometric foundation of least squares.






ImportantBridge to least squares



In linear regression, the fitted values are projections of the response vector onto a column space. The residual vector is orthogonal to every column of the design matrix. This is why dot products and projections are essential for statistics.
