title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.10 3.8 Work as a dot product
If a constant force \(F\) moves an object through a displacement \(d\), then the work done by the force is
\[
W=F\cdot d.
\]
Only the component of the force in the direction of motion contributes to work.

3.10.1 Example 3.8: Work by a constant force
A constant force
\[
F=(-1,-3,4)
\]
is applied while an object moves from
\[
A=(1,2,0)
\]
to
\[
B=(2,0,1).
\]
The displacement is
\[
d=B-A=(1,-2,1).
\]
The work is
\[
W=F\cdot d=(-1)(1)+(-3)(-2)+(4)(1)=-1+6+4=9.
\]
The positive result means that the force has a net component in the direction of motion.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.11 3.9 Cosine similarity and data vectors
For nonzero vectors \(u\) and \(v\), the quantity
\[
\frac{u\cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}
\]
is the cosine of the angle between them. In data science and AI, this value is often called cosine similarity.
Cosine similarity focuses on direction rather than magnitude:

similar direction: value near \(1\);
orthogonal or unrelated direction: value near \(0\);
opposite direction: value near \(-1\).

This is useful when the magnitude of a vector is less important than its pattern. For example, in text analysis, two documents may have different lengths but similar word-frequency directions.

3.11.1 Example 3.9: Cosine similarity of data vectors
Suppose two observations are
\[
x=(2,1,0,3), \qquad y=(4,2,0,6).
\]
Then \(y=2x\), so the two observations point in exactly the same direction. Their cosine similarity is \(1\).
Now compare
\[
x=(2,1,0,3), \qquad z=(0,3,4,0).
\]
The dot product is
\[
x\cdot z=(2)(0)+(1)(3)+(0)(4)+(3)(0)=3,
\]
so the cosine similarity is positive but not close to \(1\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

vectors = np.array([
    [2, 1, 0, 3],
    [4, 2, 0, 6],
    [0, 3, 4, 0],
    [1, 0, 2, 1]
], dtype=float)

norms = np.linalg.norm(vectors, axis=1, keepdims=True)
unit_vectors = vectors / norms
cosine_matrix = unit_vectors @ unit_vectors.T

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
im = ax.imshow(cosine_matrix, vmin=-1, vmax=1)
ax.set_xticks(range(4))
ax.set_yticks(range(4))
ax.set_xticklabels(['x', 'y', 'z', 'w'])
ax.set_yticklabels(['x', 'y', 'z', 'w'])
for i in range(4):
    for j in range(4):
        ax.text(j, i, f'{cosine_matrix[i,j]:.2f}', ha='center', va='center')
ax.set_title('Cosine similarity matrix')
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
plt.show()







Figure 3.4: Cosine similarity measures direction rather than length.

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
section: 3.12 3.10 High-dimensional geometry: most random directions are nearly orthogonal
text:
3.12 3.10 High-dimensional geometry: most random directions are nearly orthogonal
High-dimensional geometry can be surprising. In low dimensions, random vectors often have visibly meaningful angles. In high dimensions, randomly chosen directions are usually close to orthogonal.
This fact helps explain why high-dimensional data can be difficult to visualize and why algorithms must be designed carefully.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2321)
num_pairs = 4000
dimensions = [2, 10, 100]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for d in dimensions:
    A = rng.normal(size=(num_pairs, d))
    B = rng.normal(size=(num_pairs, d))
    A = A / np.linalg.norm(A, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    B = B / np.linalg.norm(B, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    cosines = np.sum(A * B, axis=1)
    ax.hist(cosines, bins=40, alpha=0.45, density=True, label=f'd={d}')

ax.set_xlabel('cosine between random unit vectors')
ax.set_ylabel('density')
ax.set_title('Random directions in high dimension')
ax.legend()
plt.show()







Figure 3.5: Cosines between random unit vectors concentrate near zero in high dimensions.











NoteModern interpretation



In high-dimensional data spaces, distance and angle can behave differently from our three-dimensional intuition. This is one reason why normalization, projection, regularization, and dimensionality reduction are central in statistics and machine learning.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.13 3.11 Norms beyond the Euclidean norm
The Euclidean norm is the main norm used in this chapter, but other norms are important in applied mathematics and data science.
For \(v=(v_1,\ldots,v_n)\):
\[
\|v\|_1=|v_1|+\cdots+|v_n|
\]
is the \(\ell^1\) norm, and
\[
\|v\|_\infty=\max\{|v_1|,\ldots,|v_n|\}
\]
is the \(\ell^\infty\) norm.
The Euclidean norm is
\[
\|v\|_2=\sqrt{v_1^2+\cdots+v_n^2}.
\]
Different norms encode different ideas of size.








Norm
Formula
Common interpretation




\(\|v\|_1\)
sum of absolute values
sparsity, total variation, Manhattan distance


\(\|v\|_2\)
square root of sum of squares
Euclidean length, energy, least squares


\(\|v\|_\infty\)
maximum absolute component
worst-case component size




import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-1.2, 1.2, 400)
y = np.linspace(-1.2, 1.2, 400)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
L1 = np.abs(X) + np.abs(Y)
L2 = np.sqrt(X**2 + Y**2)
Linf = np.maximum(np.abs(X), np.abs(Y))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.contour(X, Y, L1, levels=[1], linewidths=2)
ax.contour(X, Y, L2, levels=[1], linewidths=2)
ax.contour(X, Y, Linf, levels=[1], linewidths=2)
ax.text(0.05, 1.05, r'$\|v\|_1=1$', fontsize=11)
ax.text(0.75, 0.70, r'$\|v\|_2=1$', fontsize=11)
ax.text(1.03, -0.95, r'$\|v\|_\infty=1$', fontsize=11)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_title('Unit balls for three norms')
plt.show()







Figure 3.6: Different norms produce different unit balls in the plane.











ImportantWhy this matters later



Least-squares regression is built from the \(\ell^2\) norm. Lasso regression is built from the \(\ell^1\) norm. Robust and worst-case methods often use \(\ell^\infty\)-type constraints. Norms are not just geometric objects; they shape models.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
section: 3.14 3.12 Python implementation of core vector operations
text:
3.14 3.12 Python implementation of core vector operations
The formulas in this chapter are simple enough to compute by hand, but they also form the basic operations used by numerical linear algebra and machine learning libraries.

import numpy as np

u = np.array([1.0, 2.0])
v = np.array([-1.0, 3.0])

norm_u = np.linalg.norm(u)
norm_v = np.linalg.norm(v)
dot_uv = u @ v
cos_theta = dot_uv / (norm_u * norm_v)
projection = (dot_uv / (v @ v)) * v
residual = u - projection

print('||u|| =', norm_u)
print('||v|| =', norm_v)
print('u dot v =', dot_uv)
print('cos(theta) =', cos_theta)
print('projection of u onto v =', projection)
print('residual =', residual)
print('residual dot v =', residual @ v)

||u|| = 2.23606797749979
||v|| = 3.1622776601683795
u dot v = 5.0
cos(theta) = 0.7071067811865475
projection of u onto v = [-0.5  1.5]
residual = [1.5 0.5]
residual dot v = 0.0


The last line should be approximately zero. This confirms that the residual \(u-\operatorname{proj}_v(u)\) is orthogonal to \(v\).
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.15 3.13 Worked example summary

3.15.1 Example 3.10: Full vector analysis
Let
\[
u=(2,-1,2), \qquad v=(1,2,2).
\]
Step 1: Compute the norms.
\[
\|u\|=\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+2^2}=3,
\]
and
\[
\|v\|=\sqrt{1^2+2^2+2^2}=3.
\]
Step 2: Compute the dot product.
\[
u\cdot v=(2)(1)+(-1)(2)+(2)(2)=2-2+4=4.
\]
Step 3: Find the angle.
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}=\frac{4}{9}.
\]
So
\[
\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\frac49\right).
\]
Step 4: Project \(u\) onto \(v\).
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v
=\frac49(1,2,2)
=\left(\frac49,\frac89,\frac89\right).
\]
Step 5: Interpret.
The vector \(u\) has a component in the direction of \(v\) and a residual component perpendicular to \(v\). This is the same geometric idea that later appears in least-squares approximation.

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
section: 3.16 3.14 Mini-project: cosine similarity for short documents
text:
3.16 3.14 Mini-project: cosine similarity for short documents
A simple way to represent a document is by a vector of word counts. Suppose our vocabulary is
\[
(\text{calculus},\text{data},\text{matrix},\text{gradient}).
\]
Three short documents might have count vectors
\[
d_1=(3,1,0,2), \qquad d_2=(2,1,0,3), \qquad d_3=(0,3,4,0).
\]
Cosine similarity compares their directions.

import numpy as np

docs = np.array([
    [3, 1, 0, 2],
    [2, 1, 0, 3],
    [0, 3, 4, 0]
], dtype=float)

names = ['doc1', 'doc2', 'doc3']
unit_docs = docs / np.linalg.norm(docs, axis=1, keepdims=True)
sim = unit_docs @ unit_docs.T

for i, name_i in enumerate(names):
    for j, name_j in enumerate(names):
        print(f'{name_i} vs {name_j}: {sim[i, j]:.3f}')

doc1 vs doc1: 1.000
doc1 vs doc2: 0.929
doc1 vs doc3: 0.160
doc2 vs doc1: 0.929
doc2 vs doc2: 1.000
doc2 vs doc3: 0.160
doc3 vs doc1: 0.160
doc3 vs doc2: 0.160
doc3 vs doc3: 1.000


Documents \(d_1\) and \(d_2\) are more similar to each other than either is to \(d_3\). This is a small example of the geometry behind search, clustering, and embedding models.

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.17 Practice problems

3.17.1 Conceptual questions

Explain the difference between a point and a vector using an example in \(\mathbb R^2\).
Why does multiplying a vector by a negative scalar reverse its direction?
What does it mean for two vectors in \(\mathbb R^n\) to be orthogonal?
Why is the dot product a measure of alignment?
Explain why cosine similarity ignores magnitude.
Why is projection important for least-squares approximation?



3.17.2 Computational practice

Let \(v=(6,8)\). Find \(\|v\|\) and a unit vector in the direction of \(v\).
Let \(u=(1,2,3)\) and \(v=(4,0,-1)\). Compute \(u+v\), \(u-v\), \(3u\), and \(-2v\).
Find the displacement vector from \(A=(2,-1,4)\) to \(B=(5,3,-2)\).
Compute the distance between \(A=(2,-1,4)\) and \(B=(5,3,-2)\).
Compute the dot product of \(u=(2,-1,3)\) and \(v=(1,5,1)\). Are they orthogonal?
Find the angle between \(u=(1,1)\) and \(v=(1,-1)\).
Find the projection of \(u=(3,4)\) onto \(v=(1,0)\).
Find the projection of \(u=(1,2)\) onto \(v=(-1,3)\).
A force \(F=(2,-1,4)\) moves an object from \(A=(0,1,2)\) to \(B=(3,0,5)\). Compute the work done.
Let \(x=(1,0,2,2)\) and \(y=(2,0,4,4)\). Compute their cosine similarity.



3.17.3 Applied and data-oriented problems

Suppose two students have feature vectors \(x=(80,90,70)\) and \(y=(40,45,35)\) representing scores on three assessments. Compute their cosine similarity. Interpret why this similarity may be high even though the magnitudes differ.
Suppose \(r=y-\widehat y\) is a residual vector in a regression problem. What does it mean geometrically if \(r\) is orthogonal to the fitted-value vector \(\widehat y\)?
In a nearest-neighbor method, why might scaling the features change the computed distances?
Find a vector perpendicular to \((5,-2)\). Then normalize it.
Let \(w=(w_1,w_2,\ldots,w_n)\) be a model parameter vector. Explain why \(\|w\|_2\) can be interpreted as a measure of model size.



3.17.4 Challenge problems

Prove that if \(v\ne 0\), then \(u-\operatorname{proj}_v(u)\) is orthogonal to \(v\).
Prove the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality in the special case of \(\mathbb R^2\):
\[
|u\cdot v|\le \|u\|\|v\|.
\]
Show that the projection formula minimizes the distance from \(u\) to the line spanned by \(v\):
\[
\min_{c\in\mathbb R}\|u-cv\|.
\]
Let \(u\) and \(v\) be nonzero vectors. Show that their cosine similarity is unchanged if \(u\) and \(v\) are multiplied by positive constants.
In \(\mathbb R^n\), show that if \(u\) is orthogonal to \(v_1,\ldots,v_k\), then \(u\) is orthogonal to every linear combination \(c_1v_1+\cdots+c_kv_k\).

title: 3  Chapter 3. Vectors, Norms, Dot Products, and Projections
text:
3.18 Chapter summary
This chapter developed the core vector operations needed for multivariable calculus and its applications.
The central formulas are:
\[
u+v=(u_1+v_1,\ldots,u_n+v_n),
\]
\[
\|v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+\cdots+v_n^2},
\]
\[
\widehat v=\frac{v}{\|v\|},
\]
\[
u\cdot v=u_1v_1+\cdots+u_nv_n,
\]
\[
u\cdot v=\|u\|\|v\|\cos\theta,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v.
\]
The most important conceptual message is that dot products and projections translate geometry into computation. They measure alignment, define orthogonality, compute work, compare data vectors, and prepare the ground for tangent planes, gradients, least squares, optimization, and machine learning.

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5 Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
In single-variable calculus, the most important local geometric object is the tangent line. In multivariable calculus, the corresponding objects are lines, planes, and more generally hyperplanes. These are not only geometric objects in \(\mathbb R^2\) and \(\mathbb R^3\); they are also the basic language of linear models, constraints, optimization, regression, classification, tangent spaces, and numerical algorithms.
A line can describe a path of motion. A plane can describe a constraint surface or a local approximation. A hyperplane can separate two classes of data. A line segment can describe interpolation between data points. A parametric surface can describe a plane patch, a sphere, a cylinder, or the local tangent plane to a curved surface.
This chapter builds a careful foundation for these ideas. The main theme is simple:
We will move between three complementary descriptions:
By the end of the chapter, lines and planes should feel less like formulas to memorize and more like flexible geometric objects that can be used in calculus, optimization, and data science.

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.2 4.1 Why parametric geometry matters
A line in the plane is often introduced as
\[
y = mx + b.
\]
This is useful, but it has two limitations. First, it does not describe vertical lines. Second, it does not generalize naturally to curves, lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), planes in \(\mathbb R^3\), or hyperplanes in \(\mathbb R^n\).
The vector point of view is more general. A line is determined by:

one point on the line;
one nonzero direction vector.

A plane is determined by either:

one point and one normal vector; or
one point and two nonparallel direction vectors.

A hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\) is determined by:

one point and one nonzero normal vector.

This is the same geometry that appears in calculus when we construct tangent lines, tangent planes, and local linear approximations.






TipConceptual principle



A point tells us where an object is. A vector tells us how to move along the object or which direction is perpendicular to it.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.3 4.2 Lines in the plane

5.3.1 Slope form
A nonvertical line in \(\mathbb R^2\) through \((x_0,y_0)\) with slope \(m\) has equation
\[
y-y_0=m(x-x_0).
\]
This form is familiar from algebra and single-variable calculus. But it hides the vector structure of the line. The slope \(m\) means that a direction vector for the line is
\[
v = \langle 1,m\rangle.
\]
Thus the same line can be written as
\[
\langle x,y\rangle = \langle x_0,y_0\rangle + t\langle 1,m\rangle.
\]
Here \(t\) is a parameter. As \(t\) changes, the point moves along the line.


5.3.2 Vector form
More generally, if \(P=(x_0,y_0)\) is a point and \(v=\langle a,b\rangle\) is a nonzero direction vector, then the line through \(P\) parallel to \(v\) is
\[
r(t)=r_0+tv,
\]
or
\[
\langle x,y\rangle = \langle x_0,y_0\rangle + t\langle a,b\rangle.
\]
In coordinates,
\[
x=x_0+at, \qquad y=y_0+bt.
\]
The same idea works in \(\mathbb R^3\) and \(\mathbb R^n\).


5.3.3 Point-normal form in the plane
A line in \(\mathbb R^2\) can also be described by a normal vector. If \(n=\langle A,B\rangle\) is perpendicular to a line and \(P_0=(x_0,y_0)\) is on the line, then any point \(P=(x,y)\) on the line satisfies
\[
n\cdot(P-P_0)=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
A(x-x_0)+B(y-y_0)=0.
\]
Expanding gives the standard form
\[
Ax+By+C=0.
\]






NoteDirection vector versus normal vector



For a line in \(\mathbb R^2\):

a direction vector points along the line;
a normal vector points perpendicular to the line.

If \(v=\langle a,b\rangle\) is a direction vector, then \(n=\langle -b,a\rangle\) and \(n=\langle b,-a\rangle\) are normal vectors.




5.3.4 Example 4.1: Same line, three descriptions
Find vector, parametric, and standard equations for the line through \(P=(2,-1)\) with direction vector \(v=\langle 3,2\rangle\).
Solution. The vector equation is
\[
r(t)=\langle 2,-1\rangle+t\langle 3,2\rangle.
\]
The parametric equations are
\[
x=2+3t, \qquad y=-1+2t.
\]
A normal vector is \(n=\langle -2,3\rangle\). Therefore
\[
-2(x-2)+3(y+1)=0.
\]
Expanding,
\[
-2x+3y+7=0.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

P = np.array([2.0, -1.0])
v = np.array([3.0, 2.0])
t = np.linspace(-1.5, 1.5, 200)
pts = P[:, None] + v[:, None] * t

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(pts[0], pts[1], label='line')
ax.scatter([P[0]], [P[1]], s=60, label='P')
ax.quiver(P[0], P[1], v[0], v[1], angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=1, width=0.006)
ax.text(P[0] + 0.1, P[1] - 0.35, 'P')
ax.text(P[0] + v[0] * 0.45, P[1] + v[1] * 0.45, 'v')
ax.axhline(0, linewidth=0.8)
ax.axvline(0, linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlim(-3, 7)
ax.set_ylim(-5, 4)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.legend()
plt.show()







Figure 5.1: A line in the plane described by a point and a direction vector

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.4 4.3 Lines in \(\mathbb R^n\)
The vector equation of a line is dimension independent. In \(\mathbb R^n\), a line through a point
\[
p=(p_1,p_2,\dots,p_n)
\]
with nonzero direction vector
\[
v=(v_1,v_2,\dots,v_n)
\]
is
\[
r(t)=p+tv, \qquad -\infty<t<\infty.
\]
In coordinates,
\[
x_1=p_1+tv_1,\quad x_2=p_2+tv_2,\quad \dots,\quad x_n=p_n+tv_n.
\]
The parameter \(t\) is not always time, but it often behaves like time: as \(t\) increases, the point moves along the line at constant velocity \(v\).

5.4.1 Lines in \(\mathbb R^3\)
If \(P=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) and \(v=\langle a,b,c\rangle\), then
\[
\langle x,y,z\rangle = \langle x_0,y_0,z_0\rangle + t\langle a,b,c\rangle.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
x=x_0+at,\qquad y=y_0+bt,\qquad z=z_0+ct.
\]
If \(a,b,c\) are nonzero, the symmetric form is
\[
\frac{x-x_0}{a}=\frac{y-y_0}{b}=\frac{z-z_0}{c}.
\]
If one component of the direction vector is zero, the corresponding coordinate is constant and should not be placed in the denominator.


5.4.2 Example 4.2: A line through two points
Find a vector equation and symmetric equation for the line through
\[
P=(-5,5,4), \qquad Q=(7,-1,2).
\]
Solution. A direction vector is
\[
v=Q-P=\langle 12,-6,-2\rangle.
\]
Thus
\[
r(t)=\langle -5,5,4\rangle+t\langle 12,-6,-2\rangle.
\]
The parametric equations are
\[
x=-5+12t,\qquad y=5-6t,\qquad z=4-2t.
\]
The symmetric form is
\[
\frac{x+5}{12}=\frac{y-5}{-6}=\frac{z-4}{-2}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

P = np.array([-5.0, 5.0, 4.0])
Q = np.array([7.0, -1.0, 2.0])
v = Q - P

t = np.linspace(-0.15, 1.15, 100)
L = P[:, None] + v[:, None] * t

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot(L[0], L[1], L[2])
ax.scatter([P[0], Q[0]], [P[1], Q[1]], [P[2], Q[2]], s=60)
ax.text(P[0], P[1], P[2], ' P')
ax.text(Q[0], Q[1], Q[2], ' Q')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Line through P and Q')
plt.show()







Figure 5.2: A line in space passing through two specified points

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.5 4.4 Line segments and interpolation
The full line through \(P\) and \(Q\) extends infinitely in both directions. The line segment from \(P\) to \(Q\) is obtained by restricting the parameter:
\[
r(t)=P+t(Q-P),\qquad 0\le t\le 1.
\]
At \(t=0\), we get \(P\). At \(t=1\), we get \(Q\). At \(t=1/2\), we get the midpoint.
This formula is one of the most useful formulas in applied mathematics. It describes linear interpolation between two states, two observations, two images, two parameter vectors, or two model weights.






TipLine segment formula



The line segment from \(P\) to \(Q\) is
\[
r(t)=(1-t)P+tQ,\qquad 0\le t\le 1.
\]
This is the same as \(P+t(Q-P)\), but the form \((1-t)P+tQ\) makes the interpolation interpretation clear.



5.5.1 Example 4.3: Interpolating between two model parameter vectors
Suppose a simple model has parameter vector
\[
w_0=(1,-2,0.5)
\]
before training and
\[
w_1=(3,1,-1)
\]
after training. The path
\[
w(t)=(1-t)w_0+tw_1,\qquad 0\le t\le 1,
\]
linearly interpolates between the two models. This does not mean gradient descent moves along this exact path. It only gives a useful way to compare intermediate parameter vectors.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

w0 = np.array([1.0, -2.0, 0.5])
w1 = np.array([3.0, 1.0, -1.0])
t = np.linspace(0, 1, 30)
W = (1 - t)[:, None] * w0 + t[:, None] * w1

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot(W[:, 0], W[:, 1], W[:, 2], marker='o', markersize=3)
ax.scatter([w0[0], w1[0]], [w0[1], w1[1]], [w0[2], w1[2]], s=80)
ax.text(w0[0], w0[1], w0[2], ' start')
ax.text(w1[0], w1[1], w1[2], ' end')
ax.set_xlabel('w1')
ax.set_ylabel('w2')
ax.set_zlabel('w3')
ax.set_title('A straight path in parameter space')
plt.show()







Figure 5.3: Linear interpolation between two vectors in three-dimensional parameter space
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.6 4.5 Planes in \(\mathbb R^3\): point-normal form
A plane in \(\mathbb R^3\) is determined by a point \(P_0=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) and a nonzero normal vector
\[
n=\langle a,b,c\rangle.
\]
A point \(P=(x,y,z)\) lies on the plane exactly when \(P-P_0\) is perpendicular to \(n\). Therefore
\[
n\cdot(P-P_0)=0.
\]
This gives
\[
\langle a,b,c\rangle\cdot\langle x-x_0,y-y_0,z-z_0\rangle=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
a(x-x_0)+b(y-y_0)+c(z-z_0)=0.
\]
Expanding gives standard form
\[
ax+by+cz+d=0.
\]






NoteMeaning of the normal vector



In the equation
\[
ax+by+cz+d=0,
\]
the vector \(n=\langle a,b,c\rangle\) is perpendicular to the plane. This is why the coefficients of \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) are geometrically meaningful.



5.6.1 Example 4.4: Equation of a plane from point and normal vector
Find the equation of the plane through \(P_0=(1,2,3)\) with normal vector \(n=\langle 4,5,6\rangle\).
Solution. Use the point-normal equation:
\[
4(x-1)+5(y-2)+6(z-3)=0.
\]
Expanding,
\[
4x+5y+6z-32=0.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

P0 = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
n = np.array([4.0, 5.0, 6.0])

# Plane: 4(x-1)+5(y-2)+6(z-3)=0
x = np.linspace(-1.5, 3.5, 20)
y = np.linspace(-0.5, 4.5, 20)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = P0[2] - (n[0] * (X - P0[0]) + n[1] * (Y - P0[1])) / n[2]

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.45, linewidth=0)
ax.scatter([P0[0]], [P0[1]], [P0[2]], s=70)
ax.quiver(P0[0], P0[1], P0[2], n[0], n[1], n[2], length=0.45, normalize=True)
ax.text(P0[0], P0[1], P0[2], ' P0')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Plane and normal vector')
plt.show()







Figure 5.4: A plane in space with a normal vector drawn from a point on the plane
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.7 4.6 Planes from three points
Three noncollinear points determine a plane. Suppose
\[
P,\quad Q,\quad R
\]
are three points in \(\mathbb R^3\). Two direction vectors in the plane are
\[
Q-P,\qquad R-P.
\]
If the points are not collinear, then these vectors are not parallel. A normal vector is
\[
n=(Q-P)\times(R-P).
\]
Then the plane is
\[
n\cdot\bigl(\langle x,y,z\rangle-P\bigr)=0.
\]

5.7.1 Example 4.5: Plane through three points
Find the plane through
\[
P=(1,2,3),\qquad Q=(2,2,5),\qquad R=(3,4,1).
\]
Solution. Compute
\[
Q-P=\langle 1,0,2\rangle,
\]
and
\[
R-P=\langle 2,2,-2\rangle.
\]
A normal vector is
\[
(Q-P)\times(R-P)=\langle -4,6,2\rangle.
\]
We may divide by \(2\) and use
\[
n=\langle -2,3,1\rangle.
\]
Therefore the plane is
\[
-2(x-1)+3(y-2)+(z-3)=0.
\]
Expanding,
\[
-2x+3y+z-7=0.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.8 4.7 Parametric form of a plane
A plane may also be described by one point and two nonparallel direction vectors. If \(P_0\) is a point and \(u,v\) are nonparallel vectors, then
\[
r(s,t)=P_0+su+tv
\]
is the plane through \(P_0\) spanned by \(u\) and \(v\).
This is the two-parameter analogue of a line.

A line has one parameter: \(r(t)=P_0+tv\).
A plane has two parameters: \(r(s,t)=P_0+su+tv\).

The normal vector to the plane is
\[
n=u\times v.
\]
If \(u\times v=0\), then \(u\) and \(v\) are parallel, and the formula does not describe a genuine plane.

5.8.1 Example 4.6: Parametric and scalar plane equations
Let
\[
P_0=(1,0,2),\qquad u=\langle 1,2,0\rangle,\qquad v=\langle 0,1,3\rangle.
\]
A parametric equation of the plane is
\[
r(s,t)=\langle 1,0,2\rangle+s\langle 1,2,0\rangle+t\langle 0,1,3\rangle.
\]
In coordinates,
\[
x=1+s,\qquad y=2s+t,\qquad z=2+3t.
\]
A normal vector is
\[
u\times v=\langle 6,-3,1\rangle.
\]
Therefore a scalar equation is
\[
6(x-1)-3(y-0)+(z-2)=0,
\]
or
\[
6x-3y+z-8=0.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

P0 = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 2.0])
u = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 0.0])
v = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 3.0])

s = np.linspace(-1, 1, 15)
t = np.linspace(-1, 1, 15)
S, T = np.meshgrid(s, t)
X = P0[0] + S * u[0] + T * v[0]
Y = P0[1] + S * u[1] + T * v[1]
Z = P0[2] + S * u[2] + T * v[2]

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.45, linewidth=0)
ax.scatter([P0[0]], [P0[1]], [P0[2]], s=70)
ax.quiver(P0[0], P0[1], P0[2], u[0], u[1], u[2], length=1, normalize=False)
ax.quiver(P0[0], P0[1], P0[2], v[0], v[1], v[2], length=1, normalize=False)
ax.text(P0[0], P0[1], P0[2], ' P0')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Parametric plane')
plt.show()







Figure 5.5: A parametric plane generated by two nonparallel direction vectors

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.9 4.8 Intersections
Intersections are where algebra meets geometry. In calculus and applied mathematics, intersection problems appear when we solve constraints, find feasible sets, or describe where surfaces meet.

5.9.1 Line-plane intersection
Suppose a line is given by
\[
r(t)=P+tv
\]
and a plane is given by
\[
n\cdot(x-P_0)=0.
\]
Substitute \(x=r(t)\) into the plane equation:
\[
n\cdot(P+tv-P_0)=0.
\]
This is a linear equation in \(t\):
\[
n\cdot(P-P_0)+t(n\cdot v)=0.
\]
If \(n\cdot v\ne 0\), there is one intersection point:
\[
t=-\frac{n\cdot(P-P_0)}{n\cdot v}.
\]
If \(n\cdot v=0\), then the line is parallel to the plane. It either never intersects the plane or lies entirely in the plane.


5.9.2 Example 4.7: A line intersects a plane
Find the intersection point of
\[
x=1-2t,\qquad y=3+t,\qquad z=2+4t
\]
with the plane
\[
2x-y+z=0.
\]
Solution. Substitute the line into the plane:
\[
2(1-2t)-(3+t)+(2+4t)=0.
\]
Simplifying gives
\[
1-t=0,
\]
so \(t=1\). Therefore
\[
(x,y,z)=(-1,4,6).
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Line: (1,3,2) + t(-2,1,4)
P = np.array([1.0, 3.0, 2.0])
v = np.array([-2.0, 1.0, 4.0])

t = np.linspace(-0.5, 1.4, 100)
L = P[:, None] + v[:, None] * t
intersection = P + v * 1.0

# Plane: 2x - y + z = 0, so z = -2x + y
x = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 20)
y = np.linspace(1.0, 5.0, 20)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = -2 * X + Y

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.35, linewidth=0)
ax.plot(L[0], L[1], L[2], linewidth=2)
ax.scatter([intersection[0]], [intersection[1]], [intersection[2]], s=80)
ax.text(intersection[0], intersection[1], intersection[2], ' intersection')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Line-plane intersection')
plt.show()







Figure 5.6: A line meeting a plane at one point







5.9.3 Intersection of two planes
Two nonparallel planes in \(\mathbb R^3\) intersect in a line. Suppose
\[
n_1\cdot x=d_1,
\]
and
\[
n_2\cdot x=d_2.
\]
If \(n_1\) and \(n_2\) are not parallel, then the direction vector of the intersection line is
\[
v=n_1\times n_2.
\]
To find a point on the line, solve the two plane equations simultaneously. Since there are two equations in three unknowns, one variable can usually be chosen freely.


5.9.4 Example 4.8: Intersection of two planes
Find a parametric equation for the intersection of
\[
x+y+z=3
\]
and
\[
2x+3y+z=8.
\]
Solution. Subtract the first equation from the second:
\[
x+2y=5.
\]
Let \(z=t\). Then from the first equation,
\[
x+y=3-t.
\]
Solving
\[
x+2y=5,\qquad x+y=3-t
\]
gives
\[
y=2+t,
\]
and
\[
x=1-2t.
\]
So the intersection line is
\[
r(t)=\langle 1,2,0\rangle+t\langle -2,1,1\rangle.
\]
The direction vector can also be found from the cross product of normal vectors:
\[
\langle 1,1,1\rangle\times\langle 2,3,1\rangle=\langle -2,1,1\rangle.
\]

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.10 4.9 Angles involving lines and planes

5.10.1 Angle between two lines
If two lines have direction vectors \(u\) and \(v\), the angle \(\theta\) between the lines satisfies
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{|u\cdot v|}{|u||v|}.
\]
The absolute value gives the acute angle between the lines.


5.10.2 Angle between two planes
If two planes have normal vectors \(n_1\) and \(n_2\), then the angle between the planes is the acute angle between their normal vectors:
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{|n_1\cdot n_2|}{|n_1||n_2|}.
\]


5.10.3 Example 4.9: Angle between two planes
Find the angle between
\[
x+2y+z=5
\]
and
\[
x+y=8.
\]
Solution. The normal vectors are
\[
n_1=\langle 1,2,1\rangle,
\qquad
n_2=\langle 1,1,0\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{|n_1\cdot n_2|}{|n_1||n_2|}
=\frac{3}{\sqrt{6}\sqrt{2}}
=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}.
\]

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
section: 5.11 4.10 Distances to lines, planes, and hyperplanes
text:
5.11 4.10 Distances to lines, planes, and hyperplanes
Distance formulas are important because they turn geometry into quantitative measurement. In data science, the distance from a data point to a hyperplane is a signed confidence score for a linear classifier. In optimization, it measures violation of a linear constraint.

5.11.1 Distance from a point to a plane
Suppose the plane is
\[
ax+by+cz+d=0.
\]
The distance from \(P=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) to the plane is
\[
\operatorname{dist}(P,\Pi)=\frac{|ax_0+by_0+cz_0+d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}.
\]
The signed distance is
\[
\frac{ax_0+by_0+cz_0+d}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}.
\]


5.11.2 Distance from a point to a line in \(\mathbb R^3\)
If a line is \(r(t)=P_0+tv\) and \(P\) is a point, then
\[
\operatorname{dist}(P,L)=\frac{|(P-P_0)\times v|}{|v|}.
\]
This formula comes from the area of a parallelogram. The numerator is the area of the parallelogram with sides \(P-P_0\) and \(v\), and the base length is \(|v|\).


5.11.3 Example 4.10: Distance from a point to a plane
Find the distance from \(P=(1,2,3)\) to
\[
2x-y+2z-5=0.
\]
Solution. Use the formula:
\[
\operatorname{dist}(P,\Pi)
=\frac{|2(1)-2+2(3)-5|}{\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+2^2}}
=\frac{1}{3}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(10)
X1 = rng.normal(loc=[-1.2, -0.6], scale=0.45, size=(35, 2))
X2 = rng.normal(loc=[1.2, 0.8], scale=0.45, size=(35, 2))
X = np.vstack([X1, X2])
y_label = np.array([0] * len(X1) + [1] * len(X2))

# Decision boundary: w dot x + b = 0
w = np.array([1.0, -0.75])
b = -0.05
xx = np.linspace(-2.8, 2.8, 200)
yy = -(w[0] * xx + b) / w[1]

signed_dist = (X @ w + b) / np.linalg.norm(w)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(X[y_label == 0, 0], X[y_label == 0, 1], label='class 0')
ax.scatter(X[y_label == 1, 0], X[y_label == 1, 1], label='class 1')
ax.plot(xx, yy, label='w dot x + b = 0')

# Draw distance segments for a few points
for p in X[::12]:
    foot = p - ((p @ w + b) / (w @ w)) * w
    ax.plot([p[0], foot[0]], [p[1], foot[1]], linewidth=1)

ax.axhline(0, linewidth=0.8)
ax.axvline(0, linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlim(-3, 3)
ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)
ax.set_xlabel('feature 1')
ax.set_ylabel('feature 2')
ax.set_title('Distances to a decision boundary')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
ax.legend()
plt.show()

print('First five signed distances:', np.round(signed_dist[:5], 3))







Figure 5.7: Signed distances to a line used as a linear decision boundary





First five signed distances: [-0.841 -0.994 -0.764 -0.568 -0.347]

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.12 4.11 Hyperplanes in \(\mathbb R^n\)
A hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\) is the set of points satisfying one linear equation:
\[
w\cdot x+b=0,
\]
where \(w\ne 0\) is a vector in \(\mathbb R^n\) and \(b\) is a scalar.
The vector \(w\) is normal to the hyperplane. The hyperplane has dimension \(n-1\). For example:

in \(\mathbb R^2\), a hyperplane is a line;
in \(\mathbb R^3\), a hyperplane is a plane;
in \(\mathbb R^{100}\), a hyperplane is a \(99\)-dimensional flat object.

We usually cannot draw high-dimensional hyperplanes, but the algebra remains the same.
The two sides of the hyperplane are half-spaces:
\[
w\cdot x+b>0
\]
and
\[
w\cdot x+b<0.
\]
The signed distance from \(x\) to the hyperplane is
\[
\frac{w\cdot x+b}{|w|}.
\]

5.12.1 Hyperplanes and linear classifiers
A linear classifier assigns a label according to the sign of
\[
w\cdot x+b.
\]
This is the geometric core of logistic regression, support vector machines, perceptrons, and the final linear layer of many neural networks.






ImportantHyperplane as a decision boundary



A classifier of the form
\[
\hat y=\operatorname{sign}(w\cdot x+b)
\]
separates feature space into two half-spaces. The boundary between the two predicted classes is the hyperplane \(w\cdot x+b=0\).



import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(24)
A = rng.normal(loc=[-1.0, -0.5, -0.4], scale=0.35, size=(35, 3))
B = rng.normal(loc=[1.0, 0.7, 0.5], scale=0.35, size=(35, 3))

# Hyperplane: x + y + z = 0.2
w = np.array([1.0, 1.0, 1.0])
b = -0.2
x = np.linspace(-1.8, 1.8, 15)
y = np.linspace(-1.8, 1.8, 15)
Xg, Yg = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Zg = -(w[0] * Xg + w[1] * Yg + b) / w[2]

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.scatter(A[:, 0], A[:, 1], A[:, 2], label='class 0')
ax.scatter(B[:, 0], B[:, 1], B[:, 2], label='class 1')
ax.plot_surface(Xg, Yg, Zg, alpha=0.25, linewidth=0)
ax.set_xlabel('feature 1')
ax.set_ylabel('feature 2')
ax.set_zlabel('feature 3')
ax.set_title('Linear decision boundary in 3D')
ax.legend()
plt.show()







Figure 5.8: A plane used as a linear decision boundary in three-dimensional feature space

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.13 4.12 Affine subspaces and constraints
A line, plane, or hyperplane is an example of an affine subspace. The word affine means that the object may not pass through the origin.
A linear subspace has the form
\[
\operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}.
\]
An affine subspace has the form
\[
p+\operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}.
\]
Examples:

A line through the origin is a one-dimensional linear subspace.
A line not through the origin is a one-dimensional affine subspace.
A plane through the origin is a two-dimensional linear subspace.
A plane not through the origin is a two-dimensional affine subspace.

In optimization, linear equality constraints form affine subspaces. For example,
\[
Ax=b
\]
describes an affine set when it is consistent. The geometry of constraints is the foundation for Lagrange multipliers, constrained least squares, and convex optimization.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.14 4.13 Computational templates
The following functions are useful for computational geometry.

import numpy as np

def line_point(P, v, t):
    """Return P + t v."""
    return np.asarray(P, dtype=float) + t * np.asarray(v, dtype=float)


def plane_signed_distance(P, normal, d):
    """Signed distance from P to the plane normal dot x + d = 0."""
    P = np.asarray(P, dtype=float)
    normal = np.asarray(normal, dtype=float)
    return (normal @ P + d) / np.linalg.norm(normal)


def project_point_to_plane(P, normal, d):
    """Orthogonal projection of P onto normal dot x + d = 0."""
    P = np.asarray(P, dtype=float)
    normal = np.asarray(normal, dtype=float)
    return P - ((normal @ P + d) / (normal @ normal)) * normal


def line_plane_intersection(P, v, normal, d):
    """Intersection of P + t v and normal dot x + d = 0, if unique."""
    P = np.asarray(P, dtype=float)
    v = np.asarray(v, dtype=float)
    normal = np.asarray(normal, dtype=float)
    denom = normal @ v
    if abs(denom) < 1e-12:
        return None
    t = -(normal @ P + d) / denom
    return P + t * v

# Test examples
print('Point on a line:', line_point([1, 2, 3], [5, 1, -2], 0.5))
print('Signed distance:', plane_signed_distance([1, 2, 3], [2, -1, 2], -5))
print('Projection:', np.round(project_point_to_plane([1, 2, 3], [2, -1, 2], -5), 3))
print('Line-plane intersection:', line_plane_intersection([1, 3, 2], [-2, 1, 4], [2, -1, 1], 0))

Point on a line: [3.5 2.5 2. ]
Signed distance: 0.3333333333333333
Projection: [0.778 2.111 2.778]
Line-plane intersection: [-1.  4.  6.]


The same templates appear in many numerical algorithms. For example, projection onto a hyperplane is used in constrained optimization and feasibility methods. Signed distance to a hyperplane is used in classification. Line-plane intersection is used in graphics, computational geometry, and ray tracing.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.15 4.14 Problem-solving strategies

5.15.1 Strategy 1: Identify the data type
Ask: are you given points, direction vectors, normal vectors, or equations?

Two points usually give a direction vector by subtraction.
A plane equation gives a normal vector from its coefficients.
A parametric line gives a direction vector from the coefficients of \(t\).
A parametric plane gives two direction vectors from the coefficients of the two parameters.



5.15.2 Strategy 2: Choose the form that matches the task

To trace a line or plane, use parametric form.
To test membership, use scalar or normal form.
To find intersections, substitute parametric equations into scalar equations.
To compute angles, use dot products between direction vectors or normal vectors.
To compute distances, use projections and normal vectors.



5.15.3 Strategy 3: Check dimensions
A common source of mistakes is using a formula in the wrong dimension.

A line in \(\mathbb R^2\) can be represented by one scalar equation.
A plane in \(\mathbb R^3\) can be represented by one scalar equation.
A line in \(\mathbb R^3\) usually needs either a parametric equation or two scalar equations.
A hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\) is represented by one scalar equation.



5.15.4 Strategy 4: Check degeneracy
Some formulas require nonzero or nonparallel vectors.

A direction vector for a line cannot be zero.
Two direction vectors for a plane cannot be parallel.
Three points determine a plane only if they are not collinear.
A symmetric line equation cannot divide by a zero component.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.16 4.15 Worked review examples

5.16.1 Example 4.11: Line perpendicular to a plane
Find the line through \((1,2,3)\) perpendicular to
\[
5x+y-2z=4.
\]
Solution. A normal vector to the plane is
\[
n=\langle 5,1,-2\rangle.
\]
A line perpendicular to the plane has this normal vector as a direction vector. Therefore
\[
r(t)=\langle 1,2,3\rangle+t\langle 5,1,-2\rangle.
\]
In coordinates,
\[
x=1+5t,\qquad y=2+t,\qquad z=3-2t.
\]


5.16.2 Example 4.12: Is a line parallel to a plane?
Show that the line
\[
r(t)=\langle 1,4,5\rangle+t\langle 1,1,1\rangle
\]
is parallel to the plane
\[
4x-9y+5z-8=0.
\]
Solution. The direction vector of the line is
\[
v=\langle 1,1,1\rangle.
\]
The normal vector of the plane is
\[
n=\langle 4,-9,5\rangle.
\]
Compute
\[
v\cdot n=4-9+5=0.
\]
Thus the line direction is perpendicular to the plane normal, so the line is parallel to the plane. To decide whether the line lies in the plane or is disjoint from it, plug in a point on the line. At \((1,4,5)\),
\[
4(1)-9(4)+5(5)-8=-15\ne 0.
\]
So the line is parallel to the plane but does not lie in the plane.


5.16.3 Example 4.13: A hyperplane in four dimensions
Let
\[
w=\langle 2,-1,3,4\rangle,
\qquad b=-5.
\]
The hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^4\) is
\[
2x_1-x_2+3x_3+4x_4-5=0.
\]
For the point
\[
x=(1,0,2,-1),
\]
we get
\[
w\cdot x+b=2(1)-0+3(2)+4(-1)-5=-1.
\]
So the point lies on the negative side of the hyperplane. Its signed distance is
\[
\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+3^2+4^2}}=
-\frac{1}{\sqrt{30}}.
\]

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.18 4.17 Exercises

5.18.1 Conceptual questions

Explain the difference between a direction vector and a normal vector.
Why does \(r(t)=P+tv\) describe a line when \(v\ne 0\)?
Why does \(r(s,t)=P+su+tv\) describe a plane only when \(u\) and \(v\) are not parallel?
What is the geometric meaning of the coefficients \(a,b,c\) in \(ax+by+cz+d=0\)?
What does the sign of \(w\cdot x+b\) tell us about a point relative to a hyperplane?



5.18.2 Skills practice

Find vector and parametric equations for the line through \((2,-1,3)\) with direction vector \(\langle 4,0,-2\rangle\).
Find the line segment from \(P=(1,2,3)\) to \(Q=(4,5,7)\).
Find the plane through \((1,2,3)\) with normal vector \(\langle -1,4,2\rangle\).
Find the plane through \((0,1,2)\), \((1,0,3)\), and \((2,1,5)\).
Find the angle between the planes \(x-y+z=2\) and \(2x+y-z=5\).
Find the intersection of the line \(x=1+t\), \(y=2-t\), \(z=3+2t\) with the plane \(x+y+z=10\).
Determine whether the line \(r(t)=\langle 1,0,2\rangle+t\langle 2,-1,3\rangle\) is parallel to the plane \(2x+y-z=4\).



5.18.3 Applications and computation

Write a Python function that returns the signed distance from a point in \(\mathbb R^n\) to the hyperplane \(w\cdot x+b=0\).
Generate random points in \(\mathbb R^2\) and classify them by the sign of \(2x_1-x_2+1\).
For the hyperplane \(x_1+x_2+x_3=1\), compute the signed distances of the points \((1,0,0)\), \((1,1,1)\), and \((0,0,0)\).
A linear regression model with two features has prediction \(\hat y=\beta_0+\beta_1x_1+\beta_2x_2\). Explain why the graph of \(\hat y\) as a function of \((x_1,x_2)\) is a plane.
In logistic regression, the decision boundary is \(w\cdot x+b=0\). Explain why this boundary is a line in two features and a plane in three features.



5.18.4 Challenge problems

Derive the point-to-plane distance formula using projection onto the normal vector.
Let \(P,Q,R\) be three points in \(\mathbb R^3\). Prove that they are collinear if and only if \((Q-P)\times(R-P)=0\).
Let \(H=\{x\in\mathbb R^n:w\cdot x+b=0\}\). Show that \(H\) is a translate of the subspace \(\{x\in\mathbb R^n:w\cdot x=0\}\) whenever \(H\) is nonempty.
Suppose \(A\) is a \(k\times n\) matrix and \(b\in\mathbb R^k\). Explain why the solution set of \(Ax=b\) is an affine subspace when it is nonempty.
Let \(H\) be a hyperplane \(w\cdot x+b=0\). Derive the formula for the orthogonal projection of a point \(p\) onto \(H\).

title: 4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry
text:
5.19 4.18 Chapter summary
The central objects of this chapter are the flat geometric objects of multivariable calculus.

A line is described by \(r(t)=P+tv\).
A line segment is described by \(r(t)=(1-t)P+tQ\) for \(0\le t\le 1\).
A plane in \(\mathbb R^3\) can be written as \(n\cdot(x-P_0)=0\).
A plane can also be parameterized as \(r(s,t)=P_0+su+tv\).
A hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\) is described by \(w\cdot x+b=0\).
Normal vectors are used for scalar equations, distances, and decision boundaries.
Direction vectors are used for parametric equations and tracing objects.
Intersections are usually found by substitution or by solving linear systems.

These ideas prepare us for cross products, curves, tangent planes, gradients, optimization, constrained problems, and linear models in statistics and machine learning.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
4  Chapter 4: Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Parametric Geometry

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.1 Learning goals
In the previous chapters we learned how to describe points, vectors, lines, planes, and hyperplanes. This chapter adds two geometric tools that are essential for the rest of multivariable calculus.
The first tool is the cross product. Unlike the dot product, which returns a scalar, the cross product returns a vector. It encodes orientation, normal directions, area, and volume. It is the natural algebraic language for finding normal vectors to planes, surface orientations, torque, curl, and flux.
The second tool is the calculus of vector-valued functions. A curve in the plane or in space can be described by a position vector
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle.
\]
Differentiating this vector gives velocity. Differentiating again gives acceleration. Integrating speed gives arc length. Differentiating the unit tangent vector gives curvature. These ideas connect geometry, physics, optimization paths, gradient descent trajectories, and data streams evolving through time.
This chapter is a bridge from vector geometry to vector calculus. Later, when we study line integrals, vector fields, Green’s theorem, Stokes’ theorem, and the divergence theorem, we will use the ideas here constantly.
After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.3 5.2 Definition and computation of the cross product
text:
5.3 5.2 Definition and computation of the cross product
Let
\[
\mathbf a=\langle a_1,a_2,a_3\rangle,
\qquad
\mathbf b=\langle b_1,b_2,b_3\rangle.
\]
The cross product \(\mathbf a\times \mathbf b\) is the vector
\[
\mathbf a\times \mathbf b
=
\left\langle
a_2b_3-a_3b_2,
a_3b_1-a_1b_3,
a_1b_2-a_2b_1
\right\rangle.
\]
It is often remembered by the determinant notation
\[
\mathbf a\times \mathbf b
=
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf i & \mathbf j & \mathbf k\\
a_1 & a_2 & a_3\\
b_1 & b_2 & b_3
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
Expanding along the first row gives
\[
\mathbf a\times \mathbf b
=(a_2b_3-a_3b_2)\mathbf i
-(a_1b_3-a_3b_1)\mathbf j
+(a_1b_2-a_2b_1)\mathbf k.
\]






WarningThe cross product exists in this form only in \(\mathbb R^3\)



In this calculus course, the cross product is a special operation for three-dimensional vectors. In higher-dimensional linear algebra, the related ideas are expressed using determinants, exterior products, orientations, and differential forms.



5.3.1 Example 5.1: Compute a cross product
Let
\[
\mathbf a=\langle 1,2,3\rangle,
\qquad
\mathbf b=\langle 3,2,1\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf a\times \mathbf b
&=
\left\langle
2\cdot 1-3\cdot 2,
3\cdot 3-1\cdot 1,
1\cdot 2-2\cdot 3
\right\rangle\\
&=\langle -4,8,-4\rangle.
\end{aligned}
\]
This vector is perpendicular to both \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\):
\[
\langle -4,8,-4\rangle\cdot \langle 1,2,3\rangle=-4+16-12=0,
\]
and
\[
\langle -4,8,-4\rangle\cdot \langle 3,2,1\rangle=-12+16-4=0.
\]
This same computation is a standard way to find a direction vector for a line perpendicular to two given vectors, or a normal vector to a plane spanned by two vectors.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
b = np.array([3, 2, 1])
c = np.cross(a, b)

origin = np.zeros(3)
for vector, label in [(a, 'a'), (b, 'b'), (c, 'a x b')]:
    ax.quiver(*origin, *vector, arrow_length_ratio=0.08)
    ax.text(*(vector * 1.05), label)

# Draw the parallelogram spanned by a and b.
parallelogram = np.array([origin, a, a + b, b, origin])
ax.plot(parallelogram[:, 0], parallelogram[:, 1], parallelogram[:, 2], linewidth=2)

ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Cross product as a normal vector')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 5.1: The cross product \(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b\) is perpendicular to both \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\).

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.4 5.3 Basic properties of the cross product
text:
5.4 5.3 Basic properties of the cross product
For vectors \(\mathbf a,\mathbf b,\mathbf c\) in \(\mathbb R^3\) and scalars \(\lambda\), the cross product satisfies:
\[
\mathbf a\times \mathbf b=-(\mathbf b\times \mathbf a),
\]
\[
\mathbf a\times (\mathbf b+\mathbf c)=\mathbf a\times \mathbf b+\mathbf a\times \mathbf c,
\]
\[
(\lambda \mathbf a)\times \mathbf b
=\lambda(\mathbf a\times \mathbf b)
=\mathbf a\times (\lambda \mathbf b),
\]
\[
\mathbf a\times \mathbf a=\mathbf 0.
\]
The first identity is especially important: order matters. Reversing the order reverses the direction.

5.4.1 Standard basis orientation
The standard basis vectors satisfy
\[
\mathbf i\times \mathbf j=\mathbf k,
\qquad
\mathbf j\times \mathbf k=\mathbf i,
\qquad
\mathbf k\times \mathbf i=\mathbf j.
\]
Reversing the order changes the sign:
\[
\mathbf j\times \mathbf i=-\mathbf k,
\qquad
\mathbf k\times \mathbf j=-\mathbf i,
\qquad
\mathbf i\times \mathbf k=-\mathbf j.
\]
This orientation is called the right-hand rule. If the fingers of your right hand rotate from \(\mathbf a\) toward \(\mathbf b\), then your thumb points in the direction of \(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

vectors = [np.array([1, 0, 0]), np.array([0, 1, 0]), np.array([0, 0, 1])]
labels = ['i', 'j', 'k']
origin = np.zeros(3)

for vector, label in zip(vectors, labels):
    ax.quiver(*origin, *vector, arrow_length_ratio=0.1)
    ax.text(*(1.12 * vector), label, fontsize=12)

ax.set_xlim(0, 1.3)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.3)
ax.set_zlim(0, 1.3)
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Right-handed orientation')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 5.2: The standard right-handed frame: \(\mathbf i\times\mathbf j=\mathbf k\).

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.5 5.4 Area from the cross product
The magnitude of the cross product has a geometric meaning:
\[
|\mathbf a\times \mathbf b|=|\mathbf a||\mathbf b|\sin\theta,
\]
where \(\theta\) is the angle between \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\).
This is the area of the parallelogram spanned by \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\).
Therefore:
\[
\text{Area of parallelogram}=|\mathbf a\times\mathbf b|,
\]
and
\[
\text{Area of triangle}=\frac12|\mathbf a\times\mathbf b|.
\]

5.5.1 Example 5.2: Area of a triangle
Find the area of the triangle with vertices
\[
O=(0,0,0),\qquad A=(1,2,3),\qquad B=(3,2,1).
\]
The two side vectors from \(O\) are
\[
\overrightarrow{OA}=\langle 1,2,3\rangle,
\qquad
\overrightarrow{OB}=\langle 3,2,1\rangle.
\]
We computed
\[
\overrightarrow{OA}\times \overrightarrow{OB}=\langle -4,8,-4\rangle.
\]
Thus
\[
|\overrightarrow{OA}\times \overrightarrow{OB}|
=\sqrt{(-4)^2+8^2+(-4)^2}
=\sqrt{96}=4\sqrt 6.
\]
So the area of the triangle is
\[
\frac12(4\sqrt6)=2\sqrt6.
\]
This example is also a good reminder that a cross product gives both magnitude and orientation.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.6 5.5 Normal vectors and planes
A plane in \(\mathbb R^3\) can be described using a point \(P_0=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) and a normal vector
\[
\mathbf n=\langle A,B,C\rangle.
\]
The equation is
\[
\mathbf n\cdot \langle x-x_0,y-y_0,z-z_0\rangle=0.
\]
In standard form,
\[
A(x-x_0)+B(y-y_0)+C(z-z_0)=0.
\]
If a plane is spanned by two nonparallel vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\), then a normal vector is
\[
\mathbf n=\mathbf u\times \mathbf v.
\]

5.6.1 Example 5.3: Plane through three points
Find an equation of the plane through
\[
P=(1,2,3),\qquad Q=(2,2,5),\qquad R=(3,4,1).
\]
First compute two direction vectors in the plane:
\[
\overrightarrow{PQ}=Q-P=\langle 1,0,2\rangle,
\]
and
\[
\overrightarrow{PR}=R-P=\langle 2,2,-2\rangle.
\]
A normal vector is
\[
\overrightarrow{PQ}\times \overrightarrow{PR}
=
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf i & \mathbf j & \mathbf k\\
1 & 0 & 2\\
2 & 2 & -2
\end{vmatrix}
=\langle -4,6,2\rangle.
\]
We may divide by \(2\) and use
\[
\mathbf n=\langle -2,3,1\rangle.
\]
Using point \(P=(1,2,3)\), the plane is
\[
-2(x-1)+3(y-2)+(z-3)=0.
\]
Simplifying gives
\[
-2x+3y+z-7=0.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

P = np.array([1, 2, 3])
Q = np.array([2, 2, 5])
R = np.array([3, 4, 1])
u = Q - P
v = R - P
n = np.cross(u, v)

s_vals = np.linspace(-0.5, 1.2, 8)
t_vals = np.linspace(-0.5, 1.2, 8)
S, T = np.meshgrid(s_vals, t_vals)
X = P[0] + S * u[0] + T * v[0]
Y = P[1] + S * u[1] + T * v[1]
Z = P[2] + S * u[2] + T * v[2]

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.35)
ax.scatter([P[0], Q[0], R[0]], [P[1], Q[1], R[1]], [P[2], Q[2], R[2]], s=50)

for point, label in [(P, 'P'), (Q, 'Q'), (R, 'R')]:
    ax.text(*(point + np.array([0.05, 0.05, 0.05])), label)

ax.quiver(*P, *u, arrow_length_ratio=0.08)
ax.quiver(*P, *v, arrow_length_ratio=0.08)
ax.quiver(*P, *(n / np.linalg.norm(n)), length=2.0, arrow_length_ratio=0.1)
ax.text(*(P + 2.1 * n / np.linalg.norm(n)), 'normal')

ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Plane and normal from a cross product')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 5.3: A cross product gives a normal vector to the plane through three non-collinear points.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.7 5.6 Scalar triple product and volume
The scalar triple product of \(\mathbf a\), \(\mathbf b\), and \(\mathbf c\) is
\[
\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c).
\]
Geometrically,
\[
|\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)|
\]
is the volume of the parallelepiped determined by \(\mathbf a\), \(\mathbf b\), and \(\mathbf c\).
Using a determinant,
\[
\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)
=
\begin{vmatrix}
a_1&a_2&a_3\\
b_1&b_2&b_3\\
c_1&c_2&c_3
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
If the scalar triple product is zero, then the three vectors are coplanar.

5.7.1 Example 5.4: Test coplanarity
Let
\[
\mathbf a=\langle -3,0,6\rangle,
\qquad
\mathbf b=\langle 4,1,-7\rangle,
\qquad
\mathbf c=\langle 1,-2,-4\rangle.
\]
Compute
\[
\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)
=
\begin{vmatrix}
-3&0&6\\
4&1&-7\\
1&-2&-4
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf b\times\mathbf c
&=
\langle 1(-4)-(-7)(-2),
(-7)(1)-4(-4),
4(-2)-1(1)\rangle\\
&=\langle -18,9,-9\rangle.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)
=\langle -3,0,6\rangle\cdot\langle -18,9,-9\rangle
=54+0-54=0.
\]
Therefore the vectors are coplanar.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A non-coplanar example for visualization.
a = np.array([2, 0, 1])
b = np.array([0, 2, 1])
c = np.array([1, 1, 3])
volume = abs(np.dot(a, np.cross(b, c)))

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
origin = np.zeros(3)

for vector, label in [(a, 'a'), (b, 'b'), (c, 'c')]:
    ax.quiver(*origin, *vector, arrow_length_ratio=0.08)
    ax.text(*(1.05 * vector), label)

# Edges of the parallelepiped.
points = [origin, a, b, c, a+b, a+c, b+c, a+b+c]
edges = [(0,1),(0,2),(0,3),(1,4),(1,5),(2,4),(2,6),(3,5),(3,6),(4,7),(5,7),(6,7)]
for i, j in edges:
    p, q = points[i], points[j]
    ax.plot([p[0], q[0]], [p[1], q[1]], [p[2], q[2]], linewidth=1.5)

ax.set_title(f'Parallelepiped volume = {volume:.1f}')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 1])
plt.show()







Figure 5.4: The scalar triple product measures signed volume; the absolute value is the volume of the parallelepiped.

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.8 5.7 Vector-valued functions and space curves
A vector-valued function takes a scalar input and returns a vector:
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle.
\]
The image of \(\mathbf r(t)\) is a curve in space. The parameter \(t\) may represent time, but it does not have to. It may simply be a variable that traces the curve.

5.8.1 Example 5.5: A helix
The curve
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle
\]
is a helix. Its projection onto the \(xy\)-plane is the unit circle, while the \(z\)-coordinate increases linearly.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 6 * np.pi, 500)
x = np.cos(t)
y = np.sin(t)
z = t

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot(x, y, z, linewidth=2)

# Show a tangent vector at t0.
t0 = np.pi
p = np.array([np.cos(t0), np.sin(t0), t0])
v = np.array([-np.sin(t0), np.cos(t0), 1])
ax.quiver(*p, *v, length=1.3, normalize=True, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
ax.text(*(p + v / np.linalg.norm(v) * 1.4), 'tangent')

ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('A space curve and one tangent vector')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 3])
plt.show()







Figure 5.5: The helix \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle\cos t,\sin t,t\rangle\) winds around the \(z\)-axis.

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.9 5.8 Derivatives and integrals of vector-valued functions
text:
5.9 5.8 Derivatives and integrals of vector-valued functions
The derivative of
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle
\]
is computed component by component:
\[
\mathbf r'(t)=\langle x'(t),y'(t),z'(t)\rangle.
\]
Similarly,
\[
\int_a^b \mathbf r(t)\,dt
=
\left\langle
\int_a^b x(t)\,dt,
\int_a^b y(t)\,dt,
\int_a^b z(t)\,dt
\right\rangle.
\]
The product rules are analogous to ordinary calculus:
\[
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\mathbf a(t)\cdot\mathbf b(t)\right)
=\mathbf a'(t)\cdot\mathbf b(t)+\mathbf a(t)\cdot\mathbf b'(t),
\]
and
\[
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\mathbf a(t)\times\mathbf b(t)\right)
=\mathbf a'(t)\times\mathbf b(t)+\mathbf a(t)\times\mathbf b'(t).
\]

5.9.1 Example 5.6: Differentiate a vector function
Let
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle t^3,\tfrac12t,t^2-4t\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf r'(t)=\langle 3t^2,\tfrac12,2t-4\rangle,
\]
and
\[
\mathbf r''(t)=\langle 6t,0,2\rangle.
\]
At \(t=1\),
\[
\mathbf r'(1)=\langle 3,\tfrac12,-2\rangle,
\qquad
\mathbf r''(1)=\langle 6,0,2\rangle.
\]

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.10 5.9 Motion in space: velocity, speed, and acceleration
text:
5.10 5.9 Motion in space: velocity, speed, and acceleration
Suppose a particle moves through space with position vector
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle.
\]
The velocity vector is
\[
\mathbf v(t)=\mathbf r'(t).
\]
The speed is the magnitude of the velocity vector:
\[
\text{speed}=|\mathbf v(t)|=|\mathbf r'(t)|.
\]
The acceleration vector is
\[
\mathbf a(t)=\mathbf v'(t)=\mathbf r''(t).
\]






ImportantVector versus scalar quantities



Velocity is a vector because it has magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar because it is only the magnitude of velocity.



5.10.1 Example 5.7: Motion on a helix
Let
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle \sin t,\cos t,t\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf v(t)=\mathbf r'(t)=\langle \cos t,-\sin t,1\rangle,
\]
and
\[
\mathbf a(t)=\mathbf r''(t)=\langle -\sin t,-\cos t,0\rangle.
\]
The speed is
\[
|\mathbf v(t)|=\sqrt{\cos^2t+\sin^2t+1}=\sqrt2.
\]
So the particle moves with constant speed even though its direction is changing.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, 400)
x = np.sin(t)
y = np.cos(t)
z = t

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot(x, y, z, linewidth=2)

for t0 in [np.pi/3, 4*np.pi/3, 7*np.pi/3]:
    p = np.array([np.sin(t0), np.cos(t0), t0])
    v = np.array([np.cos(t0), -np.sin(t0), 1])
    a = np.array([-np.sin(t0), -np.cos(t0), 0])
    ax.quiver(*p, *(v / np.linalg.norm(v)), length=0.9, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.quiver(*p, *(a / np.linalg.norm(a)), length=0.7, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)

ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Velocity and acceleration along a helix')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 3])
plt.show()







Figure 5.6: On a helix, velocity is tangent to the curve while acceleration points inward toward the axis.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.11 5.10 Recovering velocity and position from acceleration
text:
5.11 5.10 Recovering velocity and position from acceleration
Because
\[
\mathbf a(t)=\mathbf v'(t),
\]
we can recover velocity by integration:
\[
\mathbf v(t)=\mathbf v(0)+\int_0^t \mathbf a(u)\,du.
\]
Because
\[
\mathbf v(t)=\mathbf r'(t),
\]
we can recover position by another integration:
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\mathbf r(0)+\int_0^t \mathbf v(u)\,du.
\]

5.11.1 Example 5.8: From acceleration to position
A particle starts at
\[
\mathbf r(0)=\langle 0,0,1\rangle
\]
with initial velocity
\[
\mathbf v(0)=\mathbf i+\mathbf k=\langle 1,0,1\rangle.
\]
Its acceleration is
\[
\mathbf a(t)=\langle 2t,1,6t\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf v(t)
=\langle 1,0,1\rangle+
\int_0^t \langle 2u,1,6u\rangle\,du
=\langle 1+t^2,t,1+3t^2\rangle.
\]
Now integrate again:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf r(t)
&=\langle 0,0,1\rangle+
\int_0^t \langle 1+u^2,u,1+3u^2\rangle\,du\\
&=\left\langle t+\frac{t^3}{3},\frac{t^2}{2},1+t+t^3\right\rangle.
\end{aligned}
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 2.5, 300)
x = t + t**3 / 3
y = t**2 / 2
z = 1 + t + t**3

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot(x, y, z, linewidth=2)
ax.scatter([x[0]], [y[0]], [z[0]], s=50)
ax.text(x[0], y[0], z[0], 'start')
ax.scatter([x[-1]], [y[-1]], [z[-1]], s=50)
ax.text(x[-1], y[-1], z[-1], 'end')

ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Position from acceleration')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 2])
plt.show()







Figure 5.7: A particle trajectory recovered by integrating acceleration to velocity and position.

Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.12 5.11 Arc length
Suppose a curve is given by
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle,
\qquad a\le t\le b,
\]
and assume the curve is traversed exactly once. The length of the curve is
\[
L=
\int_a^b |\mathbf r'(t)|\,dt.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
L=
\int_a^b
\sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+
\left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^2+
\left(\frac{dz}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt.
\]

5.12.1 Example 5.9: Length of a circular helix
Let
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le 2\pi.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf r'(t)=\langle -\sin t,\cos t,1\rangle,
\]
so
\[
|\mathbf r'(t)|=\sqrt{\sin^2t+\cos^2t+1}=\sqrt2.
\]
Therefore
\[
L=\int_0^{2\pi}\sqrt2\,dt=2\sqrt2\pi.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.13 5.12 Arc length parameterization
The arc length function from \(t=a\) to \(t\) is
\[
s(t)=\int_a^t |\mathbf r'(u)|\,du.
\]
When \(s(t)\) can be solved for \(t\) as a function of \(s\), we can reparameterize the curve by arc length. This is useful because the new parameter directly measures distance traveled along the curve.

5.13.1 Example 5.10: Reparameterize a helix by arc length
For
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle,
\qquad t\ge 0,
\]
we have \(|\mathbf r'(t)|=\sqrt2\). Thus
\[
s(t)=\int_0^t \sqrt2\,du=\sqrt2t.
\]
Solving for \(t\) gives
\[
t=\frac{s}{\sqrt2}.
\]
Therefore the arc length parameterization is
\[
\mathbf r(s)=
\left\langle
\cos\left(\frac{s}{\sqrt2}\right),
\sin\left(\frac{s}{\sqrt2}\right),
\frac{s}{\sqrt2}
\right\rangle.
\]
With this parameterization,
\[
\left|\frac{d\mathbf r}{ds}\right|=1.
\]
That is why an arc length parameterization is also called a unit-speed parameterization.
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.14 5.13 Unit tangent vector
For a smooth curve with \(\mathbf r'(t)\ne \mathbf 0\), the unit tangent vector is
\[
\mathbf T(t)=\frac{\mathbf r'(t)}{|\mathbf r'(t)|}.
\]
This vector points in the direction of motion, but has length \(1\).
For the helix
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle,
\]
we have
\[
\mathbf T(t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}\langle -\sin t,\cos t,1\rangle.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part I. Geometry, Vectors, and Spaces
5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.15 5.14 Curvature
Curvature measures how quickly a curve changes direction. A straight line has curvature \(0\). A small circle has larger curvature than a large circle.
The conceptual definition is
\[
\kappa=\left|\frac{d\mathbf T}{ds}\right|,
\]
where \(s\) is arc length.
A practical formula is
\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{|\mathbf T'(t)|}{|\mathbf r'(t)|}.
\]
For many computations in \(\mathbb R^3\), the most convenient formula is
\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{|\mathbf r'(t)\times\mathbf r''(t)|}{|\mathbf r'(t)|^3}.
\]

5.15.1 Example 5.11: Curvature of a circle
Let a circle of radius \(a\) be parameterized by
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle a\cos t,a\sin t,0\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf r'(t)=\langle -a\sin t,a\cos t,0\rangle,
\]
and
\[
\mathbf r''(t)=\langle -a\cos t,-a\sin t,0\rangle.
\]
We have
\[
|\mathbf r'(t)|=a,
\]
and
\[
|\mathbf r'(t)\times\mathbf r''(t)|=a^2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{a^2}{a^3}=\frac1a.
\]
Thus a circle with larger radius has smaller curvature.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 400)
radii = [0.7, 1.2, 2.0]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for radius in radii:
    x = radius * np.cos(theta)
    y = radius * np.sin(theta)
    ax.plot(x, y, label=f'radius {radius}, curvature {1/radius:.2f}')

ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_title('Curvature of circles')
ax.legend()
plt.show()







Figure 5.8: Smaller circles have larger curvature; a circle of radius \(a\) has curvature \(1/a\).







5.15.2 Example 5.12: Curvature of the circular helix
Let
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf r'(t)=\langle -\sin t,\cos t,1\rangle,
\]
and
\[
\mathbf r''(t)=\langle -\cos t,-\sin t,0\rangle.
\]
Compute the cross product:
\[
\mathbf r'(t)\times \mathbf r''(t)
=\langle \sin t,-\cos t,1\rangle.
\]
Thus
\[
|\mathbf r'(t)\times \mathbf r''(t)|=\sqrt{\sin^2t+\cos^2t+1}=\sqrt2,
\]
and
\[
|\mathbf r'(t)|=\sqrt2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{\sqrt2}{(\sqrt2)^3}=\frac12.
\]
The helix has constant curvature.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 5 * np.pi, 500)
r = np.column_stack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t), t])
rp = np.column_stack([-np.sin(t), np.cos(t), np.ones_like(t)])
rpp = np.column_stack([-np.cos(t), -np.sin(t), np.zeros_like(t)])
curvature = np.linalg.norm(np.cross(rp, rpp), axis=1) / np.linalg.norm(rp, axis=1)**3

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
curve = ax.scatter(r[:, 0], r[:, 1], r[:, 2], c=curvature, s=8)
fig.colorbar(curve, ax=ax, shrink=0.65, label='curvature')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_zlabel('z')
ax.set_title('Curvature along a helix')
ax.set_box_aspect([1, 1, 3])
plt.show()







Figure 5.9: A circular helix has constant curvature; its tangent direction turns at a constant rate relative to arc length.

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.16 5.15 Finding when speed is minimized
In applications, a trajectory may have variable speed. Since speed is nonnegative, minimizing speed is equivalent to minimizing the square of speed.

5.16.1 Example 5.13: Minimum speed
Let
\[
\mathbf r(t)=\langle t^2+1,3t,t^2-4t\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf v(t)=\mathbf r'(t)=\langle 2t,3,2t-4\rangle.
\]
The speed is
\[
|\mathbf v(t)|=\sqrt{(2t)^2+3^2+(2t-4)^2}.
\]
Instead of minimizing this square root, minimize
\[
|\mathbf v(t)|^2=4t^2+9+(2t-4)^2.
\]
Simplify:
\[
|\mathbf v(t)|^2=4t^2+9+4t^2-16t+16=8t^2-16t+25.
\]
This quadratic is minimized when
\[
t=\frac{-(-16)}{2\cdot 8}=1.
\]
Therefore the speed is minimized at \(t=1\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(-1, 3, 400)
speed = np.sqrt((2*t)**2 + 3**2 + (2*t - 4)**2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(t, speed, linewidth=2)
ax.axvline(1, linestyle='--')
ax.scatter([1], [np.sqrt(17)], s=50)
ax.set_xlabel('t')
ax.set_ylabel('speed')
ax.set_title('Speed along a trajectory')
plt.show()







Figure 5.10: The speed of \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle t^2+1,3t,t^2-4t\rangle\) is minimized at \(t=1\).

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.17 5.16 Modern viewpoint: trajectories in data and optimization
text:
5.17 5.16 Modern viewpoint: trajectories in data and optimization
A vector-valued function does not need to describe a physical particle. It can describe any time-indexed or parameter-indexed state.

5.17.1 Example: gradient descent as a curve
In machine learning, an optimization algorithm generates a sequence of model parameters
\[
\theta_0,\theta_1,\theta_2,\dots
\]
where each \(\theta_k\) may be a vector in a very high-dimensional space. If we interpolate these points, we can view training as a curve in parameter space.
The velocity of this curve describes how fast the parameters are changing. The curvature describes how sharply the path bends. A highly curved path may indicate unstable optimization, poor scaling, or a loss landscape with narrow valleys.


5.17.2 Example: data streams
A time series with several measurements at each time can be represented as
\[
\mathbf x(t)=\langle x_1(t),x_2(t),\dots,x_n(t)\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbf x'(t)
\]
measures the instantaneous change of all features. This is useful in dynamical systems, finance, biology, robotics, and sensor data.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A toy loss surface and gradient descent path.
def grad(theta):
    x, y = theta
    return np.array([2*x + 0.8*y, 0.8*x + 6*y])

theta = np.array([3.0, 2.0])
path = [theta.copy()]
eta = 0.12
for _ in range(35):
    theta = theta - eta * grad(theta)
    path.append(theta.copy())
path = np.array(path)

x = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 200)
y = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 200)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = X**2 + 0.8*X*Y + 3*Y**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=25)
ax.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], marker='o', markersize=3)
ax.set_xlabel('parameter 1')
ax.set_ylabel('parameter 2')
ax.set_title('Gradient descent trajectory')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
plt.show()







Figure 5.11: An optimization path can be viewed as a vector-valued trajectory in parameter space.

text:
5.18 5.17 Summary of core formulas







Concept
Formula




Cross product
\(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b=\langle a_2b_3-a_3b_2, a_3b_1-a_1b_3, a_1b_2-a_2b_1\rangle\)


Orthogonality
\((\mathbf a\times\mathbf b)\cdot\mathbf a=0\), \((\mathbf a\times\mathbf b)\cdot\mathbf b=0\)


Parallelogram area
\(|\mathbf a\times\mathbf b|\)


Triangle area
\(\frac12|\mathbf a\times\mathbf b|\)


Scalar triple product
\(\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)\)


Parallelepiped volume
\(|\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)|\)


Curve
\(\mathbf r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle\)


Velocity
\(\mathbf v(t)=\mathbf r'(t)\)


Speed
\(|\mathbf v(t)|=|\mathbf r'(t)|\)


Acceleration
\(\mathbf a(t)=\mathbf r''(t)\)


Arc length
\(L=\int_a^b |\mathbf r'(t)|\,dt\)


Arc length function
\(s(t)=\int_a^t |\mathbf r'(u)|\,du\)


Unit tangent
\(\mathbf T(t)=\frac{\mathbf r'(t)}{|\mathbf r'(t)|}\)


Curvature
\(\kappa=\left|\frac{d\mathbf T}{ds}\right|\)


Curvature formula
\(\kappa(t)=\frac{|\mathbf r'(t)\times\mathbf r''(t)|}{|\mathbf r'(t)|^3}\)

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
text:
5.19 5.18 Practice problems

5.19.1 Conceptual questions

Explain why \(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b\) is perpendicular to both \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\).
Why does reversing the order of a cross product reverse its direction?
Why does \(|\mathbf a\times\mathbf b|\) represent area?
What does it mean if \(\mathbf a\cdot(\mathbf b\times\mathbf c)=0\)?
Explain the difference between velocity and speed.
Why is arc length parameterization called unit-speed parameterization?
Why is curvature defined using \(d\mathbf T/ds\) rather than simply \(d\mathbf T/dt\)?



5.19.2 Computational exercises

Compute \(\langle 2,-1,4\rangle\times\langle 0,3,1\rangle\).
Find the area of the parallelogram spanned by \(\langle 1,2,0\rangle\) and \(\langle 3,-1,4\rangle\).
Find the area of the triangle with vertices \(P=(1,0,2)\), \(Q=(3,1,1)\), and \(R=(0,2,4)\).
Find a normal vector to the plane through \(P=(1,1,0)\), \(Q=(2,0,1)\), and \(R=(0,3,2)\).
Find an equation of the plane through the points in Problem 4.
Compute the volume of the parallelepiped determined by \(\mathbf a=\langle 1,0,2\rangle\), \(\mathbf b=\langle 2,1,1\rangle\), and \(\mathbf c=\langle 0,3,1\rangle\).
Determine whether \(\langle 1,2,3\rangle\), \(\langle 2,4,6\rangle\), and \(\langle 0,1,1\rangle\) are coplanar.
Let \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle t,t^2,t^3\rangle\). Find \(\mathbf v(t)\), \(\mathbf a(t)\), and speed.
Let \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos(2t),\sin(2t),3t\rangle\). Find speed.
Find the arc length of \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle 3t,4t,12t\rangle\) for \(0\le t\le 2\).
Find the arc length of \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,2t\rangle\) for \(0\le t\le 2\pi\).
Compute the curvature of \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,0\rangle\).
Compute the curvature of \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle 2\cos t,2\sin t,0\rangle\).
Find when the speed of \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle t^2,t^2-2t,3t\rangle\) is minimized.



5.19.3 Python exercises

Write a Python function cross(a, b) that computes the cross product without using np.cross.
Use Python to verify that \(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b\) is perpendicular to both \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) for five random examples.
Plot the curve \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,0.2t\rangle\) for \(0\le t\le 8\pi\).
Plot velocity vectors at five points on the curve in Problem 3.
Numerically approximate arc length for \(\mathbf r(t)=\langle t,t^2,t^3\rangle\) on \(0\le t\le 1\).
Use finite differences to approximate velocity and acceleration from sampled position data.
Simulate a gradient descent path for a quadratic function and plot the trajectory on contour lines.

title: 5  Chapter 5: Cross Products, Curves, and Motion in Space
section: 5.20 5.19 Chapter project: tracking motion from data
text:
5.20 5.19 Chapter project: tracking motion from data
Suppose a sensor records the position of an object at equally spaced times. The data are noisy, so you do not know \(\mathbf r(t)\) exactly. Your goal is to estimate velocity, speed, acceleration, and curvature from the data.
A possible workflow:

Load or simulate position data \(\mathbf r(t_k)\).
Estimate velocity using finite differences.
Estimate speed as the norm of velocity.
Estimate acceleration using finite differences again.
Estimate curvature using

\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{|\mathbf r'(t)\times\mathbf r''(t)|}{|\mathbf r'(t)|^3}.
\]

Plot the trajectory and color it by speed or curvature.

This project connects calculus to robotics, sports tracking, physics simulations, autonomous systems, and time-dependent data analysis.

title: 6  Chapter 6. Functions of Several Variables
text:
6.1 Chapter overview
Single-variable calculus studies functions of the form
\[
y=f(x),
\]
where one real input produces one real output. Multivariable calculus studies functions such as
\[
z=f(x,y), \qquad w=f(x,y,z), \qquad F=f(x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n).
\]
The input is no longer a point on a line. It may be a point in the plane, a point in space, or a vector in a high-dimensional parameter space. The output is still a scalar: a height, temperature, density, probability, loss, likelihood, energy, cost, or risk.
This chapter builds the language for these objects. We will learn how to describe domains, draw surfaces, read contour maps, study level curves and level surfaces, and use lower-dimensional views to understand high-dimensional functions.
crumbs: Book

title: 6  Chapter 6. Functions of Several Variables
text:
NoteRole in the book



The next several chapters study limits, derivatives, gradients, optimization, and multiple integrals. All of those topics depend on the basic geometric viewpoint developed here: a multivariable function is a scalar field over a region of space.
