title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.2 Oriented curves and parameterizations
A curve \(C\) in the plane or space is usually described by a vector-valued function
\[
r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t)\rangle,
\qquad a\le t\le b,
\]
or
\[
r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle,
\qquad a\le t\le b.
\]
The direction in which \(t\) increases gives the orientation of the curve. The same geometric curve can have the opposite orientation if we traverse it in reverse.
The velocity vector is
\[
r'(t)=\langle x'(t),y'(t)\rangle
\]
in the plane, or
\[
r'(t)=\langle x'(t),y'(t),z'(t)\rangle
\]
in space. Its magnitude is the speed along the curve. The arc length differential is
\[
ds=|r'(t)|\,dt.
\]
For a space curve,
\[
|r'(t)|=\sqrt{[x'(t)]^2+[y'(t)]^2+[z'(t)]^2}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
x = np.cos(t) + 0.25*np.cos(3*t)
y = np.sin(t) + 0.25*np.sin(2*t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=2)

for s in np.linspace(0.3, 5.7, 8):
    xs = np.cos(s) + 0.25*np.cos(3*s)
    ys = np.sin(s) + 0.25*np.sin(2*s)
    dxs = -np.sin(s) - 0.75*np.sin(3*s)
    dys = np.cos(s) + 0.50*np.cos(2*s)
    norm = np.hypot(dxs, dys)
    ax.arrow(xs, ys, 0.16*dxs/norm, 0.16*dys/norm,
             head_width=0.05, length_includes_head=True)

ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Orientation and tangent direction')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 20.1: An oriented curve with tangent vectors. The arrows show the direction in which the parameter increases.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
section: 20.3 Scalar line integrals with respect to arc length
text:
20.3 Scalar line integrals with respect to arc length
Suppose \(f(x,y)\) is a scalar field defined on a curve \(C\). The scalar line integral of \(f\) along \(C\) with respect to arc length is
\[
\int_C f(x,y)\,ds.
\]
If \(C\) is parameterized by \(r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t)\rangle\) for \(a\le t\le b\), then
\[
\int_C f(x,y)\,ds
=
\int_a^b f(x(t),y(t))\sqrt{[x'(t)]^2+[y'(t)]^2}\,dt.
\]
For a space curve \(r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle\),
\[
\int_C f(x,y,z)\,ds
=
\int_a^b f(x(t),y(t),z(t))\sqrt{[x'(t)]^2+[y'(t)]^2+[z'(t)]^2}\,dt.
\]
The factor \(ds=|r'(t)|dt\) converts the parameter interval into actual distance traveled along the curve.






ImportantScalar line integrals do not depend on orientation



Because \(ds\) measures length, reversing the orientation of the same curve does not change \(\int_C f\,ds\). The curve is traversed in the opposite direction, but the same small lengths are added.



20.3.1 Example 1: scalar line integral over a quarter circle
Evaluate
\[
\int_C (3-xy^2)\,ds,
\]
where \(C\) is the first-quadrant part of the unit circle \(x^2+y^2=1\).
Use the parameterization
\[
r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le \frac{\pi}{2}.
\]
Then
\[
r'(t)=\langle -\sin t,\cos t\rangle,
\qquad |r'(t)|=1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_C (3-xy^2)\,ds
&=\int_0^{\pi/2} \left(3-\cos t\sin^2t\right)\,dt \\
&=\frac{3\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{3}.
\end{aligned}
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, np.pi/2, 300)
x = np.cos(t)
y = np.sin(t)
values = 3 - x*y**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
sc = ax.scatter(x, y, c=values, s=24)
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=1)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(r'Sampling $f(x,y)=3-xy^2$ along $C$')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
fig.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, label='field value')
plt.show()







Figure 20.2: A scalar field sampled along a quarter circle. A scalar line integral accumulates field values weighted by arc length.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.4 Mass of a wire
A major interpretation of a scalar line integral is the mass of a thin wire. Suppose a wire has shape \(C\) and density \(\rho(x,y,z)\) measured in mass per unit length. Then the mass is
\[
M=\int_C \rho(x,y,z)\,ds.
\]
If the density is constant, this becomes density times length. If the density changes from point to point, the line integral accumulates the varying density along the wire.

20.4.1 Example 2: mass of a helical wire
Let \(C\) be the helix
\[
r(t)=\langle \sin t,\cos t,t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le \pi.
\]
Suppose the density is
\[
\rho(x,y,z)=2x\sin z.
\]
Then
\[
\rho(r(t))=2\sin t\sin t=2\sin^2t.
\]
Also
\[
r'(t)=\langle \cos t,-\sin t,1\rangle,
\qquad |r'(t)|=\sqrt{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\int_C 2x\sin z\,ds
=
\int_0^\pi 2\sin^2t\sqrt{2}\,dt
=
\pi\sqrt{2}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(projection='3d')

t = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 300)
x = np.sin(t)
y = np.cos(t)
z = t
rho = 2*np.sin(t)**2

p = ax.scatter(x, y, z, c=rho, s=16)
ax.plot(x, y, z, linewidth=1)
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_zlabel(r'$z$')
ax.set_title('Scalar density along a helix')
fig.colorbar(p, ax=ax, shrink=0.65, label='density')
plt.show()







Figure 20.3: A helix with scalar density sampled along the curve. The scalar line integral gives the mass of a wire with this density.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
section: 20.5 Line integrals with respect to coordinates
text:
20.5 Line integrals with respect to coordinates
Line integrals can also be taken with respect to coordinates. In the plane, we may see
\[
\int_C f(x,y)\,dx,
\qquad
\int_C g(x,y)\,dy.
\]
If \(C\) is parameterized by \(x=x(t)\) and \(y=y(t)\), then
\[
\int_C f(x,y)\,dx
=
\int_a^b f(x(t),y(t))x'(t)\,dt,
\]
and
\[
\int_C g(x,y)\,dy
=
\int_a^b g(x(t),y(t))y'(t)\,dt.
\]
In space, similarly,
\[
\int_C f(x,y,z)\,dz
=
\int_a^b f(x(t),y(t),z(t))z'(t)\,dt.
\]
Unlike \(ds\), the coordinate differentials \(dx\), \(dy\), and \(dz\) can be positive or negative. Therefore coordinate line integrals depend on orientation.






Warning\(ds\) versus \(dx\), \(dy\), and \(dz\)



The symbol \(ds\) is a length element and is always nonnegative. The symbols \(dx\), \(dy\), and \(dz\) are signed coordinate changes. Reversing the orientation of a curve leaves \(\int_C f\,ds\) unchanged, but changes the sign of \(\int_C f\,dx\), \(\int_C f\,dy\), and \(\int_C f\,dz\).
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.6 Vector line integrals and work
Suppose an object is displaced by a vector \(d\) while a constant force \(F\) acts on it. The work done is
\[
W=F\cdot d.
\]
If the force varies from point to point and the object moves along a curve \(C\), we chop the curve into small displacement vectors \(\Delta r\). On each small piece, the work is approximately
\[
F\cdot \Delta r.
\]
Adding these contributions and taking a limit gives the vector line integral
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr.
\]
If
\[
F(x,y)=\langle P(x,y),Q(x,y)\rangle
\]
and \(r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t)\rangle\), then
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_a^b F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\,dt.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy.
\]
In space, if
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle P(x,y,z),Q(x,y,z),R(x,y,z)\rangle,
\]
then
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz.
\]






TipThe computation recipe



To compute \(\int_C F\cdot dr\):

parameterize the curve as \(r(t)\);
compute \(r'(t)\);
substitute the parameterization into the field to get \(F(r(t))\);
take the dot product \(F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\);
integrate with respect to \(t\) over the parameter interval.




20.6.1 Example 3: work along a circular arc
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle y^2,-xy\rangle,
\]
and let \(C\) be the curve
\[
r(t)=\langle \sin t,\cos t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le \frac{\pi}{2}.
\]
Then
\[
r'(t)=\langle \cos t,-\sin t\rangle.
\]
Substitute into the vector field:
\[
F(r(t))=\langle \cos^2t,-\sin t\cos t\rangle.
\]
Now compute the dot product:
\[
F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)
=
\cos^3t+\sin^2t\cos t
=
\cos t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_0^{\pi/2}\cos t\,dt
=1.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x_grid = np.linspace(-0.2, 1.2, 15)
y_grid = np.linspace(-0.2, 1.2, 15)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x_grid, y_grid)
U = Y**2
V = -X*Y

t = np.linspace(0, np.pi/2, 250)
x = np.sin(t)
y = np.cos(t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(X, Y, U, V, angles='xy')
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=3)
ax.scatter([0, 1], [1, 0], zorder=3)
ax.text(0, 1.05, 'start')
ax.text(0.88, -0.08, 'end')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(r'$F(x,y)=\langle y^2,-xy\rangle$ along $C$')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 20.4: Work along a circular arc in a vector field. The integrand is the tangential component of the field times speed.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
section: 20.7 Orientation matters for vector line integrals
text:
20.7 Orientation matters for vector line integrals
Let \(-C\) denote the same curve as \(C\) but with the opposite orientation. Then
\[
\int_{-C} F\cdot dr=-\int_C F\cdot dr.
\]
This agrees with the work interpretation. Pushing an object forward along a path may do positive work; moving the same object backward through the same field reverses the displacement direction and reverses the sign of the work.

20.7.1 Example 4: reversing a path
For the field and curve in Example 3,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=1.
\]
If the same arc is traversed from \((1,0)\) back to \((0,1)\), then
\[
\int_{-C}F\cdot dr=-1.
\]
This sign change is one of the most common sources of mistakes in vector line integrals.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.8 Piecewise smooth curves
Many useful curves are not given by one formula. They are built from several smooth pieces.
If
\[
C=C_1\cup C_2\cup \cdots \cup C_m
\]
with compatible orientations, then
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_{C_1}F\cdot dr+
\int_{C_2}F\cdot dr+\cdots+\int_{C_m}F\cdot dr.
\]
The same additivity holds for scalar line integrals.

20.8.1 Example 5: a piecewise line integral in space
Evaluate
\[
\int_C y\,dx+z\,dy+x\,dz,
\]
where \(C=C_1\cup C_2\), \(C_1\) is the line segment from \((3,4,0)\) to \((3,4,5)\), and \(C_2\) is the line segment from \((3,4,5)\) to \((2,0,0)\).
For \(C_1\), use
\[
r_1(t)=\langle 3,4,t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le 5.
\]
Then \(dx=0\), \(dy=0\), and \(dz=dt\). Hence
\[
\int_{C_1} y\,dx+z\,dy+x\,dz
=\int_0^5 3\,dt
=15.
\]
For \(C_2\), use
\[
r_2(s)=\langle 3-s,4-4s,5-5s\rangle,
\qquad 0\le s\le 1.
\]
Then \(dx=-ds\), \(dy=-4ds\), and \(dz=-5ds\). Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{C_2} y\,dx+z\,dy+x\,dz
&=\int_0^1\left[(4-4s)(-1)+(5-5s)(-4)+(3-s)(-5)\right]ds\\
&=\int_0^1(-39+29s)\,ds\\
&=-\frac{49}{2}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus
\[
\int_C y\,dx+z\,dy+x\,dz
=15-\frac{49}{2}
=-\frac{19}{2}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(projection='3d')

t = np.linspace(0, 5, 100)
x1 = 3 + 0*t
y1 = 4 + 0*t
z1 = t

s = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)
x2 = 3 - s
y2 = 4 - 4*s
z2 = 5 - 5*s

ax.plot(x1, y1, z1, linewidth=3)
ax.plot(x2, y2, z2, linewidth=3)
ax.scatter([3, 3, 2], [4, 4, 0], [0, 5, 0], s=45)
ax.text(3, 4, 0, ' start')
ax.text(3, 4, 5, ' corner')
ax.text(2, 0, 0, ' end')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_zlabel(r'$z$')
ax.set_title('Piecewise curve')
plt.show()







Figure 20.5: A piecewise curve in space. Line integrals over piecewise curves are computed one segment at a time.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.9 Path dependence
A major difference between line integrals and ordinary definite integrals is that path matters. Two different curves with the same starting and ending points can produce different line integrals.

20.9.1 Example 6: same endpoints, different paths
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle y,0\rangle.
\]
We compute the work from \((0,0)\) to \((1,1)\) along two paths.
Path A: straight line. Let
\[
r_A(t)=\langle t,t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le 1.
\]
Then
\[
F(r_A(t))=\langle t,0\rangle,
\qquad r_A'(t)=\langle 1,1\rangle.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_{C_A}F\cdot dr
=\int_0^1 t\,dt
=\frac12.
\]
Path B: horizontal then vertical. First go from \((0,0)\) to \((1,0)\), then from \((1,0)\) to \((1,1)\).
On the horizontal segment, \(y=0\), so \(F=\langle 0,0\rangle\). On the vertical segment, \(dx=0\), and \(F\cdot dr=y\,dx=0\). Hence
\[
\int_{C_B}F\cdot dr=0.
\]
The endpoints are the same, but the work is different. This field is path-dependent.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x_grid = np.linspace(-0.1, 1.1, 13)
y_grid = np.linspace(-0.1, 1.1, 13)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x_grid, y_grid)
U = Y
V = 0*Y

t = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(X, Y, U, V, angles='xy')
ax.plot(t, t, linewidth=3, label='straight path')
ax.plot([0, 1, 1], [0, 0, 1], linewidth=3, linestyle='--', label='two-segment path')
ax.scatter([0, 1], [0, 1], zorder=3)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(r'Path dependence for $F=\langle y,0\rangle$')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
ax.legend()
plt.show()







Figure 20.6: Two paths with the same endpoints can produce different vector line integrals.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.10 A three-dimensional work integral
Let
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle xy,yz,zx\rangle
\]
and let \(C\) be parameterized by
\[
r(t)=\langle t,t^2,t^3\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le 1.
\]
Then
\[
r'(t)=\langle 1,2t,3t^2\rangle.
\]
Substituting into \(F\) gives
\[
F(r(t))=\langle t^3,t^5,t^4\rangle.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)
&=t^3+2t^6+3t^6\\
&=t^3+5t^6.
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=\int_0^1(t^3+5t^6)\,dt
=\frac14+\frac57
=\frac{27}{28}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(projection='3d')

t = np.linspace(0, 1, 250)
x = t
y = t**2
z = t**3

ax.plot(x, y, z, linewidth=3)
for s in np.linspace(0.1, 0.9, 6):
    xs, ys, zs = s, s**2, s**3
    dxs, dys, dzs = 1, 2*s, 3*s**2
    norm = np.sqrt(dxs**2 + dys**2 + dzs**2)
    ax.quiver(xs, ys, zs, 0.12*dxs/norm, 0.12*dys/norm, 0.12*dzs/norm)

ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_zlabel(r'$z$')
ax.set_title(r'$r(t)=\langle t,t^2,t^3\rangle$')
plt.show()







Figure 20.7: A space curve used for a three-dimensional work integral.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
section: 20.11 Numerical approximation of vector line integrals
text:
20.11 Numerical approximation of vector line integrals
If a curve is sampled at points
\[
r_0,r_1,\ldots,r_N,
\]
then a numerical approximation to the work integral is
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
\approx
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}F\left(\frac{r_k+r_{k+1}}{2}\right)\cdot (r_{k+1}-r_k).
\]
This is the midpoint rule along a curve. It is useful when the curve is known only from data, simulation output, robot trajectories, or numerical solutions of differential equations.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def numerical_line_integral(n):
    t = np.linspace(0, np.pi/2, n + 1)
    r = np.column_stack([np.sin(t), np.cos(t)])
    dr = r[1:] - r[:-1]
    mid = 0.5*(r[1:] + r[:-1])
    Fmid = np.column_stack([mid[:, 1]**2, -mid[:, 0]*mid[:, 1]])
    return np.sum(np.einsum('ij,ij->i', Fmid, dr))

ns = np.array([4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512])
approx = np.array([numerical_line_integral(int(n)) for n in ns])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(ns, approx, marker='o')
ax.axhline(1.0, linestyle='--', linewidth=1)
ax.set_xscale('log', base=2)
ax.set_xlabel('number of subintervals')
ax.set_ylabel('midpoint approximation')
ax.set_title('Approximating a work integral')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 20.8: Numerical midpoint approximations converge to the vector line integral as the curve sampling becomes finer.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.12 Line integrals along optimization paths
In machine learning and statistics, we often study a loss function \(L(\theta)\), where \(\theta\) is a vector of parameters. The gradient \(\nabla L(\theta)\) is a vector field on parameter space. An optimization algorithm produces a path
\[
\theta_0,\theta_1,\theta_2,\ldots
\]
through that space.
The work-like quantity
\[
\int_C \nabla L\cdot d\theta
\]
measures accumulated change of the loss along the path. For a smooth path, the chain rule gives
\[
\frac{d}{dt}L(\theta(t))=\nabla L(\theta(t))\cdot \theta'(t),
\]
so line integrals of gradients are closely tied to change in objective values. This idea will become the Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals in the next chapter.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def L(theta):
    x, y = theta
    return 0.5*(4*x**2 + y**2) + 0.6*x*y

def grad_L(theta):
    x, y = theta
    return np.array([4*x + 0.6*y, y + 0.6*x])

xs = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 220)
ys = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 220)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)
Z = 0.5*(4*X**2 + Y**2) + 0.6*X*Y

theta = np.array([2.2, 1.8])
path = [theta.copy()]
step = 0.12
for _ in range(28):
    theta = theta - step*grad_L(theta)
    path.append(theta.copy())
path = np.array(path)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=24)
ax.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], marker='o', markersize=3, linewidth=2)
ax.scatter([0], [0], s=60, zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\theta_1$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\theta_2$')
ax.set_title('Gradient descent path on a loss landscape')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 20.9: A path through a quadratic loss landscape. Line integrals can measure accumulated change along paths in parameter space.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.13 Work, circulation, and flow
The vector line integral \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) measures the component of the field tangent to the curve. If the field points mostly along the direction of motion, the integral is positive. If it points mostly opposite the direction of motion, the integral is negative. If the field is mostly perpendicular to the motion, the contribution is small.
For a closed curve, the integral
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr
\]
is called the circulation of \(F\) around \(C\). Circulation measures how much the vector field tends to push around the loop. It is a central idea behind Green’s theorem and Stokes’ theorem.

20.13.1 Example 7: circulation of a rotational field
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle.
\]
On the unit circle with counterclockwise orientation,
\[
r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le 2\pi.
\]
Then
\[
F(r(t))=\langle -\sin t,\cos t\rangle
\]
and
\[
r'(t)=\langle -\sin t,\cos t\rangle.
\]
Thus
\[
F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)=1.
\]
So
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr=\int_0^{2\pi}1\,dt=2\pi.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

grid = np.linspace(-1.4, 1.4, 17)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(grid, grid)
U = -Y
V = X

t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
x = np.cos(t)
y = np.sin(t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(X, Y, U, V, angles='xy')
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=3)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(r'Circulation of $F=\langle -y,x\rangle$')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 20.10: Positive circulation around the unit circle for the rotational field \(F=\langle -y,x\rangle\).
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.14 Common mistakes






WarningMistake 1: forgetting \(|r'(t)|\) in scalar line integrals



For scalar line integrals, the formula is
\[
\int_C f\,ds=\int_a^b f(r(t))|r'(t)|\,dt.
\]
The factor \(|r'(t)|\) is essential unless the parameterization has unit speed.








WarningMistake 2: using \(|r'(t)|\) in vector line integrals



For vector line integrals, the formula is
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=\int_a^b F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\,dt.
\]
Do not replace \(r'(t)\) by \(|r'(t)|\). The direction of motion matters.








WarningMistake 3: ignoring orientation



Vector line integrals depend on orientation. Reversing the path changes the sign.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.15 Chapter summary
A line integral accumulates quantities along curves.
For scalar fields,
\[
\int_C f\,ds
=
\int_a^b f(r(t))|r'(t)|\,dt.
\]
This measures total mass, charge, cost, or exposure along a curve when \(f\) is a density per unit length.
For vector fields,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_a^b F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\,dt.
\]
This measures work done by a force field or accumulated tangential flow along an oriented curve. In coordinates,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy
\]
in the plane, and
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
=
\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz
\]
in space.
The essential themes are parameterization, orientation, tangential components, and path dependence. The next chapter studies the special vector fields for which line integrals depend only on endpoints.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work

title: 20  Chapter 20. Line Integrals and Work
text:
20.16 Exercises

20.16.1 Conceptual exercises

Explain in your own words the difference between \(\int_C f\,ds\) and \(\int_C F\cdot dr\).
Why does reversing the orientation of a curve not change \(\int_C f\,ds\)?
Why does reversing the orientation of a curve change the sign of \(\int_C F\cdot dr\)?
What does it mean for a vector field to do positive work along a curve?
Give a physical interpretation of \(\int_C \rho\,ds\) when \(\rho\) is a density along a wire.
Give a data-science interpretation of integrating a cost or risk function along a trajectory.



20.16.2 Computational exercises

Evaluate \(\int_C (x+y)\,ds\), where \(C\) is the line segment from \((0,0)\) to \((2,1)\).
Evaluate \(\int_C x^2\,ds\), where \(C\) is the unit circle parameterized counterclockwise.
Evaluate \(\int_C y\,dx+x\,dy\), where \(C\) is the line segment from \((0,0)\) to \((1,2)\).
Evaluate \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) for \(F(x,y)=\langle x,y\rangle\) and \(r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t\rangle\), \(0\le t\le 2\pi\).
Evaluate \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) for \(F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle\) and \(r(t)=\langle 2\cos t,2\sin t\rangle\), \(0\le t\le 2\pi\).
Evaluate \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) for \(F(x,y,z)=\langle z,x,y\rangle\) and \(r(t)=\langle t,t^2,t^3\rangle\), \(0\le t\le 1\).
Evaluate \(\int_C z\,ds\) for the helix \(r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t,t\rangle\), \(0\le t\le 2\pi\).
Let \(F(x,y)=\langle y,0\rangle\). Compute the work from \((0,0)\) to \((1,1)\) along the curve \(y=x^2\).
Let \(F(x,y)=\langle 0,x\rangle\). Compute the work from \((0,0)\) to \((1,1)\) along the straight line and along the path that goes vertically first and then horizontally.



20.16.3 Python exercises

Write a Python function that approximates \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) from sampled curve points.
Use numerical approximation to estimate the work done by \(F(x,y)=\langle \sin y,\cos x\rangle\) along \(r(t)=\langle t,t^2\rangle\), \(0\le t\le 1\).
Compare the numerical approximation from Exercise 17 for \(N=10,20,40,80,160\) subintervals.
Plot the vector field \(F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle\) and three closed curves. Estimate the circulation around each.
Generate a random polygonal path from \((0,0)\) to \((1,1)\) and estimate \(\int_C \langle y,0\rangle\cdot dr\).



20.16.4 Challenge exercises

Find a vector field \(F\) and two paths from \(A\) to \(B\) such that the work along one path is positive and the work along the other path is negative.
Let \(F(x,y)=\langle ax+by,cx+dy\rangle\). Determine conditions on \(a,b,c,d\) so that \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) has the same value for every path from \((0,0)\) to \((1,1)\).
For \(F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle\), compute the circulation around a circle of radius \(a\).
Let \(C\) be any circle centered at the origin, oriented counterclockwise. Explain why \(F(x,y)=\langle x,y\rangle\) has zero circulation around \(C\).
Suppose a curve is reparameterized by a smooth increasing change of variables. Prove that \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) is unchanged.
Suppose a curve is reparameterized by a smooth decreasing change of variables. Prove that \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) changes sign.

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.1 Learning goals
The previous chapter introduced line integrals. We learned how to integrate a scalar field along a curve and how to integrate a vector field along an oriented path. The main question now becomes more structural:
This question is not merely a computational shortcut. It is one of the conceptual centers of vector calculus. In physics, it separates conservative forces from forces with circulation. In geometry, it connects local derivatives to global behavior around closed curves. In optimization, it explains why gradients of loss functions behave differently from rotational vector fields. In data science and machine learning, it gives a language for understanding energy functions, potential landscapes, and dynamics that are or are not driven by a scalar objective.
This chapter develops two major results:
Together, they form the two-dimensional foundation for Stokes’ theorem and the divergence theorem.
After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.2 Gradient fields and conservative vector fields
text:
21.2 Gradient fields and conservative vector fields
A scalar function
\[
f(x,y)
\]
has gradient field
\[
\nabla f(x,y)=\langle f_x(x,y),f_y(x,y)\rangle.
\]
A scalar function
\[
f(x,y,z)
\]
has gradient field
\[
\nabla f(x,y,z)=\langle f_x(x,y,z),f_y(x,y,z),f_z(x,y,z)\rangle.
\]
A vector field \(F\) is called conservative if it is the gradient of some scalar function \(f\):
\[
F=\nabla f.
\]
The function \(f\) is called a potential function for \(F\).
This terminology is natural in physical examples. A gravitational or electrostatic field often points in the direction in which potential energy changes most rapidly. In optimization, the vector field \(-\nabla L\) points in the direction of steepest decrease of the loss function \(L\).






ImportantPotential function



If
\[
F=\langle P,Q\rangle
\]
is conservative in the plane, then there is a scalar function \(f\) such that
\[
P=f_x,
\qquad
Q=f_y.
\]
The scalar function \(f\) is the potential. The vector field \(F\) is the gradient field generated by \(f\).



import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 41)
y = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 41)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Phi = 0.5*(X**2 + 2*Y**2)
U = X
V = 2*Y

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.contour(X, Y, Phi, levels=12, linewidths=1.0)
ax.quiver(X[::3, ::3], Y[::3, ::3], U[::3, ::3], V[::3, ::3], angles='xy')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(r'Gradient field $\nabla \phi$ for $\phi=(x^2+2y^2)/2$')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 21.1: A gradient field is organized by level curves of a potential function. The arrows point perpendicular to the level curves.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.3 The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals
text:
21.3 The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals
The one-variable Fundamental Theorem of Calculus says that if \(F'(x)=f(x)\), then
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=F(b)-F(a).
\]
The multivariable version has the same spirit. If a vector field is a gradient field, then its line integral is the change in the potential.






TipFundamental Theorem for Line Integrals



Let \(C\) be a smooth curve from point \(A\) to point \(B\), and let \(f\) be differentiable on a region containing \(C\). Then
\[
\int_C \nabla f\cdot dr=f(B)-f(A).
\]
Therefore, the integral depends only on the endpoints, not on the path.



21.3.1 Why the theorem is true
Suppose \(C\) is parameterized by
\[
r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t)\rangle,
\qquad a\le t\le b.
\]
Then
\[
\int_C \nabla f\cdot dr
=\int_a^b \nabla f(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\,dt.
\]
By the chain rule,
\[
\frac{d}{dt}f(r(t))=\nabla f(r(t))\cdot r'(t).
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int_C \nabla f\cdot dr
=\int_a^b \frac{d}{dt}f(r(t))\,dt
=f(r(b))-f(r(a)).
\]
The theorem is just the chain rule plus the one-variable Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.


21.3.2 Worked example: use a potential instead of parameterizing the path
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle 2xy, x^2+3y^2\rangle.
\]
Compute
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr
\]
for any smooth curve \(C\) from \(A=(1,0)\) to \(B=(2,1)\).
We look for a potential \(f\) such that
\[
f_x=2xy,
\qquad
f_y=x^2+3y^2.
\]
Integrating \(f_x=2xy\) with respect to \(x\) gives
\[
f(x,y)=x^2y+g(y).
\]
Then
\[
f_y=x^2+g'(y).
\]
Matching \(f_y=x^2+3y^2\) gives
\[
g'(y)=3y^2,
\qquad
g(y)=y^3+C.
\]
Thus one potential is
\[
f(x,y)=x^2y+y^3.
\]
By the Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=f(2,1)-f(1,0)=(4+1)-0=5.
\]
No parameterization of \(C\) is needed.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.4 Path independence
A vector line integral is path independent if any two curves with the same starting point and ending point give the same value.
For a conservative field,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=f(B)-f(A),
\]
so the integral is path independent.
The converse is also true under standard connectedness assumptions: if line integrals of \(F\) are path independent on a region, then \(F\) is conservative on that region.






ImportantEquivalent ideas



For a well-behaved vector field on a suitable region, the following ideas are essentially equivalent:

\(F\) is conservative.
\(F=\nabla f\) for some potential function \(f\).
\(\int_C F\cdot dr\) depends only on endpoints.
\(\int_C F\cdot dr=0\) for every closed curve \(C\).




import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

A = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
B = np.array([2.0, 1.2])
t = np.linspace(0, 1, 300)
path1 = A[:, None]*(1-t) + B[:, None]*t
path2 = np.vstack([2*t, 1.2*t + 0.55*np.sin(np.pi*t)])

x = np.linspace(-0.3, 2.3, 23)
y = np.linspace(-0.3, 1.7, 23)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
U = 2*X*Y
V = X**2 + 3*Y**2
speed = np.hypot(U, V)
U_scaled = U/(1 + speed)
V_scaled = V/(1 + speed)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(X, Y, U_scaled, V_scaled, angles='xy', alpha=0.65)
ax.plot(path1[0], path1[1], linewidth=2, label='straight path')
ax.plot(path2[0], path2[1], linewidth=2, label='curved path')
ax.scatter([A[0], B[0]], [A[1], B[1]], s=60)
ax.text(A[0]-0.08, A[1]-0.08, 'A')
ax.text(B[0]+0.04, B[1]+0.04, 'B')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Path independence for a conservative field')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 21.2: Two different paths from the same start point to the same endpoint. In a conservative field, both paths give the same line integral.






21.4.1 Numerical check: two paths, one answer
For
\[
F(x,y)=\langle 2xy,x^2+3y^2\rangle=\nabla(x^2y+y^3),
\]
the line integral from \((0,0)\) to \((2,1.2)\) should equal
\[
f(2,1.2)-f(0,0).
\]
The code below approximates the integral along two different paths.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

N = 3000
t = np.linspace(0, 1, N)

def F(x, y):
    return 2*x*y, x**2 + 3*y**2

def line_integral(x, y):
    dxdt = np.gradient(x, t)
    dydt = np.gradient(y, t)
    P, Q = F(x, y)
    return np.trapz(P*dxdt + Q*dydt, t)

x1 = 2*t
y1 = 1.2*t

x2 = 2*t
y2 = 1.2*t + 0.55*np.sin(np.pi*t)

I1 = line_integral(x1, y1)
I2 = line_integral(x2, y2)
exact = (2**2)*(1.2) + 1.2**3

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
labels = ['straight path', 'curved path', 'endpoint formula']
values = [I1, I2, exact]
ax.bar(labels, values)
ax.set_ylabel('line integral value')
ax.set_title('The two path integrals agree')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=15)
plt.show()

print(f'Straight path approximation: {I1:.8f}')
print(f'Curved path approximation:   {I2:.8f}')
print(f'Endpoint formula:            {exact:.8f}')







Figure 21.3: Numerical line-integral approximations along two different paths in the same conservative field.





Straight path approximation: 6.52800036
Curved path approximation:   6.52800066
Endpoint formula:            6.52800000

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.5 How to find a potential function
text:
21.5 How to find a potential function
Suppose
\[
F(x,y)=\langle P(x,y),Q(x,y)\rangle.
\]
To find a potential function \(f\):

integrate \(P=f_x\) with respect to \(x\);
include an unknown function of \(y\);
differentiate the result with respect to \(y\);
compare with \(Q=f_y\);
solve for the unknown function.


21.5.1 Worked example: a conservative field
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle y\cos(xy)+2x, x\cos(xy)+4y^3\rangle.
\]
We want \(f_x=y\cos(xy)+2x\). Integrating with respect to \(x\) gives
\[
f(x,y)=\sin(xy)+x^2+g(y).
\]
Then
\[
f_y=x\cos(xy)+g'(y).
\]
Matching the second component gives
\[
g'(y)=4y^3,
\qquad
g(y)=y^4+C.
\]
So one potential is
\[
f(x,y)=\sin(xy)+x^2+y^4.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.6 The component test in the plane
text:
21.6 The component test in the plane
For a plane vector field
\[
F=\langle P,Q\rangle,
\]
if \(F=\nabla f\), then
\[
P=f_x,
\qquad
Q=f_y.
\]
If the second partial derivatives are continuous, then
\[
P_y=f_{xy}=f_{yx}=Q_x.
\]
Therefore, every conservative field satisfies
\[
P_y=Q_x.
\]
On a simply connected region, this condition is also sufficient.






WarningA necessary condition is not always sufficient



The equation \(P_y=Q_x\) is a local test. It guarantees conservativeness only when the domain has no holes and the field is defined throughout the region. Singularities and holes can create nonzero circulation even when \(P_y=Q_x\) away from the singularity.



21.6.1 Worked example: not conservative
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle.
\]
Here
\[
P=-y,
\qquad
Q=x.
\]
Then
\[
P_y=-1,
\qquad
Q_x=1.
\]
Since \(P_y\ne Q_x\), the field is not conservative.
This field rotates counterclockwise around the origin. Closed-loop integrals are generally nonzero.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 21)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 21)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)

U1, V1 = X, Y
U2, V2 = -Y, X

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.4))
for ax, U, V, title in [
    (axes[0], U1, V1, 'Conservative radial field'),
    (axes[1], U2, V2, 'Rotational field')
]:
    speed = np.hypot(U, V)
    ax.quiver(X, Y, U/(1+speed), V/(1+speed), angles='xy')
    theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 300)
    ax.plot(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta), linewidth=2)
    ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
    ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 21.4: A conservative radial field and a rotational field. The rotational field has circulation around closed curves.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.7 Closed curves and circulation
A curve \(C\) is closed if its endpoint is the same as its starting point. For a plane field
\[
F=\langle P,Q\rangle,
\]
the line integral around a closed curve is often written
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr
=
\oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy.
\]
This is called the circulation of the vector field around \(C\).
If \(F\) is conservative, then every closed-loop integral is zero:
\[
\oint_C \nabla f\cdot dr=0.
\]
If a closed-loop integral is not zero, then the field cannot be conservative.

21.7.1 Worked example: circulation around a circle
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle,
\]
and let \(C\) be the circle
\[
r(t)=\langle R\cos t,R\sin t\rangle,
\qquad
0\le t\le 2\pi.
\]
Then
\[
dr=\langle -R\sin t,R\cos t\rangle\,dt,
\]
and
\[
F(r(t))=\langle -R\sin t,R\cos t\rangle.
\]
Thus
\[
F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)=R^2\sin^2 t+R^2\cos^2 t=R^2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr=\int_0^{2\pi}R^2\,dt=2\pi R^2.
\]
The circulation is twice the area of the disk.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.8 Green’s theorem: circulation form
Green’s theorem is a two-dimensional result that converts circulation around a boundary curve into a double integral over the enclosed region.
Let \(D\) be a plane region with positively oriented boundary curve \(C\). Positive orientation means that the region stays on the left as the curve is traversed; for a simple outer boundary, this is counterclockwise orientation.






TipGreen’s Theorem: circulation form



Let
\[
F=\langle P,Q\rangle
\]
where \(P\) and \(Q\) have continuous first partial derivatives on a region containing \(D\). If \(C\) is the positively oriented boundary of \(D\), then
\[
\oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy
=
\iint_D \left(Q_x-P_y\right)\,dA.
\]


The quantity
\[
Q_x-P_y
\]
is the two-dimensional scalar curl of the field. Green’s theorem says:

Circulation around the boundary equals total curl inside the region.


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 500)
r = 1.0 + 0.18*np.cos(3*t) + 0.12*np.sin(5*t)
x = r*np.cos(t)
y = r*np.sin(t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.fill(x, y, alpha=0.22)
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=2)
for s in np.linspace(0.2, 5.8, 12):
    rs = 1.0 + 0.18*np.cos(3*s) + 0.12*np.sin(5*s)
    xs, ys = rs*np.cos(s), rs*np.sin(s)
    dxds = (-0.54*np.sin(3*s) + 0.60*np.cos(5*s))*np.cos(s) - rs*np.sin(s)
    dyds = (-0.54*np.sin(3*s) + 0.60*np.cos(5*s))*np.sin(s) + rs*np.cos(s)
    norm = np.hypot(dxds, dyds)
    ax.arrow(xs, ys, 0.12*dxds/norm, 0.12*dyds/norm,
             head_width=0.04, length_includes_head=True)
ax.text(0, 0, r'$D$', ha='center', va='center', fontsize=14)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Positive orientation: region on the left')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 21.5: Green’s theorem relates circulation around a positively oriented boundary to total curl over the enclosed region.






21.8.1 Worked example: use Green’s theorem on a rectangle
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle -y,x\rangle.
\]
Compute
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr
\]
where \(C\) is the positively oriented boundary of the rectangle
\[
0\le x\le a,
\qquad
0\le y\le b.
\]
Here
\[
P=-y,
\qquad
Q=x.
\]
Then
\[
Q_x-P_y=1-(-1)=2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\oint_C -y\,dx+x\,dy
=
\iint_D 2\,dA
=2ab.
\]
The answer is twice the area of the rectangle.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

a, b = 2.4, 1.3
N = 500

# Counterclockwise rectangle parameterized piecewise by sampled points.
x_bottom = np.linspace(0, a, N)
y_bottom = np.zeros(N)
x_right = a*np.ones(N)
y_right = np.linspace(0, b, N)
x_top = np.linspace(a, 0, N)
y_top = b*np.ones(N)
x_left = np.zeros(N)
y_left = np.linspace(b, 0, N)

x = np.concatenate([x_bottom, x_right, x_top, x_left])
y = np.concatenate([y_bottom, y_right, y_top, y_left])

dx = np.diff(x)
dy = np.diff(y)
x_mid = 0.5*(x[:-1] + x[1:])
y_mid = 0.5*(y[:-1] + y[1:])
P_mid = -y_mid
Q_mid = x_mid
line_approx = np.sum(P_mid*dx + Q_mid*dy)
green_value = 2*a*b

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=2)
ax.fill(x, y, alpha=0.18)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Rectangle boundary')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()

print(f'Boundary line integral approximation: {line_approx:.8f}')
print(f'Green theorem value:                 {green_value:.8f}')







Figure 21.6: Numerical verification of Green’s theorem for the field \(F=\langle -y,x\rangle\) on a rectangle.





Boundary line integral approximation: 6.24000000
Green theorem value:                 6.24000000

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.9 Area from a line integral
Green’s theorem can be used to compute area. If we choose \(P\) and \(Q\) so that
\[
Q_x-P_y=1,
\]
then
\[
\text{Area}(D)=\iint_D 1\,dA
=
\oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy.
\]
Three common choices are:
\[
P=0,
\quad
Q=x,
\qquad
\text{so}
\qquad
\text{Area}(D)=\oint_C x\,dy.
\]
\[
P=-y,
\quad
Q=0,
\qquad
\text{so}
\qquad
\text{Area}(D)=\oint_C -y\,dx.
\]
\[
P=-\frac{y}{2},
\quad
Q=\frac{x}{2},
\qquad
\text{so}
\qquad
\text{Area}(D)=\frac12\oint_C x\,dy-y\,dx.
\]
The last formula is especially symmetric.

21.9.1 Worked example: area of an ellipse
Let \(C\) be the ellipse
\[
r(t)=\langle a\cos t,b\sin t\rangle,
\qquad
0\le t\le 2\pi.
\]
Using
\[
\text{Area}(D)=\frac12\oint_C x\,dy-y\,dx,
\]
we have
\[
x=a\cos t,
\qquad
y=b\sin t,
\]
\[
dx=-a\sin t\,dt,
\qquad
dy=b\cos t\,dt.
\]
Thus
\[
x\,dy-y\,dx
=ab\cos^2t\,dt+ab\sin^2t\,dt
=ab\,dt.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\text{Area}(D)=\frac12\int_0^{2\pi}ab\,dt=\pi ab.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 1000)
a, b = 2.2, 1.1
x = a*np.cos(t)
y = b*np.sin(t)

dxdt = np.gradient(x, t)
dydt = np.gradient(y, t)
area_approx = 0.5*np.trapz(x*dydt - y*dxdt, t)
area_exact = np.pi*a*b

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y, linewidth=2)
ax.fill(x, y, alpha=0.2)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Area of an ellipse from a boundary integral')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()

print(f'Boundary-area approximation: {area_approx:.8f}')
print(f'Exact area:                  {area_exact:.8f}')







Figure 21.7: Area can be computed from boundary data alone using Green’s theorem.





Boundary-area approximation: 7.60260410
Exact area:                  7.60265422

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.10 Green’s theorem: flux form
Green’s theorem also has a flux form. Let
\[
F=\langle P,Q\rangle.
\]
The outward flux across \(C\) is
\[
\oint_C F\cdot n\,ds,
\]
where \(n\) is the outward unit normal. For a positively oriented plane curve, the outward normal satisfies
\[
n\,ds=\langle dy,-dx\rangle.
\]
Therefore,
\[
F\cdot n\,ds=P\,dy-Q\,dx.
\]
Green’s theorem in flux form is
\[
\oint_C F\cdot n\,ds
=
\iint_D (P_x+Q_y)\,dA.
\]
The quantity
\[
P_x+Q_y
\]
is the two-dimensional divergence of \(F\). Thus:

Outward flux through the boundary equals total divergence inside the region.

This result foreshadows the divergence theorem in three dimensions.

21.10.1 Worked example: outward flux of a radial field
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\langle x,y\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{div}F=P_x+Q_y=1+1=2.
\]
If \(C\) bounds a region \(D\), then
\[
\oint_C F\cdot n\,ds=\iint_D 2\,dA=2\operatorname{Area}(D).
\]
For a disk of radius \(R\), this gives
\[
\oint_C F\cdot n\,ds=2\pi R^2.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.11 Holes, singularities, and why hypotheses matter
text:
21.11 Holes, singularities, and why hypotheses matter
The field
\[
F(x,y)=\left\langle \frac{-y}{x^2+y^2},\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\right\rangle
\]
is defined everywhere except the origin. Away from the origin, one can check that
\[
P_y=Q_x.
\]
However, the field is not conservative on any region that loops around the origin. On the unit circle
\[
r(t)=\langle \cos t,\sin t\rangle,
\]
we have
\[
F(r(t))=\langle -\sin t,\cos t\rangle=r'(t),
\]
so
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr=\int_0^{2\pi}1\,dt=2\pi.
\]
The origin is a singularity. The domain has a hole. This example is a warning that local derivative tests do not always determine global behavior.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 31)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 31)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
R2 = X**2 + Y**2
U = np.zeros_like(X)
V = np.zeros_like(Y)
mask = R2 > 0.08
U[mask] = -Y[mask]/R2[mask]
V[mask] = X[mask]/R2[mask]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(X[mask], Y[mask], U[mask], V[mask], angles='xy', alpha=0.75)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 400)
ax.plot(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta), linewidth=2)
ax.scatter([0], [0], s=70)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Circulation around a singularity')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 21.8: A field with a singularity at the origin. The local curl is zero away from the origin, but the circulation around the origin is nonzero.






21.11.1 Numerical experiment: circulation is almost \(2\pi\)

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def singular_circulation(R, N=5000):
    t = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, N)
    x = R*np.cos(t)
    y = R*np.sin(t)
    dxdt = -R*np.sin(t)
    dydt = R*np.cos(t)
    P = -y/(x**2 + y**2)
    Q = x/(x**2 + y**2)
    return np.trapz(P*dxdt + Q*dydt, t)

radii = np.array([0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0])
values = np.array([singular_circulation(R) for R in radii])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(radii, values, marker='o')
ax.axhline(2*np.pi, linestyle='--', label=r'$2\pi$')
ax.set_xlabel('radius')
ax.set_ylabel('circulation')
ax.set_title('Circulation does not depend on radius')
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()

for R, val in zip(radii, values):
    print(f'R={R:.1f}, circulation={val:.8f}')







Figure 21.9: Numerical circulation around circles centered at the origin for a singular rotational field.





R=0.5, circulation=6.28318531
R=0.8, circulation=6.28318531
R=1.0, circulation=6.28318531
R=1.5, circulation=6.28318531
R=2.0, circulation=6.28318531

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.12 Numerical curl and discrete Green’s theorem intuition
text:
21.12 Numerical curl and discrete Green’s theorem intuition
Green’s theorem says that circulation around a boundary measures accumulated curl inside. In computational settings, one often estimates derivatives on a grid. The scalar curl of a plane vector field
\[
F=\langle P,Q\rangle
\]
is
\[
\operatorname{curl}_{2D}F=Q_x-P_y.
\]
A gradient field has zero scalar curl when second partial derivatives commute. A rotational field has visible nonzero scalar curl.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 90)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 90)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)

dx = x[1] - x[0]
dy = y[1] - y[0]

# Gradient field of phi = x^2 y + y^3.
P_grad = 2*X*Y
Q_grad = X**2 + 3*Y**2

# Rotational field.
P_rot = -Y
Q_rot = X

def scalar_curl(P, Q):
    Q_y_index, Q_x_index = np.gradient(Q, dy, dx)
    P_y_index, P_x_index = np.gradient(P, dy, dx)
    return Q_x_index - P_y_index

curl_grad = scalar_curl(P_grad, Q_grad)
curl_rot = scalar_curl(P_rot, Q_rot)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.2))
for ax, C, title in [
    (axes[0], curl_grad, 'Numerical curl of a gradient field'),
    (axes[1], curl_rot, 'Numerical curl of a rotational field')
]:
    im = ax.imshow(C, extent=[x.min(), x.max(), y.min(), y.max()], origin='lower', aspect='equal')
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
    ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
    fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.82)
plt.show()







Figure 21.10: Numerical scalar curl for a gradient field and a rotational field. Gradient fields have near-zero curl; rotational fields do not.

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
section: 21.13 Modern interpretation: potentials, losses, and dynamics
text:
21.13 Modern interpretation: potentials, losses, and dynamics
In applied mathematics, statistics, data science, and machine learning, a scalar objective function is often the central object:
\[
L(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n).
\]
The gradient field
\[
\nabla L
\]
points in the direction of steepest increase, while
\[
-\nabla L
\]
points in the direction of steepest decrease. Gradient descent follows a trajectory determined by a conservative field:
\[
\frac{d\theta}{dt}=-\nabla L(\theta).
\]
Along such a path,
\[
\frac{d}{dt}L(\theta(t))=\nabla L(\theta(t))\cdot \theta'(t)
=-|\nabla L(\theta(t))|^2\le 0.
\]
So a pure gradient flow cannot cycle around a closed loop while strictly decreasing the loss. In contrast, a rotational component can create circulation and oscillation. This distinction matters in dynamical systems, adversarial optimization, non-equilibrium probability flows, and training dynamics for models with coupled parameters.






NoteConservative versus rotational learning dynamics



A pure gradient field comes from a scalar objective. A rotational field does not. Many modern algorithms can be viewed as combining gradient-like motion with rotational or momentum-like effects. Green’s theorem gives a mathematical language for detecting and measuring such circulation in two-dimensional slices of a high-dimensional system.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.14 Problem-solving guide
When faced with a line integral of a plane vector field:

Check whether the field is conservative. Try to find a potential function.
If the curve is open and the field is conservative, use endpoints.
If the curve is closed, check Green’s theorem. The boundary integral may become an easier double integral.
Check orientation. Counterclockwise outer boundaries are positive. Reversing orientation changes the sign.
Check the domain. Holes or singularities can invalidate a simple conservative-field conclusion.
Choose the easier representation. Some problems are easier as line integrals; others are easier as double integrals.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.15 Common mistakes






WarningWatch for these errors




Treating \(P_y=Q_x\) as sufficient without checking the domain.
Forgetting that Green’s theorem requires a closed boundary curve.
Using the wrong orientation and missing a minus sign.
Confusing circulation form with flux form.
Forgetting that a potential function is determined only up to an additive constant.
Applying Green’s theorem across a singularity where the field is not defined.

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.16 Exercises

21.16.1 Conceptual exercises

Explain in your own words what it means for a vector field to be conservative.
Why does a gradient field have zero line integral around every closed curve?
What is the difference between path independence and parameterization independence?
What does Green’s theorem say geometrically?
Why does a hole in the domain matter for the conservative-field test?
Explain why the field \(F=\langle -y,x\rangle\) should have nonzero circulation around the unit circle.



21.16.2 Computational exercises

Find a potential function for
\[
F(x,y)=\langle 3x^2y+2, x^3+4y\rangle.
\]
Evaluate \(\int_C F\cdot dr\) from \((0,0)\) to \((1,2)\) for the field in Exercise 7.
Determine whether
\[
F(x,y)=\langle y^2+e^x,2xy+\cos y\rangle
\]
is conservative. If it is, find a potential function.
Compute
\[
\oint_C -y\,dx+x\,dy
\]
where \(C\) is the positively oriented boundary of the triangle with vertices \((0,0)\), \((2,0)\), and \((0,3)\).
Use Green’s theorem to compute
\[
\oint_C (x^2-y)\,dx+(x+y^2)\,dy
\]
where \(C\) is the positively oriented boundary of the rectangle \(0\le x\le 2\), \(0\le y\le 1\).
Use a boundary integral to find the area enclosed by the parametric curve
\[
r(t)=\langle 3\cos t,2\sin t\rangle,
\qquad 0\le t\le 2\pi.
\]
Let
\[
F(x,y)=\left\langle \frac{-y}{x^2+y^2},\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\right\rangle.
\]
Compute the line integral around the circle of radius \(3\) centered at the origin. Explain why the answer does not contradict the local equality \(P_y=Q_x\) away from the origin.



21.16.3 Applied and computational exercises

Let
\[
L(x,y)=x^2+4y^2.
\]
Compute the work done by \(\nabla L\) along any curve from \((1,1)\) to \((2,0)\).
For the vector field \(F=\langle -y,x\rangle\), numerically approximate the circulation around the ellipse \(x=2\cos t\), \(y=\sin t\). Compare your answer with Green’s theorem.
Let \(F=\langle P,Q\rangle\) be sampled on a grid. Describe how finite differences could be used to estimate whether \(F\) is approximately conservative.
In a learning problem, suppose parameter dynamics show persistent closed-loop motion in a two-dimensional slice of parameter space. Explain why this suggests that the dynamics are not simply pure gradient descent on a scalar loss.
Use Green’s theorem to derive the symmetric area formula
\[
\text{Area}(D)=\frac12\oint_C x\,dy-y\,dx.
\]

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green’s Theorem
text:
21.17 Chapter summary
This chapter developed the bridge from line integrals to global vector-calculus theorems.

A vector field is conservative if it is the gradient of a potential function.
Line integrals of conservative fields depend only on endpoints.
The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals is the chain rule plus the one-variable Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Closed-loop integrals of conservative fields are zero.
In the plane, the condition \(P_y=Q_x\) is the local conservative-field test, but the domain matters.
Green’s theorem converts circulation around a closed curve into a double integral of scalar curl over the enclosed region.
Green’s theorem also has a flux form involving divergence.
Area can be computed from a boundary integral.
Singularities and holes explain why local derivative information may not determine global circulation.

In the next chapter, these ideas move from plane regions to surfaces in three-dimensional space. Curl, divergence, surface integrals, and flux are the ingredients needed for Stokes’ theorem and the divergence theorem.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
21  Chapter 21. Conservative Fields, Potentials, and Green's Theorem

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.1 Learning goals
The previous chapters developed line integrals and Green’s theorem. A line integral measures how a scalar or vector field accumulates along a curve. Green’s theorem then showed that circulation or flux around a closed plane curve can be computed by integrating a local derivative over the enclosed region.
This chapter moves that idea into three-dimensional space. Instead of curves enclosing planar regions, we now study surfaces in space. Instead of asking only how much a vector field pushes along a path, we ask how much it rotates near a point, how much it expands out of a point, and how much it flows through a surface.
The central objects are:
These concepts are essential for vector calculus, physics, partial differential equations, probability flow, optimization dynamics, and geometric data analysis. They also prepare the way for Stokes’ theorem and the divergence theorem in the next chapter.
After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.2 The differential operator \(\nabla\)
For a scalar function \(f(x,y,z)\), the gradient is
\[
\nabla f=\left\langle f_x,f_y,f_z\right\rangle.
\]
For a vector field
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle P(x,y,z),Q(x,y,z),R(x,y,z)\rangle,
\]
we use the formal differential operator
\[
\nabla=\left\langle \frac{\partial}{\partial x},\frac{\partial}{\partial y},\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\right\rangle.
\]
This notation is powerful because it lets us remember several vector-calculus operations using familiar vector algebra:
\[
\nabla f \quad \text{gradient},
\]
\[
\nabla\cdot F \quad \text{divergence},
\]
\[
\nabla\times F \quad \text{curl}.
\]
The notation is formal: \(\nabla\) is not an ordinary vector of numbers. Its components are derivative operators. But the dot-product and cross-product notation correctly organizes the formulas.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.3 Curl
Let
\[
F=\langle P,Q,R\rangle
\]
be a vector field in space. The curl of \(F\) is the vector field
\[
\nabla\times F
=
\left\langle
R_y-Q_z,
P_z-R_x,
Q_x-P_y
\right\rangle.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\nabla\times F
=
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf i & \mathbf j & \mathbf k\\
\frac{\partial}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\\
P & Q & R
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
Curl measures the infinitesimal tendency of the field to rotate. Its direction is the axis of rotation determined by the right-hand rule, and its magnitude measures the local strength of the rotation.






ImportantCurl in the plane



A two-dimensional vector field
\[
F(x,y)=\langle P(x,y),Q(x,y)\rangle
\]
can be treated as the three-dimensional field \(\langle P,Q,0\rangle\). Then
\[
\nabla\times F
=
\left\langle 0,0,Q_x-P_y\right\rangle.
\]
The scalar quantity
\[
Q_x-P_y
\]
is often called the scalar curl of the plane field. It is exactly the integrand that appears in Green’s theorem in circulation form.



22.3.1 Example: a rotating field
Let
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle -y,x,0\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
P=-y,
\qquad
Q=x,
\qquad
R=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\nabla\times F
=\left\langle
0-0,
0-0,
1-(-1)
\right\rangle
=\langle 0,0,2\rangle.
\]
The field rotates counterclockwise in the \(xy\)-plane. The curl points in the positive \(z\)-direction.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 21)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 21)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
U = -Y
V = X
speed = np.sqrt(U**2 + V**2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.quiver(X, Y, U, V, speed, angles='xy')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(r'Rotational field $F=\langle -y,x\rangle$')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 22.1: The field \(F=\langle -y,x\rangle\) rotates counterclockwise. Its curl points in the positive \(z\)-direction.







22.3.2 Example: curl of a three-dimensional field
Let
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle yz,xz,xy+e^z\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
P=yz,
\qquad
Q=xz,
\qquad
R=xy+e^z.
\]
Compute:
\[
R_y=x,
\qquad
Q_z=x,
\]
\[
P_z=y,
\qquad
R_x=y,
\]
\[
Q_x=z,
\qquad
P_y=z.
\]
Hence
\[
\nabla\times F=\langle x-x,y-y,z-z\rangle=\langle 0,0,0\rangle.
\]
This zero curl suggests that the field may be conservative on a suitable simply connected domain. In fact,
\[
F=\nabla f,
\qquad
f(x,y,z)=xyz+e^z.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.4 Divergence
Let
\[
F=\langle P,Q,R\rangle.
\]
The divergence of \(F\) is the scalar field
\[
\nabla\cdot F
=
P_x+Q_y+R_z.
\]
Divergence measures the local tendency of a vector field to flow outward from a point. Positive divergence indicates local source-like behavior. Negative divergence indicates local sink-like behavior. Zero divergence indicates local incompressibility.






ImportantDivergence is a scalar field



Curl returns a vector field in three dimensions. Divergence returns a scalar field:
\[
\nabla\times F \in \mathbb R^3,
\qquad
\nabla\cdot F \in \mathbb R.
\]



22.4.1 Example: a source field
Let
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle x,y,z\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
\nabla\cdot F=1+1+1=3.
\]
Everywhere, the field points away from the origin. A small ball centered at any point experiences net outward flow.


22.4.2 Example: an incompressible rotational field
For
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle -y,x,0\rangle,
\]
we get
\[
\nabla\cdot F=0+0+0=0.
\]
The field rotates, but it does not locally expand or compress area. It has curl but no divergence.

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 17)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 17)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

U1, V1 = X, Y
axes[0].quiver(X, Y, U1, V1, angles='xy')
axes[0].set_title(r'Source field $\langle x,y\rangle$')
axes[0].set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.25)

U2, V2 = -Y, X
axes[1].quiver(X, Y, U2, V2, angles='xy')
axes[1].set_title(r'Rotational field $\langle -y,x\rangle$')
axes[1].set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.25)

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
    ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 22.2: A radial source field has positive divergence; a rotational field can have zero divergence but nonzero curl.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.5 Two fundamental identities
For sufficiently smooth functions and fields, two identities occur throughout vector calculus.
First, the curl of a gradient is zero:
\[
\nabla\times(\nabla f)=0.
\]
This says that gradient fields have no local rotation. It is the three-dimensional version of the conservative-field test.
Second, the divergence of a curl is zero:
\[
\nabla\cdot(\nabla\times F)=0.
\]
This says that a curl field has no local source or sink.






WarningSmoothness and domain matter



The identities above require enough differentiability. Also, equations such as \(\nabla\times F=0\) guarantee a global potential only under suitable assumptions on the domain. Holes and singularities can create global circulation even when local curl is zero away from the singularity.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
section: 22.6 Numerical curl and divergence on a grid
text:
22.6 Numerical curl and divergence on a grid
In applications, a vector field may be sampled rather than given by a formula. For example, a velocity field may come from fluid simulation, a weather model, image motion, or a learned dynamical system. We can approximate divergence and curl by finite differences.
For a plane field \(F=\langle P,Q\rangle\), approximate
\[
\nabla\cdot F=P_x+Q_y,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{curl}_z F=Q_x-P_y.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 80)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 80)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
P = -Y + 0.35*X
Q = X - 0.2*Y

dx = x[1] - x[0]
dy = y[1] - y[0]
P_y, P_x = np.gradient(P, dy, dx)
Q_y, Q_x = np.gradient(Q, dy, dx)
Div = P_x + Q_y
Curl = Q_x - P_y

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
cf0 = axes[0].contourf(X, Y, Div, levels=20)
axes[0].set_title(r'Approximate divergence')
axes[0].set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
fig.colorbar(cf0, ax=axes[0])

cf1 = axes[1].contourf(X, Y, Curl, levels=20)
axes[1].set_title(r'Approximate scalar curl')
axes[1].set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
fig.colorbar(cf1, ax=axes[1])

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
    ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 22.3: Finite differences approximate divergence and scalar curl on a grid.





This example is deliberately simple: the field has nearly constant divergence and curl. In real data, the plots may reveal sources, sinks, vortices, and boundaries.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.7 Surfaces and parameterizations
A curve is described by one parameter:
\[
r(t)=\langle x(t),y(t),z(t)\rangle.
\]
A surface is described by two parameters:
\[
r(u,v)=\langle x(u,v),y(u,v),z(u,v)\rangle,
\qquad
(u,v)\in D.
\]
The parameter domain \(D\) lies in the \(uv\)-plane. The map \(r\) bends and stretches this flat parameter region into a surface \(S\) in space.
For a parameterized surface, the tangent vectors are
\[
r_u=\frac{\partial r}{\partial u},
\qquad
r_v=\frac{\partial r}{\partial v}.
\]
The cross product
\[
r_u\times r_v
\]
is normal to the surface. Its magnitude gives the local area scaling factor:
\[
dS=|r_u\times r_v|\,du\,dv.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

u = np.linspace(-1.5, 1.5, 35)
v = np.linspace(-1.5, 1.5, 35)
U, V = np.meshgrid(u, v)
X = U
Y = V
Z = 0.35*(U**2 + V**2)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, alpha=0.75, linewidth=0)

# normals for z = g(x,y): <-g_x, -g_y, 1>
pts = np.linspace(-1.2, 1.2, 5)
PX, PY = np.meshgrid(pts, pts)
PZ = 0.35*(PX**2 + PY**2)
NX = -0.7*PX
NY = -0.7*PY
NZ = np.ones_like(PX)
length = np.sqrt(NX**2 + NY**2 + NZ**2)
NX, NY, NZ = NX/length, NY/length, NZ/length
ax.quiver(PX, PY, PZ, NX, NY, NZ, length=0.35)

ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_zlabel(r'$z$')
ax.set_title('Surface normals on a paraboloid')
plt.show()







Figure 22.4: For a parameterized surface, the cross product \(r_u\times r_v\) gives a normal direction and the local area scale.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.8 Surface area
The area of a parameterized surface
\[
r(u,v),
\qquad
(u,v)\in D,
\]
is
\[
A(S)=\iint_D |r_u\times r_v|\,dA.
\]
This formula is the surface analog of arc length. For curves, \(|r'(t)|\) measures how much the parameter stretches length. For surfaces, \(|r_u\times r_v|\) measures how much the two-parameter map stretches area.

22.8.1 Graph surfaces
If a surface is a graph
\[
z=g(x,y),
\qquad
(x,y)\in D,
\]
we can parameterize it as
\[
r(x,y)=\langle x,y,g(x,y)\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
r_x=\langle 1,0,g_x\rangle,
\qquad
r_y=\langle 0,1,g_y\rangle,
\]
so
\[
r_x\times r_y=\langle -g_x,-g_y,1\rangle.
\]
Therefore
\[
dS=\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}\,dA,
\]
and
\[
A(S)=\iint_D \sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}\,dA.
\]


22.8.2 Worked example: area of a plane patch
Find the area of the part of the plane
\[
z=8+2x+5y
\]
above the rectangle
\[
0\le x\le 3,
\qquad
1\le y\le 6.
\]
Here
\[
g_x=2,
\qquad
g_y=5.
\]
Thus
\[
\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}
=\sqrt{1+4+25}
=\sqrt{30}.
\]
The rectangle has area \(15\), so
\[
A(S)=15\sqrt{30}.
\]
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.9 Scalar surface integrals
If \(f(x,y,z)\) is a scalar function defined on a surface \(S\), then the scalar surface integral is
\[
\iint_S f(x,y,z)\,dS.
\]
For a parameterized surface \(r(u,v)\),
\[
\iint_S f\,dS
=
\iint_D f(r(u,v))|r_u\times r_v|\,du\,dv.
\]
If \(f\) is a surface density, then this integral gives the total mass of the surface. If \(f=1\), the integral gives surface area.

22.9.1 Worked example: surface integral over a paraboloid
Let \(S\) be the graph
\[
z=x^2+y^2
\]
over the disk \(x^2+y^2\le 1\). Compute
\[
\iint_S z\,dS.
\]
Since \(g(x,y)=x^2+y^2\), we have
\[
g_x=2x,
\qquad
g_y=2y,
\]
so
\[
dS=\sqrt{1+4x^2+4y^2}\,dA.
\]
The integral becomes
\[
\iint_D (x^2+y^2)\sqrt{1+4x^2+4y^2}\,dA.
\]
In polar coordinates,
\[
\iint_S z\,dS
=
\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^1 r^2\sqrt{1+4r^2}\,r\,dr\,d\theta.
\]
That is
\[
2\pi\int_0^1 r^3\sqrt{1+4r^2}\,dr.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.linspace(0, 1, 80)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 120)
R, T = np.meshgrid(r, theta)
X = R*np.cos(T)
Y = R*np.sin(T)
Z = R**2

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
surf = ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z, cmap='viridis', linewidth=0, alpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_zlabel(r'$z$')
ax.set_title(r'Paraboloid $z=x^2+y^2$ over the unit disk')
fig.colorbar(surf, ax=ax, shrink=0.65, label=r'$z$')
plt.show()







Figure 22.5: A scalar surface integral accumulates density over a curved surface. Here the density is \(f(x,y,z)=z\) on a paraboloid.
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.10 Orientation of surfaces
A surface is orientable if it is possible to choose a continuous unit normal vector \(n\) at every point of the surface. Choosing one of the two possible normal directions gives an orientation.
For a parameterized surface \(r(u,v)\), the vector
\[
r_u\times r_v
\]
defines one orientation. Reversing the parameter order changes the normal direction:
\[
r_v\times r_u=-(r_u\times r_v).
\]
For a closed surface, the standard orientation is outward. For an open surface, the orientation must be specified, for example upward, downward, outward, or inward.






WarningOrientation affects flux



Scalar surface integrals do not depend on orientation because \(dS\) is positive. Flux integrals do depend on orientation because changing \(n\) to \(-n\) changes the sign of \(F\cdot n\).
crumbs: Book
Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.11 Flux integrals
Let \(F\) be a vector field and let \(S\) be an oriented surface with unit normal vector \(n\). The flux of \(F\) through \(S\) is
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS.
\]
This measures the net amount of the field passing through the surface in the chosen normal direction.
Using a parameterization \(r(u,v)\), we have
\[
n\,dS=(r_u\times r_v)\,du\,dv
\]
for the orientation determined by \(r_u\times r_v\). Therefore
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS
=
\iint_D F(r(u,v))\cdot(r_u\times r_v)\,du\,dv.
\]
This formula is one of the most important computational tools in vector calculus.

22.11.1 Graph formula for flux
Suppose
\[
S: z=g(x,y),
\qquad
(x,y)\in D,
\]
with upward orientation. Then
\[
r_x\times r_y=\langle -g_x,-g_y,1\rangle.
\]
If
\[
F=\langle P,Q,R\rangle,
\]
then
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS
=
\iint_D \left(-P g_x-Q g_y+R\right)dA,
\]
where \(P,Q,R\) are evaluated at \((x,y,g(x,y))\).
For downward orientation, the sign changes.


22.11.2 Worked example: flux through a graph
Let
\[
F(x,y,z)=\langle x,y,z\rangle,
\]
and let \(S\) be the upper paraboloid
\[
z=1-x^2-y^2,
\qquad
x^2+y^2\le 1,
\]
with upward orientation.
Here
\[
g_x=-2x,
\qquad
g_y=-2y.
\]
The upward flux integrand is
\[
-Pg_x-Qg_y+R
= -x(-2x)-y(-2y)+(1-x^2-y^2).
\]
Thus
\[
-Pg_x-Qg_y+R
=1+x^2+y^2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS
=\iint_D (1+x^2+y^2)\,dA.
\]
In polar coordinates,
\[
\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^1 (1+r^2)r\,dr\,d\theta
=2\pi\left[\frac{r^2}{2}+\frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^1
=\frac{3\pi}{2}.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.linspace(0, 1, 120)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 240)
R, T = np.meshgrid(r, theta)
X = R*np.cos(T)
Y = R*np.sin(T)
Z = 1 - R**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))
cf = ax.contourf(X, Y, Z, levels=20)
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=ax, label=r'$z=1-x^2-y^2$')

pts = np.linspace(-0.8, 0.8, 7)
PX, PY = np.meshgrid(pts, pts)
mask = PX**2 + PY**2 <= 1
PX = PX[mask]
PY = PY[mask]
# Upward normal for z=1-x^2-y^2 is <2x,2y,1>.
# We draw its horizontal projection <2x,2y>.
ax.quiver(PX, PY, 2*PX, 2*PY, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=8)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Projected upward normals on the paraboloid')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 22.6: For the upward-oriented graph \(z=1-x^2-y^2\), projected normal arrows in the \(xy\)-plane point outward while the normal has positive \(z\)-component.

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.12 Flux through standard surfaces
For standard surfaces, it is often better to use geometry than algebra.

22.12.1 Sphere
The sphere of radius \(a\) centered at the origin can be parameterized by
\[
r(\phi,\theta)
=\langle a\sin\phi\cos\theta,
a\sin\phi\sin\theta,
a\cos\phi\rangle,
\]
where
\[
0\le \phi\le \pi,
\qquad
0\le \theta\le 2\pi.
\]
The outward unit normal is
\[
n=\frac{1}{a}\langle x,y,z\rangle.
\]
If \(F=\langle x,y,z\rangle\), then on the sphere,
\[
F\cdot n
=\langle x,y,z\rangle\cdot \frac{1}{a}\langle x,y,z\rangle
=\frac{x^2+y^2+z^2}{a}
=a.
\]
Since the surface area is \(4\pi a^2\), the flux is
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS
=a(4\pi a^2)=4\pi a^3.
\]
For the sphere \(x^2+y^2+z^2=4\), we have \(a=2\), so the flux is
\[
32\pi.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

a = 2.0
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 300)
xc = a*np.cos(theta)
yc = a*np.sin(theta)

sample = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 16, endpoint=False)
xs = a*np.cos(sample)
ys = a*np.sin(sample)
ux = np.cos(sample)
uy = np.sin(sample)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
ax.plot(xc, yc)
ax.quiver(xs, ys, ux, uy, angles='xy', scale_units='xy', scale=4)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title('Radial outward normals on a sphere: cross-section view')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 22.7: For a sphere centered at the origin, outward normals are radial. The cross-section shows the same orientation idea.







22.12.2 Cylinder
A circular cylinder of radius \(a\) can be parameterized by
\[
r(\theta,z)=\langle a\cos\theta,a\sin\theta,z\rangle.
\]
Then
\[
r_\theta=\langle -a\sin\theta,a\cos\theta,0\rangle,
\qquad
r_z=\langle 0,0,1\rangle.
\]
The cross product
\[
r_z\times r_\theta=\langle -a\cos\theta,-a\sin\theta,0\rangle
\]
points inward, while
\[
r_\theta\times r_z=\langle a\cos\theta,a\sin\theta,0\rangle
\]
points outward. Always check orientation.

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.13 Numerical surface integration
For computational work, a parameterized surface integral is usually approximated by sampling a grid in parameter space. If
\[
r(u,v),
\qquad
(u,v)\in D,
\]
then
\[
\iint_S f\,dS
\approx
\sum_{i,j} f(r(u_i,v_j))|r_u(u_i,v_j)\times r_v(u_i,v_j)|\Delta u\Delta v.
\]
Similarly,
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS
\approx
\sum_{i,j} F(r(u_i,v_j))\cdot(r_u(u_i,v_j)\times r_v(u_i,v_j))\Delta u\Delta v.
\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Approximate surface area of z = x^2 + y^2 over [-1,1] x [-1,1]
n = 250
x = np.linspace(-1, 1, n)
y = np.linspace(-1, 1, n)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
g_x = 2*X
g_y = 2*Y
area_density = np.sqrt(1 + g_x**2 + g_y**2)
dx = x[1] - x[0]
dy = y[1] - y[0]
area_est = area_density.sum()*dx*dy

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
cs = ax.contourf(X, Y, area_density, levels=25)
fig.colorbar(cs, ax=ax, label=r'$\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}$')
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$x$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$y$')
ax.set_title(f'Area-density plot; estimated area = {area_est:.4f}')
plt.show()







Figure 22.8: Numerical surface integration samples the parameter domain and multiplies by the local area scale.





The code computes an approximate surface area by summing the area-density factor over a grid. This is the same Riemann-sum idea that underlies all definite integrals, now applied to surfaces.

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
section: 22.14 Curl, divergence, and flux in applied modeling
text:
22.14 Curl, divergence, and flux in applied modeling

22.14.1 Fluid velocity fields
If \(F\) is a fluid velocity field, then:

\(\nabla\times F\) measures local swirling;
\(\nabla\cdot F\) measures local expansion or compression;
\(\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS\) measures the rate of flow through a surface.

These ideas appear in incompressible flow, fluid simulation, weather models, and diffusion processes.


22.14.2 Probability flow
In probability and statistics, a time-dependent density can move under a velocity field. Divergence helps describe whether probability mass is locally spreading or concentrating. In continuous normalizing flows and score-based generative modeling, vector fields and Jacobians are central tools.


22.14.3 Optimization and machine learning
Gradient descent is driven by a vector field \(-\nabla L\). Such a field has zero curl when \(L\) is smooth. More general training dynamics may include momentum, stochastic noise, constraints, or game-theoretic interactions. These dynamics can show rotational behavior, which is naturally detected by curl-like measurements.


22.14.4 Geometry and data
Surface integrals appear when data lie on curved manifolds rather than flat Euclidean spaces. Area elements and Jacobian factors explain why densities change under parameterization and why sampling uniformly in parameter coordinates does not always sample uniformly on a surface.

title: 22  Chapter 22. Curl, Divergence, and Surface Integrals
text:
22.15 Common mistakes

Forgetting that curl is a vector in space. In three dimensions, \(\nabla\times F\) has three components.
Confusing scalar curl with full curl. In the plane, \(Q_x-P_y\) is the \(z\)-component of the three-dimensional curl of \(\langle P,Q,0\rangle\).
Forgetting the area factor. A surface integral is not usually just an ordinary double integral over the parameter domain. The factor \(|r_u\times r_v|\) is essential.
Using the wrong orientation. Flux changes sign when the normal direction is reversed.
Using graph formulas outside their setting. The formula for \(z=g(x,y)\) applies only when the surface is represented as a graph over the \(xy\)-plane.
Treating \(dS\) like \(dA\). For flat horizontal surfaces, \(dS=dA\). For curved surfaces, \(dS\) includes an area-stretching factor.

Part IV. Vector Calculus, Fields, and Global Theorems
