Partial differential equations
gradient, divergence, Laplacian, flux, boundary conditions


Geometry and topology
curves, surfaces, orientation, Stokes-type theorems



The most important preparation is not memorizing every formula. It is learning how to translate a new problem into the correct mathematical object.
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation
27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study

title: 27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study
section: 27.13 27.12 Capstone project: build a calculus engine
text:
27.13 27.12 Capstone project: build a calculus engine
A strong final project for this book is to build a small Python package or notebook called calculus_engine. It should include the following functions:

finite_gradient(f, x)
finite_jacobian(F, x)
finite_hessian(f, x)
integrate_rectangle(f, xlim, ylim)
monte_carlo_integral(f, sampler)
line_integral(F, r, a, b)
surface_flux(F, r, u_range, v_range)
gradient_descent(f, grad, x0, alpha)
newton_step(f, grad, hessian, x)
plot_contours(f)

The project should include examples from probability, optimization, and vector calculus. It should also include written interpretations of each output.

27.13.1 Example project theme: one dataset, many calculus ideas
Choose a simple dataset with input-output pairs \((x_i,y_i)\). Fit a linear regression model
\[
\hat y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i.
\]
Then study the least-squares loss
\[
L(\beta_0,\beta_1)=\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2.
\]
This one example uses:

vectors for data;
a scalar field for the loss landscape;
gradients for optimization;
Hessians for curvature;
contour plots for visualization;
linear approximation for sensitivity;
probability if the errors are modeled as random noise.


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(27)
x_data = np.linspace(-2, 2, 40)
y_data = 1.0 + 1.8*x_data + rng.normal(scale=0.35, size=x_data.shape)

b0 = np.linspace(-1.0, 3.0, 180)
b1 = np.linspace(0.0, 3.5, 180)
B0, B1 = np.meshgrid(b0, b1)
Loss = np.zeros_like(B0)
for xi, yi in zip(x_data, y_data):
    Loss += (yi - B0 - B1*xi)**2

Xmat = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(x_data), x_data])
beta_hat = np.linalg.solve(Xmat.T @ Xmat, Xmat.T @ y_data)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))
cs = ax.contour(B0, B1, Loss, levels=24)
ax.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=7)
ax.plot(beta_hat[0], beta_hat[1], marker='o', markersize=8)
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\beta_0$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\beta_1$')
ax.set_title('Least-squares loss and fitted parameters')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.25)
plt.show()







Figure 27.12: A single least-squares loss landscape connects vectors, gradients, Hessians, optimization, and statistics.
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation
27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study

title: 27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study
text:
27.14 27.13 Common mistakes to avoid

Confusing points and vectors. A point gives location; a vector gives displacement. Coordinates can represent either, but the meaning differs.
Forgetting that multivariable limits require all paths. Checking one path is not enough to prove a limit exists.
Using the gradient without normalizing the direction. Directional derivatives use unit vectors.
Forgetting boundary cases in optimization. Critical points inside a region are not enough for absolute extrema.
Missing the Jacobian factor. Coordinate changes require area or volume scaling.
Using the wrong orientation. Line and surface integrals over oriented objects depend on direction.
Applying Green, Stokes, or Divergence theorem without checking hypotheses. Holes, singularities, and missing boundaries matter.
Treating numerical output as exact. Computation supports reasoning but does not replace it.
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation
27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study

title: 27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study
text:
27.15 27.14 Cumulative worked example
Consider the scalar field
\[
f(x,y)=x^2+xy+2y^2.
\]
Gradient.
\[
\nabla f(x,y)=(2x+y, x+4y).
\]
At \((1,-1)\),
\[
\nabla f(1,-1)=(1,-3).
\]
Directional derivative. In the unit direction
\[
u=\frac{1}{\sqrt 5}(1,2),
\]
we have
\[
D_u f(1,-1)=(1,-3)\cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt 5}(1,2)=\frac{-5}{\sqrt 5}=-\sqrt 5.
\]
Hessian.
\[
H_f=
\begin{bmatrix}
2&1\\
1&4
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
The Hessian is positive definite because its leading principal minors are positive. Therefore \(f\) is strictly convex and has a unique global minimum where \(\nabla f=0\), namely \((0,0)\).
Integral. The average value over the rectangle \([-1,1]\times[-1,1]\) is
\[
\frac{1}{4}\int_{-1}^1\int_{-1}^1 (x^2+xy+2y^2)\,dy\,dx.
\]
By symmetry, the \(xy\) term integrates to zero. The average is
\[
\frac14\left(\frac{4}{3}+\frac{8}{3}\right)=1.
\]
This one example uses gradients, directional derivatives, Hessians, optimization, symmetry, and double integrals.
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation
27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study

title: 27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study
text:
27.16 27.15 Practice problems

27.16.1 Conceptual review

Explain the difference between a scalar field and a vector field. Give one example of each.
Explain why the gradient is perpendicular to level curves.
Explain the difference between a double integral over a region and a line integral over a curve.
Explain why the Jacobian determinant appears in change of variables.
Compare Green’s theorem, Stokes theorem, and the Divergence theorem in one paragraph.



27.16.2 Core skills

Let \(f(x,y)=x^2+3xy+y^2\). Compute \(\nabla f\), \(H_f\), and the directional derivative at \((1,2)\) in the direction \((3,4)\).
Find the tangent plane to \(z=x^2+y^2\) at \((1,2,5)\).
Compute \(\iint_R xy\,dA\), where \(R=[0,2]\times[1,3]\).
Change the integral \(\int_0^1\int_y^1 e^{x^2}\,dx\,dy\) to the opposite order.
Use polar coordinates to evaluate \(\iint_R (x^2+y^2)\,dA\), where \(R\) is the disk \(x^2+y^2\le 4\).



27.16.3 Vector calculus

For \(F(x,y)=(-y,x)\), compute \(\oint_C F\cdot dr\) where \(C\) is the unit circle oriented counterclockwise.
For \(F(x,y,z)=(x,y,z)\), compute the flux through the sphere \(x^2+y^2+z^2=a^2\) using the Divergence theorem.
Find a potential function for \(F(x,y)=(2xy, x^2+3y^2)\) if one exists.
Compute \(\nabla\cdot F\) and \(\nabla\times F\) for \(F(x,y,z)=(yz,xz,xy)\).



27.16.4 Statistics, data science, and applied mathematics

Let \(L(\beta_0,\beta_1)=\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1x_i)^2\). Derive the gradient of \(L\).
Let \(f(x,y)=ce^{-(x^2+y^2)}\). Find \(c\) so that \(f\) is a probability density on \(\mathbb R^2\).
For a differentiable loss function \(L(\theta)\), explain how the Hessian near a minimum is related to curvature and uncertainty.
Write pseudocode for gradient descent and explain the role of the step size.
Explain why a conservation law naturally involves divergence.
Design one computational experiment that illustrates a major theorem from vector calculus.
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation
27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study

title: 27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study
text:
27.17 27.16 Python lab and interactive page

Notebook lab: Chapter27_Review_Synthesis_and_Bridge_to_Graduate_Study.ipynb
Interactive HTML page: Chapter 27 interactive page
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation
27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study

title: 27  Review, Synthesis, and Bridge to Graduate Study
text:
27.18 Chapter summary
This chapter organized multivariable calculus as a coherent language for modern quantitative work. Geometry describes the spaces and objects. Derivatives describe local change. Integrals describe accumulation. Optimization turns derivatives into algorithms. Vector calculus connects local field behavior with global boundary behavior. Probability and statistics reinterpret integrals as probabilities and expectations, and machine learning reinterprets gradients and Hessians as tools for fitting models.
The deepest lesson is that the same mathematical structures appear across many subjects. A tangent plane, a linear approximation, a least-squares projection, a gradient descent step, a probability density, a flux integral, and a conservation law are not isolated facts. They are connected parts of one multivariable calculus language.
crumbs: Book
Part V. Modern Applications for MS Preparation

Do not treat the labs as code to copy mechanically. For each notebook, focus on three questions:

30 Interactive HTML Pages
This page collects the standalone interactive HTML pages for the book. Each page is designed as a short visual activity that supports the corresponding chapter. Students can use these pages before class for intuition, during class for guided exploration, or after class for review.
The pages live in the htmls/ folder of the repository. When the book is published through GitHub Pages, the links below open the corresponding interactive page directly from the book site. When the book is rendered locally, the same links open the local files in the htmls/ folder.

title: 29  Interactive HTML Pages
text:
30.1 How to use these pages
A good workflow is:

Read the chapter section that introduces the concept.
Open the corresponding interactive page.
Change parameters, rotate figures, or compare examples.
Return to the chapter and solve the worked examples or exercises.
Use the Python lab for a deeper computational version.

For instructors, these pages can also be used as short classroom demonstrations. The best use is not to show a finished picture, but to ask students what should happen when a parameter changes and then test the prediction.

title: 29  Interactive HTML Pages
text:
30.3 Suggested development plan
The current htmls/ folder can be developed in stages.
Stage 1: Concept visualization. Each page should include a simple interactive figure: a slider, toggle, contour plot, vector field, or rotating 3D surface.
Stage 2: Guided questions. Each page should include two or three short prompts, such as “What changes?”, “What stays fixed?”, and “What does the formula predict?”
Stage 3: Computation bridge. Each page should link naturally to the corresponding Python lab, where students reproduce or extend the visualization with code.
Stage 4: Assessment. Each page can end with a short check-for-understanding question that can be used in class, discussion sections, or review assignments.

title: 29  Interactive HTML Pages

30.4 Design principles
The interactive pages should follow the same philosophy as the book:

start from geometry and visual intuition,
connect formulas to computation,
use examples from data, probability, optimization, and applied mathematics,
keep each activity short enough to use in a focused class moment,
help students move from “I can see it” to “I can compute it” to “I can explain it.”

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31 Formula Sheet
This appendix collects the central formulas used throughout the book. It is designed as a quick reference, not as a substitute for understanding the geometry behind each formula. The best way to use it is to identify the object first: point, vector, curve, surface, scalar field, vector field, density, loss function, or constraint.
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
30  Formula Sheet

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31.1 1. Euclidean Space and Vector Geometry

31.1.1 Points, vectors, distance, and norm
For points \(a=(a_1,
\dots,a_n)\) and \(b=(b_1,\dots,b_n)\) in \(\mathbb R^n\),
\[
b-a=(b_1-a_1,\dots,b_n-a_n)
\]
is the displacement vector from \(a\) to \(b\).
\[
\|v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+\cdots+v_n^2}
\]
\[
d(a,b)=\|b-a\|=\sqrt{(b_1-a_1)^2+\cdots+(b_n-a_n)^2}
\]
The unit vector in the direction of a nonzero vector \(v\) is
\[
\widehat v=\frac{v}{\|v\|}.
\]
A sphere in \(\mathbb R^3\) with center \((h,k,\ell)\) and radius \(r\) has equation
\[
(x-h)^2+(y-k)^2+(z-\ell)^2=r^2.
\]
More generally, the sphere in \(\mathbb R^n\) centered at \(a\) with radius \(r\) is
\[
\|x-a\|=r.
\]


31.1.2 Dot product, angles, orthogonality, and projections
For \(u,v\in \mathbb R^n\),
\[
u\cdot v=u_1v_1+\cdots+u_nv_n.
\]
\[
u\cdot v=\|u\|\|v\|\cos\theta.
\]
Thus,
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}.
\]
Vectors \(u\) and \(v\) are orthogonal if and only if
\[
u\cdot v=0.
\]
The scalar projection of \(u\) onto \(v\) is
\[
\operatorname{comp}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|v\|}.
\]
The vector projection of \(u\) onto \(v\) is
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v.
\]
The component of \(u\) perpendicular to \(v\) is
\[
u-\operatorname{proj}_v(u).
\]
Work by a constant force \(F\) along displacement \(d\) is
\[
W=F\cdot d.
\]
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
30  Formula Sheet

title: 30  Formula Sheet
section: 31.2 2. Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Cross Products
text:
31.2 2. Lines, Planes, Hyperplanes, and Cross Products

31.2.1 Lines and line segments
A line through a point \(p\) in direction \(v\ne 0\) is
\[
r(t)=p+tv,\qquad -\infty<t<\infty.
\]
The line segment from \(a\) to \(b\) is
\[
r(t)=(1-t)a+tb=a+t(b-a),\qquad 0\le t\le 1.
\]
The symmetric form of a line in \(\mathbb R^3\) is
\[
\frac{x-x_0}{a}=\frac{y-y_0}{b}=\frac{z-z_0}{c}
\]
when the direction vector is \((a,b,c)\) and the nonzero denominators are meaningful.


31.2.2 Planes and hyperplanes
A plane through \(p=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) with normal vector \(n=(A,B,C)\) is
\[
A(x-x_0)+B(y-y_0)+C(z-z_0)=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
n\cdot (x-p)=0.
\]
A parametric plane through \(p\) spanned by two nonparallel vectors \(u\) and \(v\) is
\[
r(s,t)=p+su+tv.
\]
A hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^n\) has the form
\[
w\cdot x=b.
\]
The distance from a point \(q\) to the plane \(n\cdot(x-p)=0\) is
\[
\operatorname{dist}(q,\text{plane})=\frac{|n\cdot(q-p)|}{\|n\|}.
\]
The angle \(\theta\) between two planes with normal vectors \(n_1\) and \(n_2\) satisfies
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{|n_1\cdot n_2|}{\|n_1\|\|n_2\|}.
\]


31.2.3 Cross product and triple product
For \(u=(u_1,u_2,u_3)\) and \(v=(v_1,v_2,v_3)\),
\[
u\times v=
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf i & \mathbf j & \mathbf k\\
u_1 & u_2 & u_3\\
v_1 & v_2 & v_3
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
u\times v=(u_2v_3-u_3v_2,\,u_3v_1-u_1v_3,\,u_1v_2-u_2v_1).
\]
The magnitude is
\[
\|u\times v\|=\|u\|\|v\|\sin\theta.
\]
Area of parallelogram spanned by \(u\) and \(v\):
\[
A=\|u\times v\|.
\]
Area of triangle with side vectors \(u\) and \(v\):
\[
A=\frac12\|u\times v\|.
\]
The scalar triple product is
\[
u\cdot(v\times w)=
\begin{vmatrix}
u_1&u_2&u_3\\
v_1&v_2&v_3\\
w_1&w_2&w_3
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
The volume of the parallelepiped spanned by \(u,v,w\) is
\[
V=|u\cdot(v\times w)|.
\]
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
30  Formula Sheet

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31.3 3. Curves and Motion in Space
For a vector-valued curve
\[
r(t)=(x(t),y(t),z(t)),
\]
velocity, speed, and acceleration are
\[
v(t)=r'(t),\qquad \text{speed}=\|r'(t)\|,\qquad a(t)=r''(t).
\]
Arc length from \(t=a\) to \(t=b\) is
\[
L=\int_a^b \|r'(t)\|\,dt.
\]
For a plane curve \(r(t)=(x(t),y(t))\),
\[
L=\int_a^b \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt.
\]
For a space curve \(r(t)=(x(t),y(t),z(t))\),
\[
L=\int_a^b \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^2+\left(\frac{dz}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt.
\]
The unit tangent vector is
\[
T(t)=\frac{r'(t)}{\|r'(t)\|}.
\]
Curvature is
\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{\|T'(t)\|}{\|r'(t)\|}.
\]
When \(r'(t)\times r''(t)\ne 0\),
\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{\|r'(t)\times r''(t)\|}{\|r'(t)\|^3}.
\]
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
30  Formula Sheet

title: 30  Formula Sheet
section: 31.4 4. Functions, Limits, Partial Derivatives, and Gradients
text:
31.4 4. Functions, Limits, Partial Derivatives, and Gradients

31.4.1 Scalar fields and level sets
A scalar field is a function
\[
f:\mathbb R^n\to \mathbb R.
\]
For \(f(x,y)\), the graph is
\[
z=f(x,y).
\]
Level curves satisfy
\[
f(x,y)=c.
\]
Level surfaces satisfy
\[
f(x,y,z)=c.
\]


31.4.2 Partial derivatives
For \(f(x,y)\),
\[
f_x(a,b)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(a+h,b)-f(a,b)}{h},
\]
\[
f_y(a,b)=\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(a,b+h)-f(a,b)}{h}.
\]
Second partial derivatives are
\[
f_{xx},\qquad f_{xy},\qquad f_{yx},\qquad f_{yy}.
\]
If \(f_{xy}\) and \(f_{yx}\) are continuous near a point, then
\[
f_{xy}=f_{yx}.
\]


31.4.3 Gradient
For \(f(x,y)\),
\[
\nabla f=(f_x,f_y).
\]
For \(f(x,y,z)\),
\[
\nabla f=(f_x,f_y,f_z).
\]
For \(f(x_1,\dots,x_n)\),
\[
\nabla f=\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\dots,\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right).
\]
The gradient is perpendicular to level curves and level surfaces.
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
30  Formula Sheet

title: 30  Formula Sheet
section: 31.5 5. Linear Approximation, Chain Rule, Jacobians, and Hessians
text:
31.5 5. Linear Approximation, Chain Rule, Jacobians, and Hessians

31.5.1 Linearization and tangent planes
For \(z=f(x,y)\), the tangent plane at \((a,b,f(a,b))\) is
\[
z=f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)(x-a)+f_y(a,b)(y-b).
\]
The linearization is
\[
L(x,y)=f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)(x-a)+f_y(a,b)(y-b).
\]
In vector form, for \(f:\mathbb R^n\to \mathbb R\),
\[
f(a+h)\approx f(a)+\nabla f(a)\cdot h.
\]
The differential is
\[
df=f_x\,dx+f_y\,dy
\]
for two variables, and
\[
df=\nabla f\cdot dx
\]
in vector notation.
For a level surface \(F(x,y,z)=c\), the tangent plane at \(p=(x_0,y_0,z_0)\) is
\[
\nabla F(p)\cdot((x,y,z)-p)=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
F_x(p)(x-x_0)+F_y(p)(y-y_0)+F_z(p)(z-z_0)=0.
\]


31.5.2 Directional derivatives
For a unit vector \(u\),
\[
D_u f(a)=\nabla f(a)\cdot u.
\]
The maximum rate of increase is
\[
\|\nabla f(a)\|,
\]
and it occurs in the direction
\[
\frac{\nabla f(a)}{\|\nabla f(a)\|}
\]
when \(\nabla f(a)\ne 0\).


31.5.3 Chain rule
If \(z=f(x,y)\) and \(x=x(t)\), \(y=y(t)\), then
\[
\frac{dz}{dt}=f_x\frac{dx}{dt}+f_y\frac{dy}{dt}.
\]
If \(z=f(x,y)\), \(x=x(s,t)\), and \(y=y(s,t)\), then
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial s}=f_x\frac{\partial x}{\partial s}+f_y\frac{\partial y}{\partial s},
\]
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial t}=f_x\frac{\partial x}{\partial t}+f_y\frac{\partial y}{\partial t}.
\]
For a map \(F:\mathbb R^n\to \mathbb R^m\), the Jacobian matrix is
\[
J_F(x)=
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x_n}\\
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\
\frac{\partial F_m}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial F_m}{\partial x_n}
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
For a composition \(H=F\circ G\),
\[
J_H(x)=J_F(G(x))J_G(x).
\]


31.5.4 Hessian and Taylor approximation
The Hessian matrix of \(f:\mathbb R^n\to \mathbb R\) is
\[
H_f(x)=
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1\partial x_n}\\
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n\partial x_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2}
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
Second-order Taylor approximation:
\[
f(a+h)\approx f(a)+\nabla f(a)\cdot h+\frac12 h^T H_f(a)h.
\]
For \(f(x,y)\) near \((a,b)\),
\[
f(x,y)\approx f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)\Delta x+f_y(a,b)\Delta y
+\frac12\left[f_{xx}(a,b)(\Delta x)^2+2f_{xy}(a,b)\Delta x\Delta y+f_{yy}(a,b)(\Delta y)^2\right].
\]

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31.6 6. Optimization

31.6.1 Critical points and second derivative test
Critical points of \(f(x,y)\) satisfy
\[
f_x=0,\qquad f_y=0.
\]
Let
\[
D=f_{xx}f_{yy}-f_{xy}^2.
\]
At a critical point:



Condition
Classification




\(D>0\) and \(f_{xx}>0\)
local minimum


\(D>0\) and \(f_{xx}<0\)
local maximum


\(D<0\)
saddle point


\(D=0\)
inconclusive



For \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\), a critical point satisfies
\[
\nabla f=0.
\]
The Hessian test is based on eigenvalues of \(H_f\):



Hessian eigenvalues
Local behavior




all positive
local minimum


all negative
local maximum


mixed signs
saddle point


zero eigenvalues present
inconclusive without more analysis





31.6.2 Gradient descent and Newton’s method
Gradient descent:
\[
x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha_k\nabla f(x_k).
\]
Newton’s method for optimization:
\[
x_{k+1}=x_k-H_f(x_k)^{-1}\nabla f(x_k).
\]


31.6.3 Lagrange multipliers
For one constraint \(g(x,y)=c\), extrema of \(f\) occur where
\[
\nabla f=\lambda \nabla g,
\]
with
\[
g(x,y)=c.
\]
For several constraints \(g_1=c_1,\dots,g_k=c_k\),
\[
\nabla f=\lambda_1\nabla g_1+\cdots+\lambda_k\nabla g_k,
\]
with all constraints imposed.

30  Formula Sheet

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31.7 7. Double and Triple Integrals

31.7.1 Double integrals
Over a rectangle \(R=[a,b]\times[c,d]\),
\[
\iint_R f(x,y)\,dA=\int_a^b\int_c^d f(x,y)\,dy\,dx
=\int_c^d\int_a^b f(x,y)\,dx\,dy.
\]
For a Type I region
\[
D=\{(x,y):a\le x\le b,\ g_1(x)\le y\le g_2(x)\},
\]
\[
\iint_D f(x,y)\,dA=\int_a^b\int_{g_1(x)}^{g_2(x)}f(x,y)\,dy\,dx.
\]
For a Type II region
\[
D=\{(x,y):c\le y\le d,\ h_1(y)\le x\le h_2(y)\},
\]
\[
\iint_D f(x,y)\,dA=\int_c^d\int_{h_1(y)}^{h_2(y)}f(x,y)\,dx\,dy.
\]
Area:
\[
A(D)=\iint_D 1\,dA.
\]
Average value:
\[
f_{\text{avg}}=\frac{1}{A(D)}\iint_D f(x,y)\,dA.
\]
Mass of a lamina with density \(\rho(x,y)\):
\[
m=\iint_D \rho(x,y)\,dA.
\]
Center of mass:
\[
\bar x=\frac{1}{m}\iint_D x\rho(x,y)\,dA,
\qquad
\bar y=\frac{1}{m}\iint_D y\rho(x,y)\,dA.
\]


31.7.2 Polar coordinates and planar change of variables
Polar coordinates:
\[
x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta.
\]
\[
r^2=x^2+y^2,\qquad \tan\theta=\frac{y}{x}.
\]
Area element:
\[
dA=r\,dr\,d\theta.
\]
Polar double integral:
\[
\iint_D f(x,y)\,dA=\int_\alpha^\beta\int_{r_1(\theta)}^{r_2(\theta)} f(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)r\,dr\,d\theta.
\]
For a transformation \((x,y)=T(u,v)\),
\[
\iint_D f(x,y)\,dA=\iint_S f(x(u,v),y(u,v))\left|\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}\right|\,du\,dv,
\]
where
\[
\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}=
\begin{vmatrix}
x_u & x_v\\
y_u & y_v
\end{vmatrix}.
\]


31.7.3 Triple integrals
Volume:
\[
V(E)=\iiint_E 1\,dV.
\]
Mass with density \(\rho(x,y,z)\):
\[
m=\iiint_E \rho(x,y,z)\,dV.
\]
Average value:
\[
f_{\text{avg}}=\frac{1}{V(E)}\iiint_E f(x,y,z)\,dV.
\]
Center of mass:
\[
\bar x=\frac{1}{m}\iiint_E x\rho\,dV,
\qquad
\bar y=\frac{1}{m}\iiint_E y\rho\,dV,
\qquad
\bar z=\frac{1}{m}\iiint_E z\rho\,dV.
\]


31.7.4 Cylindrical and spherical coordinates
Cylindrical coordinates:
\[
x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta,\qquad z=z.
\]
Volume element:
\[
dV=r\,dr\,d\theta\,dz.
\]
Spherical coordinates:
\[
x=\rho\sin\phi\cos\theta,
\qquad
y=\rho\sin\phi\sin\theta,
\qquad
z=\rho\cos\phi.
\]
Volume element:
\[
dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta.
\]
Useful identities:
\[
\rho^2=x^2+y^2+z^2,
\qquad
r=\rho\sin\phi,
\qquad
z=\rho\cos\phi.
\]

title: 30  Formula Sheet
section: 31.8 8. Line Integrals, Conservative Fields, and Green’s Theorem
text:
31.8 8. Line Integrals, Conservative Fields, and Green’s Theorem

31.8.1 Scalar line integrals
For a curve \(C\) parameterized by \(r(t)=(x(t),y(t),z(t))\), \(a\le t\le b\),
\[
\int_C f\,ds=\int_a^b f(r(t))\|r'(t)\|\,dt.
\]
Mass of a wire with density \(\rho\):
\[
m=\int_C \rho\,ds.
\]


31.8.2 Vector line integrals and work
For a vector field \(F\),
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=\int_a^b F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\,dt.
\]
In the plane, if \(F=(P,Q)\),
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy.
\]
In space, if \(F=(P,Q,R)\),
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr=\int_C P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz.
\]


31.8.3 Conservative fields
A vector field \(F\) is conservative if
\[
F=\nabla f
\]
for some potential function \(f\).
Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals:
\[
\int_C \nabla f\cdot dr=f(r(b))-f(r(a)).
\]
If \(F\) is conservative, then line integrals are path independent and
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr=0
\]
for every closed curve \(C\) in the appropriate domain.
For \(F=(P,Q)\) on a simply connected domain, a useful test is
\[
P_y=Q_x.
\]
For \(F=(P,Q,R)\) on a simply connected solid region, a useful test is
\[
\nabla\times F=0.
\]


31.8.4 Green’s theorem
Circulation form:
\[
\oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy=\iint_D (Q_x-P_y)\,dA.
\]
Flux form:
\[
\oint_C P\,dy-Q\,dx=\iint_D (P_x+Q_y)\,dA.
\]
Area formulas:
\[
A(D)=\oint_C x\,dy=-\oint_C y\,dx=\frac12\oint_C x\,dy-y\,dx.
\]

Resources and Appendices

title: 30  Formula Sheet
section: 31.9 9. Curl, Divergence, Surface Integrals, Stokes’ Theorem, and the Divergence Theorem
text:
31.9 9. Curl, Divergence, Surface Integrals, Stokes’ Theorem, and the Divergence Theorem

31.9.1 Curl and divergence
For \(F=(P,Q,R)\),
\[
\nabla\cdot F=P_x+Q_y+R_z.
\]
\[
\nabla\times F=
\begin{vmatrix}
\mathbf i & \mathbf j & \mathbf k\\
\frac{\partial}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\\
P & Q & R
\end{vmatrix}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\nabla\times F=(R_y-Q_z,\,P_z-R_x,\,Q_x-P_y).
\]
The Laplacian of a scalar field \(f\) is
\[
\Delta f=\nabla\cdot\nabla f=f_{xx}+f_{yy}+f_{zz}.
\]
Useful identities:
\[
\nabla\times(\nabla f)=0,
\]
\[
\nabla\cdot(\nabla\times F)=0.
\]


31.9.2 Parameterized surfaces and surface area
For a parameterized surface
\[
r(u,v)=(x(u,v),y(u,v),z(u,v)),
\]
a normal vector is
\[
r_u\times r_v.
\]
Surface area:
\[
A(S)=\iint_D \|r_u\times r_v\|\,dA.
\]
Scalar surface integral:
\[
\iint_S f\,dS=\iint_D f(r(u,v))\|r_u\times r_v\|\,du\,dv.
\]
For a graph \(z=g(x,y)\),
\[
dS=\sqrt{1+g_x^2+g_y^2}\,dA.
\]


31.9.3 Flux integrals
For an oriented surface \(S\) with unit normal \(n\),
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS
\]
is the flux of \(F\) through \(S\).
Using a parameterization \(r(u,v)\),
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS=\iint_D F(r(u,v))\cdot(r_u\times r_v)\,du\,dv.
\]
For a graph \(z=g(x,y)\) with upward orientation,
\[
n\,dS=(-g_x,-g_y,1)\,dA.
\]


31.9.4 Stokes’ theorem
If \(C=\partial S\) is the positively oriented boundary of an oriented surface \(S\), then
\[
\oint_C F\cdot dr=\iint_S (\nabla\times F)\cdot n\,dS.
\]


31.9.5 Divergence theorem
If \(S=\partial E\) is a closed surface oriented outward, then
\[
\iint_S F\cdot n\,dS=\iiint_E \nabla\cdot F\,dV.
\]

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31.10 10. Probability and Statistics Formulas

31.10.1 Joint densities
For a joint density \(f_{X,Y}(x,y)\),
\[
P((X,Y)\in D)=\iint_D f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dA.
\]
Marginal densities:
\[
f_X(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy,
\]
\[
f_Y(y)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx.
\]
Conditional density:
\[
f_{Y|X}(y|x)=\frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_X(x)}.
\]
Independence:
\[
f_{X,Y}(x,y)=f_X(x)f_Y(y).
\]
Expectation:
\[
E[g(X,Y)]=\iint g(x,y)f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dA.
\]
Covariance:
\[
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[(X-E[X])(Y-E[Y])].
\]


31.10.2 Change of variables for densities
If \(Y=T(X)\) and \(T\) is invertible, then
\[
f_Y(y)=f_X(T^{-1}(y))\left|\det J_{T^{-1}}(y)\right|.
\]


31.10.3 Multivariate normal distribution
For \(x\in\mathbb R^n\), mean \(\mu\), and positive definite covariance matrix \(\Sigma\),
\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}|\Sigma|^{1/2}}
\exp\left[-\frac12(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right].
\]
Level sets of the multivariate normal are ellipsoids:
\[
(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)=c.
\]


31.10.4 Likelihood, score, and observed information
For log-likelihood \(\ell(\theta)\),
\[
\text{score}=\nabla \ell(\theta).
\]
The maximum likelihood estimator often satisfies
\[
\nabla \ell(\widehat\theta)=0.
\]
Observed information:
\[
J(\widehat\theta)=-H_\ell(\widehat\theta).
\]
Local quadratic approximation:
\[
\ell(\theta)\approx \ell(\widehat\theta)-\frac12(\theta-\widehat\theta)^T J(\widehat\theta)(\theta-\widehat\theta).
\]
Delta method approximation:
\[
\operatorname{Var}(g(\widehat\theta))\approx \nabla g(\theta)^T\operatorname{Cov}(\widehat\theta)\nabla g(\theta).
\]

title: 30  Formula Sheet
section: 31.11 11. Data Science and Machine Learning Formulas
text:
31.11 11. Data Science and Machine Learning Formulas

31.11.1 Least squares
For data matrix \(X\in\mathbb R^{m\times p}\), response \(y\in\mathbb R^m\), and parameter vector \(\beta\in\mathbb R^p\),
\[
L(\beta)=\frac12\|X\beta-y\|^2.
\]
Gradient:
\[
\nabla L(\beta)=X^T(X\beta-y).
\]
Hessian:
\[
H_L=X^TX.
\]
Normal equations:
\[
X^TX\widehat\beta=X^Ty.
\]
Ridge regression:
\[
L_\lambda(\beta)=\frac12\|X\beta-y\|^2+\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\beta\|^2.
\]
Gradient:
\[
\nabla L_\lambda(\beta)=X^T(X\beta-y)+\lambda\beta.
\]
Ridge solution:
\[
\widehat\beta=(X^TX+\lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty.
\]


31.11.2 Logistic regression
Sigmoid function:
\[
\sigma(t)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-t}}.
\]
Binary logistic model:
\[
p_i=\sigma(x_i^T\beta).
\]
Negative log-likelihood:
\[
L(\beta)=-\sum_{i=1}^m\left[y_i\log p_i+(1-y_i)\log(1-p_i)\right].
\]
Gradient:
\[
\nabla L(\beta)=X^T(p-y).
\]
Hessian:
\[
H_L=X^T W X,
\]
where
\[
W=\operatorname{diag}(p_i(1-p_i)).
\]


31.11.3 Neural-network chain rule
For a computational graph with intermediate variables,
\[
\frac{dL}{d\theta}=\sum_j \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_j}\frac{\partial z_j}{\partial \theta}.
\]
For composition of maps, gradients are propagated by Jacobian products:
\[
J_{F_k\circ\cdots\circ F_1}=J_{F_k}\cdots J_{F_1}.
\]
Backpropagation is reverse-mode repeated application of the chain rule.


31.11.4 Numerical gradient check
For small \(h\) and standard basis vector \(e_i\),
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(x)\approx \frac{f(x+he_i)-f(x-he_i)}{2h}.
\]

title: 30  Formula Sheet
text:
31.12 12. Applied Mathematics and PDE Formulas
Gradient:
\[
\nabla u=(u_x,u_y,u_z).
\]
Divergence of a vector field \(F=(P,Q,R)\):
\[
\nabla\cdot F=P_x+Q_y+R_z.
\]
Curl:
\[
\nabla\times F=(R_y-Q_z,\,P_z-R_x,\,Q_x-P_y).
\]
Laplacian:
\[
\Delta u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}+u_{zz}.
\]
Heat equation:
\[
u_t=\kappa\Delta u.
\]
Advection equation with velocity field \(v\):
\[
u_t+v\cdot\nabla u=0.
\]
Conservation law:
\[
u_t+\nabla\cdot F=0.
\]
Poisson equation:
\[
-\Delta u=f.
\]
Laplace equation:
\[
\Delta u=0.
\]
Gradient flow for minimizing an energy \(E(u)\):
\[
\frac{du}{dt}=-\nabla E(u).
\]
Continuous diffusion, optimization, and probabilistic smoothing are connected by gradient, divergence, Laplacian, and conservation laws.

text:
31.13 13. Strategy Map







Task
First formula to try




Distance between points
\(d(a,b)=\|b-a\|\)


Angle between vectors
\(\cos\theta=\frac{u\cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}\)

\(\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v\)


Plane through point with normal
\(n\cdot(x-p)=0\)


Tangent plane to graph
\(z=f(a,b)+f_x(a,b)(x-a)+f_y(a,b)(y-b)\)


Tangent plane to level surface
\(\nabla F(p)\cdot(x-p)=0\)


Fastest increase
direction \(\nabla f/\|\nabla f\|\)


Local extrema
solve \(\nabla f=0\) and inspect Hessian


Constraint extrema
solve \(\nabla f=\lambda\nabla g\)


Area by integration
\(\iint_D 1\,dA\)


Volume by integration
\(\iiint_E 1\,dV\)


Polar integral
include factor \(r\)


Spherical integral
include factor \(\rho^2\sin\phi\)


Work along a curve
\(\int_C F\cdot dr\)


Conservative field
find \(f\) with \(F=\nabla f\)


Circulation around a plane curve
Green’s theorem


Flux through closed surface
Divergence theorem


Circulation around space boundary
Stokes’ theorem


Probability over region
integrate joint density over the region


Fit least-squares model
use \(\nabla L=X^T(X\beta-y)\)


Simulate or approximate integral
use grid quadrature or Monte Carlo

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.1 A minimal working environment
This appendix is a practical reference for the Python code used throughout the book. It is not meant to replace a full programming textbook. Its purpose is to give a compact set of patterns for multivariable calculus, geometry, numerical approximation, probability, statistics, data science, and applied mathematics.
The most important habit is to connect code with mathematical meaning. A vector in code should represent a vector in the problem. A meshgrid should represent a region in the plane. A contour plot should represent a level set. A numerical derivative should be checked against the analytic derivative when possible.
The labs use the standard scientific Python stack.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
For symbolic checks, use SymPy.
import sympy as sp
For numerical integration, optimization, and scientific computing, use SciPy when available.
from scipy import integrate, optimize
In Google Colab, these packages are usually already installed. If a package is missing, install it in a notebook cell with:
%pip install numpy matplotlib scipy sympy pandas scikit-learn
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.2 Reproducible random numbers
Many labs use simulation. Use a random number generator with a fixed seed when you want reproducible results.

rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)

x = rng.normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0, size=5)
print(x)

Changing the seed changes the simulated data, but keeping the seed fixed makes your results repeatable.
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.3 Arrays as vectors
A vector such as \(v=(1,2,3)\) can be represented by a NumPy array.

v = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
w = np.array([4.0, -1.0, 2.0])

print(v + w)        # vector addition
print(3*v)          # scalar multiplication
print(v - w)        # displacement-like difference

Use floating-point numbers when you expect numerical computations.

v = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])   # good for numerical calculus

Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
section: 31.4 Norms, distances, dot products, and angles
text:
31.4 Norms, distances, dot products, and angles
The Euclidean norm of \(v\) is \(|v|=\sqrt{v_1^2+\cdots+v_n^2}\) in mathematical notation and np.linalg.norm(v) in NumPy.

v = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
w = np.array([4.0, -1.0, 2.0])

norm_v = np.linalg.norm(v)
distance = np.linalg.norm(v - w)
dot_product = np.dot(v, w)      # same as v @ w

cos_theta = dot_product / (np.linalg.norm(v) * np.linalg.norm(w))
theta = np.arccos(np.clip(cos_theta, -1.0, 1.0))

print(norm_v, distance, dot_product, theta)

The np.clip step protects against small floating-point errors that may make a cosine slightly larger than \(1\) or slightly smaller than \(-1\).
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.5 Projections
The projection of \(u\) onto the direction of \(v\) is
\[
\operatorname{proj}_v(u)=\frac{u\cdot v}{v\cdot v}v.
\]

def projection(u, v):
    u = np.array(u, dtype=float)
    v = np.array(v, dtype=float)
    return (u @ v) / (v @ v) * v

u = np.array([3.0, 2.0])
v = np.array([1.0, 4.0])

proj = projection(u, v)
residual = u - proj

print('projection =', proj)
print('residual =', residual)
print('dot residual with v =', residual @ v)

The residual should be orthogonal to \(v\), up to numerical roundoff.
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
section: 31.6 Cross products and scalar triple products
text:
31.6 Cross products and scalar triple products
In \(\mathbb R^3\), the cross product gives a normal vector and an area vector.

a = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
b = np.array([3.0, 0.0, 1.0])

cross = np.cross(a, b)
area_parallelogram = np.linalg.norm(cross)
area_triangle = 0.5 * area_parallelogram

print(cross)
print(area_parallelogram)
print(area_triangle)

The scalar triple product \(a\cdot (b\times c)\) gives signed volume.

c = np.array([0.0, 2.0, 1.0])
volume = abs(a @ np.cross(b, c))
print(volume)
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.7 Lines, planes, and parameterized objects
A line through point \(p\) with direction \(v\) is \(r(t)=p+tv\).

p = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 0.0])
v = np.array([2.0, -1.0, 3.0])

t = np.linspace(-2, 2, 100)
R = p[None, :] + t[:, None] * v[None, :]

x, y, z = R[:, 0], R[:, 1], R[:, 2]

A parameterized plane through \(p\) with directions \(u\) and \(v\) is \(r(s,t)=p+su+tv\).

p = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 1.0])
u = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
v = np.array([0.0, 1.0, -1.0])

s = np.linspace(-1, 1, 40)
t = np.linspace(-1, 1, 40)
S, T = np.meshgrid(s, t)

X = p[0] + S*u[0] + T*v[0]
Y = p[1] + S*u[1] + T*v[1]
Z = p[2] + S*u[2] + T*v[2]

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.15 Numerical gradients
For \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\), the gradient can be approximated coordinate by coordinate.

def finite_gradient(f, x, h=1e-6):
    x = np.array(x, dtype=float)
    grad = np.zeros_like(x)
    for j in range(len(x)):
        e = np.zeros_like(x)
        e[j] = 1.0
        grad[j] = (f(x + h*e) - f(x - h*e)) / (2*h)
    return grad

f = lambda x: x[0]**2 + 3*x[0]*x[1] + np.sin(x[1])
point = np.array([1.0, 0.5])
print(finite_gradient(f, point))

Use finite differences to check hand derivatives and gradients in optimization code.
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.16 Numerical Jacobians
For \(F:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R^m\), the Jacobian matrix has entries \(J_{ij}=\partial F_i/\partial x_j\).

def finite_jacobian(F, x, h=1e-6):
    x = np.array(x, dtype=float)
    Fx = np.array(F(x), dtype=float)
    m = len(Fx)
    n = len(x)
    J = np.zeros((m, n))
    for j in range(n):
        e = np.zeros_like(x)
        e[j] = 1.0
        J[:, j] = (np.array(F(x + h*e)) - np.array(F(x - h*e))) / (2*h)
    return J

F = lambda x: np.array([x[0]**2 + x[1], np.exp(x[0]*x[1])])
print(finite_jacobian(F, np.array([1.0, 2.0])))
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices
31  Python Reference

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.17 Numerical Hessians
For second-order methods, the Hessian matrix can be approximated by applying finite differences to the gradient.

def finite_hessian(f, x, h=1e-5):
    x = np.array(x, dtype=float)
    n = len(x)
    H = np.zeros((n, n))
    for j in range(n):
        e = np.zeros_like(x)
        e[j] = 1.0
        grad_plus = finite_gradient(f, x + h*e, h=h)
        grad_minus = finite_gradient(f, x - h*e, h=h)
        H[:, j] = (grad_plus - grad_minus) / (2*h)
    return 0.5 * (H + H.T)

f = lambda x: x[0]**2 + x[0]*x[1] + 4*x[1]**2
print(finite_hessian(f, np.array([1.0, 1.0])))

Symmetrizing with 0.5*(H + H.T) reduces small numerical asymmetries.

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.19 Linear approximation
For \(f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R\), the first-order approximation near \(a\) is
\[
f(a+h)\approx f(a)+\nabla f(a)\cdot h.
\]

def linear_approx(f, grad_f, a, x):
    a = np.array(a, dtype=float)
    x = np.array(x, dtype=float)
    return f(a) + grad_f(a) @ (x - a)

f = lambda x: np.exp(x[0] + x[1])
grad_f = lambda x: np.exp(x[0] + x[1]) * np.array([1.0, 1.0])

a = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
x_new = np.array([0.05, -0.02])
print(linear_approx(f, grad_f, a, x_new))
print(f(x_new))

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.20 Taylor approximation with a Hessian
The second-order Taylor approximation is
\[
f(a+h)\approx f(a)+\nabla f(a)\cdot h+\frac12 h^T H_f(a)h.
\]

def quadratic_approx(f, grad_f, hess_f, a, x):
    a = np.array(a, dtype=float)
    x = np.array(x, dtype=float)
    h = x - a
    return f(a) + grad_f(a) @ h + 0.5 * h @ hess_f(a) @ h

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.21 Gradient descent
Gradient descent updates parameters by moving opposite the gradient:
\[
x_{k+1}=x_k-\alpha \nabla f(x_k).
\]

def gradient_descent(grad_f, x0, alpha=0.1, steps=100):
    x = np.array(x0, dtype=float)
    path = [x.copy()]
    for _ in range(steps):
        x = x - alpha * grad_f(x)
        path.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(path)

grad_f = lambda x: np.array([2*x[0], 8*x[1]])
path = gradient_descent(grad_f, x0=[2.0, 2.0], alpha=0.1, steps=40)
print(path[-1])
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.22 Newton’s method in several variables
Newton’s method uses the Hessian to correct for curvature:
\[
x_{k+1}=x_k-H_f(x_k)^{-1}\nabla f(x_k).
\]
In numerical code, solve a linear system rather than computing an inverse.

def newton_method(grad_f, hess_f, x0, steps=10):
    x = np.array(x0, dtype=float)
    path = [x.copy()]
    for _ in range(steps):
        step = np.linalg.solve(hess_f(x), grad_f(x))
        x = x - step
        path.append(x.copy())
    return np.array(path)

Resources and Appendices

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.23 Least squares from scratch
For a linear model \(y\approx X\beta\), the least-squares objective is
\[
L(\beta)=\|y-X\beta\|^2.
\]

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 80)
y = 1.5 + 2.0*x + rng.normal(scale=0.4, size=x.shape)

X = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(x), x])
beta_hat = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)[0]
print(beta_hat)

The gradient of \(L(\beta)\) is \(2X^T(X\beta-y)\).

def least_squares_grad(beta, X, y):
    return 2 * X.T @ (X @ beta - y)

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.28 Monte Carlo integration
Monte Carlo integration estimates an average by random sampling.

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
N = 100_000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, N)
y = rng.uniform(0, 1, N)

f_values = np.exp(-(x**2 + y**2))
estimate = f_values.mean()   # area of unit square is 1
print(estimate)

For a region \(R\) inside a box, sample from the box and keep points in \(R\).

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
N = 200_000
x = rng.uniform(-1, 1, N)
y = rng.uniform(-1, 1, N)
inside = x**2 + y**2 <= 1

f_values = x[inside]**2 + y[inside]**2
area_box = 4
estimate = area_box * inside.mean() * f_values.mean()
print(estimate)

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.29 Polar, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates
Polar coordinates:
\[
x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta,\qquad dA=r\,dr\,d\theta.
\]

r = np.linspace(0, 2, 100)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
R, TH = np.meshgrid(r, theta)
X = R*np.cos(TH)
Y = R*np.sin(TH)

Spherical coordinates:
\[
x=\rho\sin\phi\cos\theta,\qquad
y=\rho\sin\phi\sin\theta,\qquad
z=\rho\cos\phi.
\]

rho = 1.0
phi = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 80)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 160)
PHI, TH = np.meshgrid(phi, theta)
X = rho*np.sin(PHI)*np.cos(TH)
Y = rho*np.sin(PHI)*np.sin(TH)
Z = rho*np.cos(PHI)

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.30 Change of variables and Jacobians
For a transformation \(T(u,v)=(x(u,v),y(u,v))\), the area-scaling factor is the absolute value of the Jacobian determinant.

def jacobian_det_2d(T, uv, h=1e-6):
    J = finite_jacobian(T, np.array(uv, dtype=float), h=h)
    return np.linalg.det(J)

T = lambda uv: np.array([uv[0]*np.cos(uv[1]), uv[0]*np.sin(uv[1])])
print(jacobian_det_2d(T, [2.0, 0.7]))   # should be close to r = 2

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.31 Line integrals by numerical sampling
For a scalar line integral,
\[
\int_C f\,ds = \int_a^b f(r(t))|r'(t)|\,dt.
\]

def scalar_line_integral(f, r, rp, a, b, n=10_000):
    t = np.linspace(a, b, n)
    pts = r(t)
    speeds = np.linalg.norm(rp(t), axis=0)
    values = f(pts) * speeds
    return np.trapz(values, t)

r = lambda t: np.vstack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t)])
rp = lambda t: np.vstack([-np.sin(t), np.cos(t)])
f = lambda pts: 1 + pts[0]**2

print(scalar_line_integral(f, r, rp, 0, 2*np.pi))

For a vector line integral,
\[
\int_C F\cdot dr = \int_a^b F(r(t))\cdot r'(t)\,dt.
\]

def vector_line_integral(F, r, rp, a, b, n=10_000):
    t = np.linspace(a, b, n)
    pts = r(t)
    field = F(pts)
    tangent = rp(t)
    values = np.sum(field * tangent, axis=0)
    return np.trapz(values, t)

F = lambda pts: np.vstack([-pts[1], pts[0]])
print(vector_line_integral(F, r, rp, 0, 2*np.pi))
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.32 Surface parameterization and surface area
For a parameterized surface \(r(u,v)\), the surface-area factor is \(|r_u\times r_v|\).

def sphere_param(u, v):
    return np.array([
        np.sin(u)*np.cos(v),
        np.sin(u)*np.sin(v),
        np.cos(u)
    ])

def sphere_ru(u, v):
    return np.array([
        np.cos(u)*np.cos(v),
        np.cos(u)*np.sin(v),
        -np.sin(u)
    ])

def sphere_rv(u, v):
    return np.array([
        -np.sin(u)*np.sin(v),
        np.sin(u)*np.cos(v),
        0*u
    ])

For numerical surface integrals, sample a grid in parameter space and integrate the area factor.

u = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 300)
v = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 300)
U, V = np.meshgrid(u, v, indexing='ij')

RU = sphere_ru(U, V)
RV = sphere_rv(U, V)
cross = np.cross(RU.reshape(3, -1).T, RV.reshape(3, -1).T)
area_factor = np.linalg.norm(cross, axis=1).reshape(U.shape)

area_u = np.trapz(area_factor, u, axis=0)
area = np.trapz(area_u, v)
print(area)       # close to 4*pi
crumbs: Book
Resources and Appendices

title: 31  Python Reference
section: 31.33 Curl, divergence, and Laplacian on a grid
text:
31.33 Curl, divergence, and Laplacian on a grid
For gridded data, use finite differences. Suppose \(F=(P,Q)\) is a vector field in the plane.

x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y, indexing='xy')
P = -Y
Q = X

dx = x[1] - x[0]
dy = y[1] - y[0]

Q_y, Q_x = np.gradient(Q, dy, dx)
P_y, P_x = np.gradient(P, dy, dx)

curl_2d = Q_x - P_y
divergence = P_x + Q_y

For a scalar field \(u(x,y)\), the Laplacian is \(u_{xx}+u_{yy}\).

U = np.exp(-(X**2 + Y**2))
U_y, U_x = np.gradient(U, dy, dx)
U_yy, U_yx = np.gradient(U_y, dy, dx)
U_xy, U_xx = np.gradient(U_x, dy, dx)
Laplacian = U_xx + U_yy

31.36 Train-test split and scaling

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_data, y_data, test_size=0.25, random_state=0

X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.37 Common plotting patterns
Use equal aspect ratio when geometry matters.

ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')

Add a colorbar for heatmaps and filled contours.

fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label='value')

Use raw strings for LaTeX labels with backslashes.

ax.set_xlabel(r'$\beta_0$')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\beta_1$')

This avoids Python interpreting sequences such as \b as escape characters.

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.38 Common numerical mistakes

Confusing points and arrays. A point, a vector, and a row of data may all be stored as arrays, but they play different mathematical roles.
Forgetting the Jacobian factor. In polar coordinates, \(dA\) becomes \(r\,dr\,d\theta\), not just \(dr\,d\theta\).
Using matrix inverse unnecessarily. Prefer np.linalg.solve(A, b) over np.linalg.inv(A) @ b.
Using too large a finite-difference step. Large \(h\) causes truncation error; tiny \(h\) causes roundoff error. Values such as \(10^{-5}\) or \(10^{-6}\) are often reasonable starting points.
Ignoring shape errors. Print array shapes when code behaves unexpectedly.


print(X.shape)
print(y.shape)
print(beta.shape)

Resources and Appendices

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.39 Recommended workflow for labs

Read the mathematical goal first.
Run the starter code without changes.
Identify which line represents the mathematical object being studied.
Change one parameter and rerun the cell.
Explain what changed in the plot or output.
Check a numerical result against a hand computation when possible.
Save a clean copy of the notebook before experimenting heavily.

Resources and Appendices

title: 31  Python Reference
text:
31.40 A compact starter template

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)

# Define a mathematical object.
def f(x, y):
    return x**2 + y**2

# Build a grid.
x = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)
y = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
Z = f(X, Y)

# Visualize.
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
cs = ax.contour(X, Y, Z, levels=20)
ax.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8)
ax.set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
ax.set_xlabel('x')
ax.set_ylabel('y')
ax.set_title('Level curves of f(x,y)')
plt.show()

This small template contains the central computational pattern of multivariable calculus: define a mathematical object, sample it, compute from it, and visualize its geometry.

title: 32  References
text:
32.1 Core calculus and vector calculus
The main mathematical spine of this book follows the standard multivariable calculus sequence: geometry in \(\mathbb R^n\), functions of several variables, partial derivatives, multiple integrals, vector fields, line integrals, surface integrals, Green’s theorem, Stokes theorem, and the divergence theorem. Useful references for these topics include Stewart (2008), Massey (2017), Strang and Herman (2016), Marsden and Tromba (2012), Hubbard and Hubbard (2015), and Lang (1987).
For students who want a more theoretical viewpoint, Spivak (1965) gives a compact bridge from advanced calculus to differential forms, while Rudin (1976) and Folland (1999) provide deeper analysis background.

title: 32  References
text:
32.2 Linear algebra and geometry
Multivariable calculus becomes clearer when gradients, derivatives, tangent planes, Jacobians, Hessians, and quadratic approximations are read through the language of vectors and matrices. For linear algebra background and its connection to data, see Strang (2016) and Strang (2019).

Resources and Appendices

title: 32  References
text:
32.3 Optimization and machine learning
Optimization is one of the central modern uses of multivariable calculus. The chapters on directional derivatives, Hessians, Lagrange multipliers, gradient descent, Newton’s method, empirical risk, logistic regression, and neural-network backpropagation connect naturally to Boyd and Vandenberghe (2004), Nocedal and Wright (2006), Hastie et al. (2009), James et al. (2021), Bishop (2006), Goodfellow et al. (2016), Mohri et al. (2018), and Murphy (2022).

title: 32  References
text:
32.4 Probability and statistics
Multiple integrals and change of variables are essential for joint distributions, marginal densities, conditional densities, covariance, likelihood, Fisher information, asymptotic approximation, and Bayesian posterior geometry. Good references for these connections include Casella and Berger (2002), Wasserman (2004), Lehmann and Casella (1998), and Murphy (2022).

title: 32  References
section: 32.5 Applied mathematics and partial differential equations
text:
32.5 Applied mathematics and partial differential equations
The vector-calculus chapters also prepare students for applied mathematics: conservation laws, diffusion, heat flow, flux, divergence, curl, Laplacians, and variational thinking. For further study, see Evans (2010) and Haberman (2013).

title: 32  References
text:
32.6 Python and computational tools
The Python labs use the scientific Python ecosystem for visualization, numerical approximation, symbolic checking, simulation, and machine-learning experiments. The main computational references include NumPy Harris et al. (2020), SciPy Virtanen et al. (2020), pandas McKinney (2010), Matplotlib Hunter (2007), Jupyter Kluyver et al. (2016), scikit-learn Pedregosa et al. (2011), and SymPy Meurer et al. (2017).
